Classify these NIH grants by SCIENTIFIC CONTENT (not administrative mechanism).

These are F/K/T awards that were previously auto-classified as "training" based on activity code.
Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

application_id: 11285297
activity_code: F31
title: The effect of mild cognitive impairment on reactive stepping responses and motor adaptation to trip perturbations
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Trips account for over 50% of falls each year, leading to an enormous physical, psychosocial, and financial
burden on the population. Older adults with mild cognitive impairment experience twice as many falls as
cognitively intact older adults; however, it has rarely been examined how mild cognitive impairment affects the
ability to prevent falling and recover balance when unexpected perturbations are experienced. In this study, we
will examine differences in reactive balance between older adults with and without mild cognitive impairment
when exposed to trip perturbations while walking on a treadmill to investigate the mechanisms underlying
increased fall risk in this population.

application_id: 11101121
activity_code: K08
title: Contact-dependent interbacterial responses modulate intestinal colonization by Bacteroides species
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative
Bacteroidetes are major components of the intestinal microbiota linked to many human diseases. The proposed
research will identify interbacterial responses among Bacteroides spp. important for intestinal colonization and
persistence. Better understanding of commensal bacterial interactions will ultimately guide development of
targeted therapeutics.

application_id: 11084496
activity_code: K43
title: Community-based Medication Adherence Support for Older Adults Living with HIV and Hypertension (CBA Intervention)
org_name: MOI UNIVERSITY COLLEGE OF HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
OALWH with hypertension have an increased risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality due to suboptimal
adherence to HTN medication. There is a critical need to develop interventions that promote patient-
centeredness, improves HTN medication adherence and reduce CVD risks. This project will design, pilot and
evaluate a community-based medication adherence support for OALWH with HTN.

application_id: 10889996
activity_code: F32
title: Immune response and viral dynamics within the female genital tract during hyperacute and acute HIV infection
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Despite being a primary site of infection, the human female genital tract remains largely uninvestigated in the setting of hyperacute and acute HIV infection. Utilizing unique samples obtained from the Females Rising through Education, Support and Health (FRESH) cohort, I propose to investigate both the immune response and viral dynamics that occur in the female genital tract following natural HIV exposure by leveraging RNA sequencing, microscopic imaging, and cytokine analysis techniques. This will provide new insight into HIV pathogenesis, allow for the validation of existing experimental models, and help to accelerate the development of therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 10854762
activity_code: K23
title: The Effect of High Frequency rTMS to the Medial Prefrontal Cortex in Alcohol Use Disorder
org_name: NEW YORK STATE PSYCHIATRIC INSTITUTE DBA RESEARCH FOUNDATION FOR MENTAL HYGIENE, INC
phr: Project Narrative Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation is emerging as a potential treatment to decrease alcohol craving and consumption. This project will test the effects of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation to the medial prefrontal cortex with and without naltrexone on participants’ choices to drink alcohol and on brain neurotransmitter levels in a controlled, hospital-based setting. This application will provide Dr. Wai with the necessary training and expertise to conduct translational research using brain stimulation and magnetic resonance spectroscopy to identify novel treatment approaches and study neurotransmitter response.

application_id: 11220572
activity_code: F32
title: Lipid Metabolism, Mitochondrial Stress, and Senescence
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative Insulin resistance, a common and serious health problem, can be associated both with obesity and old age. Recent discoveries point to dysfunction in fat tissue as a culprit; proposed studies will build on those findings and explore the altered biochemistry of fats that leads to that dysfunction. Insights from these studies will inform therapies for age- and obesity-acquired insulin resistance, for which treatment options are currently limited.

application_id: 11059592
activity_code: F30
title: High-Throughput Measurements of RNA-Mediated Regulation of Gene Expression and Oncogenesis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) control most aspects of post-transcriptional gene regulation and cause several
types of human cancers when mutated, which disrupts their interactions with many different RNAs. Currently, it
is not well understood how RBPs are organized to regulate the expression of the RNAs they bind, and which
specific domains within them have regulatory potential that is sensitive to mutation. In this work, I will develop
and use a high-throughput recruitment assay to identify functional ‘effector’ domains within RBPs, link them to
their molecular and cellular mechanisms of regulating RNA, and determine how mutations within them lead to
cancer developmen.

application_id: 11410167
activity_code: U54
title: United States-East Africa HIV-Associated Malignancy Research Center (USEAHAMRC) for Career Development and the Prevention, Early Detection and Efficient Linkage to Care for Virus-related Cancers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: The burden of cervical cancer and Kaposi’s sarcoma in sub-Saharan Africa is daunting; both cancers are
common with poor survival because the region lacks effective prevention, early detection and treatment.
Efforts to improve this are hindered by a shortage of qualified researchers and efficient research networks.
We will create a multi-country collaboration amongst scientists in the U.S. and East Africa to conduct
relevant research on prevention and early detection of these cancers while also training both East African
and U.S. junior scientists.

activity_code: R25
title: Integrated Training Program in Vaccinology (ITP-Vax)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: The utility and cost effectiveness of vaccination has been shown multiple times and most recently during the COVID-19 pandemic. To be ready for the next pandemic, we need to ensure that there are sufficient individuals entering and staying in the field of vaccinology. The goal of the Integrated Training Program in Vaccinology (ITP- Vax) is to encourage more outstanding trainees to join the field of vaccinology and to enable our existing outstanding early-career vaccinologists to excel in mentorship and to become fully independent.

title: Influence of fluoxetine on the disposition kinetics of dolutegravir among people living with HIV with major depression in Nigeria
org_name: COLLEGE OF MEDICINE, UNIVERSITY OF IBADAN
phr: Project Narrative
The overall goal of this project is to provide advanced training to equip Dr Adedeji Waheed A (a
physician/scientist) with skills and competencies in clinical trial, population pharmacokinetics,
pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics modeling and pharmacogenomics. These skills will equip
Dr Adedeji to establish a research career in HIV clinical pharmacology and pharmacogenomics.

the disease in the world but lacks research capacity in HIV clinical pharmacology and
pharmacogenomics.

title: Exposure to Mixtures of Emerging Contaminants in the Environment - Are Communities in Uganda at Health Risk?- A Case Study of Mbarara City.
org_name: MBARARA UNIVERSITY/SCIENCE/ TECHNOLOGY
phr: Relevance to Public Health: Growing evidence indicates that exposure to chemicals in the

well understood. In Uganda, the estimated risk of mortality from NCDs is 22%, primarily due to

application_id: 11167576
activity_code: K01
title: State economic support policies on the prevention of child abuse and neglect during and post the COVID-19 pandemic: Bridging evidence with policy implementation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Child abuse and neglect (CAN) is a pressing and costly public health issue, and economic support policies
may be effective strategies towards reducing CAN by strengthening families' economic well-being and
reducing related stress. This proposed project will rigorously examine the impact of state-based economic
support policies during and post COVID in preventing CAN, and engage families and service providers in
how policies are implemented at the local level to identify effective CAN prevention strategies in
community settings. Findings will inform states' selection and implementation of policies to prevent CAN
and prepare the PI to engage in independent, community-based, and policy-relevant research.

org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES

application_id: 11159386
activity_code: F31
title: Biofilm antigens drive natural immunity to Streptococcus pneumoniae.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Streptococcus pneumoniae is a Gram-positive bacterium that resides in the nasopharynx and can cause
opportunistic infections such as pneumonia, bacteremia, and meningitis. The proposed research will investigate
how repeated exposure events to S. pneumoniae biofilms during colonization leads to proinflammatory damage
to the nasal epithelia and the development of a humoral immune response. Findings from this work will extend
our knowledge of S. pneumoniae biology and its pathogenesis while potentially identifying novel antigens
appropriate for next-generation vaccines.

application_id: 11415427
activity_code: U54
title: Genomic and Microenvironmental Determinants, Temporal Dynamics, and Treatment Efficacy of Radiation-Based Combination Therapies
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Overall Section
 This proposal focuses on elucidating the genomic and microenvironmental determinants, and temporal
dynamics underlying efficacy of radiation-based combination therapies. We will use an innovative molecular
characterization trial testing radiation plus antibody-drug conjugate in bladder cancer and radiation plus
immunotherapy in head and neck cancer to characterize the mechanistic drivers underlying these next
generation RT-based therapies. Our studies will help build a foundation to optimize multimodal, radiation-
based definitive treatment strategies.

application_id: 11169737
activity_code: K01
title: Improving cross ancestry polygenic prediction of tobacco and alcohol use
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
Genetic studies of tobacco and alcohol use, which are of great public health importance, have identified
thousands of associated loci that can be used to predict substance use behaviors through polygenic risk scores.
The proposed project leverages genetic data from >5 million individuals to improve the polygenic prediction of
substance use, and to make this predictive utility applicable to all individuals across populations. Increasing
genomic prediction accuracy allows for greater understanding of the genetic architecture of substance use and
has substantial implications for the prevention and treatment of substance use, as well as important ethical,
legal, and social implications.

application_id: 10824414
activity_code: F32
title: Role of adenosinergic inhibition of serotonin neurons in seizure induced respiratory arrest
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative The leading cause of death in patients who have epilepsy, but do not have good control over their seizures is sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP). It is currently unclear why some seizures result in death when so many others do not. Identifying how two transmitter systems, serotonin and adenosine, interact during seizures may provide insights into why seizure-induced death happens and what can be done to prevent it.

application_id: 11062794
activity_code: F31
title: Effects of the TECTB-C225Y Variant in the Inner Ear
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative The tectorial membrane plays an important role in the inner ear by stimulating hair cells in order to transduce sound. We have recently discovered a mutation in TECTB, which encodes an important organizing protein in the tectorial membrane which causes hearing loss in humans. We propose to investigate the effects of this mutation on the tectorial membrane through a mouse model to better understand its pathology and potential treatments, as well as supplement critical data towards establishing TECTB as a human deafness gene.

application_id: 11170746
activity_code: K01
title: Genetic Association Between Alzheimer's Disease and Cardio-Cerebrovascular Risk Factors
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardio and cerebrovascular risk factors (CVRFs) such as hypertension, diabetes, heart disease, obesity, and stroke
increase the risk of Alzheimer's disease (AD). This project uses cutting-edge multi-omics data in humans with advanced
statistical methods to identify novel genes that interact with CVRFs in AD using GWAS and to identify epigenomic and
transcriptomic profiles, and integrative pathways in AD that interacts with vascular pathologies. My findings may elucidate
how genes and CVRFs interplay to influence AD risk, provide insights into the racial/ethnic disparities in AD, suggest
personalized strategies for preventing AD in elderly individuals with CVRFs, and reveal novel molecular targets for potential
AD treatment.

application_id: 10818099
activity_code: F31
title: Evaluating the mechanisms of cell free fetal DNA signaling in the promotion of labor
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Human labor is a highly inflammatory event, but what causes it to start is unclear. We have found that cell-free fetal DNA (cffDNA) can activate the mother’s immune system and promote the transition towards labor. This proposal aims to understand what about cffDNA makes it activate immune cells and whether we can block the inflammation caused by cffDNA, which is particularly important for preventing preterm birth.

org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES

application_id: 11172585
activity_code: K01
title: Reducing Mobility-associated Interruption in ART among Men in Malawi (ReMIT)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
Mobility, especially unexpected travel, puts men living with HIV in sub-Saharan Africa at risk of HIV treatment
interruption and poor health outcomes. By refining and piloting an intervention to reduce treatment interruption
among mobile men in Malawi, the proposed project will provide knowledge on how to improve treatment
outcomes among this vulnerable population. The project has the potential to improve public health by
improving viral suppression among mobile men living with HIV, a critical population in the effort to end the HIV
epidemic.

application_id: 10988267
activity_code: F31
title: Impact of fentanyl dependence on a parabrachio-amygdalar opioid circuit
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Prolonged use of opioid agonists such as fentanyl, resulting from chronic pain management and from illicit use, induce physical dependence and withdrawal, and a paradoxical decrease in pain tolerance (opioid-induced hyperalgesia, OIH). Opioid withdrawal and OIH are associated with enhanced neural excitability in the central amygdala, but the specific circuits affected, and the behavioral implications of hyperexcitability in those cell types, are not completely understood. This proposal will investigate the impact of fentanyl dependence on neural activity characterized cell type-specific ascending pain pathway between the parabrachial nucleus and the central amygdala and examine how its activity drives withdrawal and OIH.

activity_code: F31
title: Circadian Regulation of In Vitro Differentiated Adipocytes
org_name: DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cell-autonomous circadian biological clocks work in all cells of the human body to regulate metabolism, and their influence is prominent in adipocytes. By studying adipocyte circadian cell biology in pure cells in culture in the absence of organismal cues, we can understand how clock control works in this important cell type, and potentially learn how clock control may be used to understand and address issues of metabolic syndrome and obesity.

title: Friendship Bench for Women Who Use Methamphetamine in Vietnam
org_name: HANOI MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The high comorbidity of mental health issues and meth use in women worsens both their mental health and

application_id: 10870019
activity_code: K23
title: Trigeminovascular sensitization in post-traumatic headache: electrophysiologic and blood biomarkers in youth
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Concussion afflicts 1 in 5 youths in the United States. Headache is one of the most common and disabling symptoms following concussion, affecting 25% of youth for longer than 1 month, yet there are no established recommendations on when or how to treat post-traumatic headache. This project will investigate the biological processes of migraine in youth following concussion to guide the timing and types of treatments used in post- traumatic headache.

application_id: 11179162
activity_code: K01
title: Understanding antiretroviral adherence changes from the community to the nursing home
org_name: NORTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
Antiretroviral therapy (ART) adherence among nursing home (NH) residents with HIV is less than optimal
where 21% of people with HIV were not dispensed ART from their NH contrasts with 87.5% adherence that is
reported among older people with HIV in the community. Viral suppression, stability of chronic conditions,
and mortality are vulnerable to changes in ART adherence. We propose examining facilitators and barriers to
changes in ART adherence during the community-to-long-stay-NH-transition period using mixed methods
with administrative claims data, thematic analysis of interviews with people with HIV that have had a long-
NH-stay, and triangulation.

application_id: 10896201
activity_code: K43
title: Primary Prevention of Stroke in Children with Sickle Cell Anaemia in Nigeria: Community vs Teaching Hospital
org_name: BARAU DIKKO TEACHING HOSPITAL/ KADUNA STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Stroke is a devastating complication of sickle cell anemia (SCA) still occurring in approximately 11% of
children affected with the disease in resource-limited countries, compared to 1% in high-income countries.
In Nigeria, the country with more than 50% of the global burden of SCA, screening for at risk children using
transcranial Doppler ultrasound (TCD) measurements is limited to tertiary hospitals. This mentored award
is focused on initiating a stroke prevention program in a resource-limited community hospital by task
shifting of TCD screening to nurses, a strategy that is expected to increase access for affected children with
prompt institution of a primary prevention strategy for those with increased stroke risk.

application_id: 10932179
activity_code: F31
title: Naturalistic Social Communication in Autistic Females: Identification of Speech Prosody Markers
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposed study will examine speech prosody in autistic females, a highly underrepresented population in research whose prosodic characteristics are largely unknown. Given that prosody is an early marker of autism, characterizing speech prosody in autistic females has the potential to improve their rate of timely diagnosis. Irrespective of sex, this study will be the first to take a comprehensive look at speech prosody using a battery of multi-modal assessments, which will enhance our knowledge of the associations between developmentally critical areas such as structured language, social skills, and adaptability around restricted and repetitive behaviors.

application_id: 11196081
activity_code: K01
title: Feasibility, Acceptability, and Pilot Testing of a Behavioral Intervention for Chronic Migraine
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ARIZONA
phr: Project Narrative
This proposal will involve a pilot study to evaluate the feasibility, acceptability, and outcomes of regular
lifestyle behavior (RLB i.e. regular sleep, exercise, mealtime) protocol in managing chronic migraine.
Additionally, the proposal will investigate biomolecules that are uniquely involved in chronic migraine
patients who respond to RLB. Successful completion of this proposal will inform the design of a future full-
scale clinical trial testing the efficacy of RLB in chronic migraine, as well as provide the candidate with
intensive, supervised training to help him become an independent research scientist focusing on headache
medicine, more specifically on managing migraine using health-promoting lifestyle behaviors.

application_id: 11209582
activity_code: K01
title: Investigation of socio-genomic associations related to survivial among a population-based sample of those diagnosed with endometrial cancer in Metropolitan Detroit
org_name: WAYNE STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Among women with endometrial cancer, some high-risk populations have double the mortality rate compared to others, which cannot be completely explained by known social and biological factors. Socio-genomic studies, which investigate how social factors influence genomic and biologic activity, are critically needed to improve understanding of survival differences in women with endometrial cancer, without which improvements in outcomes will remain challenging. This project investigates how chronic stress, which is reported at high rates in the project catchment area, may modify the relationship between disease characteristics and survival, which may in part explain these survival differences.

application_id: 11228694
activity_code: K01
title: Impact of sex, flow, and glomerular permeability on endocytic uptake along the kidney proximal tubule
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Proteinuria occurs when the kidney proximal tubule is unable to fully reclaim albumin and other proteins that escape the
glomerular ﬁltration barrier, and it is linked to increased risk and progression of chronic kidney disease. This project aims
to develop a multiscale mathematical model of protein uptake along the length of the proximal tubule that includes the
impact of baseline sex diﬀerences and variations in glomerular ﬁltration of ﬂuid and protein. Ultimately, this work will
enhance our ability to predict kidney disease progression and response to potential therapeutics.

application_id: 11229392
activity_code: K01
title: Place-Based Factors Contributing to Sleep Health Disparities in the United States
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE
Sleep deficiency is a major contributor to cardiovascular disease, the leading cause of death globally; both sleep
deficiency and cardiovascular disease are associated with health disparities. We will quantify – using both self-
report and geospatial modeling of relevant environmental and social factors - how place-based factors contribute
to sleep health disparities. The results can be used to develop and target both individual and structural
interventions to improve the health of people most at risk due to health disparity factors.

application_id: 10875450
activity_code: K25
title: Reinforcing dental adhesives with monomers capable dynamic rearrangement and self-recovery
org_name: MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Dental adhesives are employed to create a sturdy and long-lasting bond between dentin and composite
restorations, through the formation of a robust hybrid layer comprised of a three-dimensional polymer network
that interlocks with native bone tissue. To date, the performance of such adhesives is sub-optimal, as the majority
of composite failures are observed at restoration margins. The fundamental goal of this project is to understand
the phase structure and composition of heterogeneities that form during adhesive polymerization, and to
reinforce weakened regions of the hybrid layer through the development and application of monomers capable
of adaptable, dynamic bond rearrangement.

application_id: 10877709
activity_code: K25
title: Smart Needle with Intelligent Robotic Control for Prostate Brachytherapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF HAWAII AT MANOA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will develop a “smart” needle for accurate and less invasive radioactive seed implantation in prostate
brachytherapy. Analytical and computational models, with model parameter updates, will be developed based on realistic
tissue properties to have a better understanding of needle-tissue interaction mechanism. The smart needle will be integrated
with a teleoperated user control interface, with better understanding of the dynamics of the system and shape- and force-
sensing, to improve needle guidance into a desire path towards the target, and thereby improved clinical outcomes.

application_id: 10824902
activity_code: F31
title: The effect of mild cognitive impairment on reactive stepping responses and motor adaptation to trip perturbations
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Trips account for over 50% of falls each year, leading to an enormous physical, psychosocial, and financial burden on the population. Older adults with mild cognitive impairment experience twice as many falls as cognitively intact older adults; however, it has rarely been examined how mild cognitive impairment affects the ability to prevent falling and recover balance when unexpected perturbations are experienced. In this study, we will examine differences in reactive balance between older adults with and without mild cognitive impairment when exposed to trip perturbations while walking on a treadmill to investigate the mechanisms underlying increased fall risk in this population.

application_id: 11182542
activity_code: T32
title: Research Training in Rheumatology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative
Through intensive mentoring in a highly interactive and collaborative research environment, this T32 program
will support the development of outstanding MD, MD/PhD and PhD investigators whose research focus is on
rheumatic and autoimmune disease pathogenesis, prevention and treatment. This program has demonstrated
substantial success in supporting predoctoral trainees and postdoctoral fellows who are currently in academic
positions or evolving academic tracks. Based on positive outcomes in the current funding cycle, we propose to
continue to include trainees at both of these key earlier career development periods, as well as short term
medical students who are highly motivated to develop research skills and insights into disease.

application_id: 10744195
activity_code: K25
title: Tracking M2-Like Macrophages in Cardiopulmonary Diseases by Magnetic Resonance Imaging
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Two major activated macrophages, classically activated macrophages (M1) and alternatively activated
macrophages (M2), play critical roles in various diseases. Manipulation of the M1/M2 macrophage balance has
emerged as an attractive approach to the treatment of these diseases, including myocardial infarction and
cancer. A noninvasive imaging method to detect M2-like macrophages will provide a powerful tool to monitor
disease progression and evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of these emerging therapies.

application_id: 11234261
activity_code: K01
title: Leveraging within-host M. tuberculosis diversity data to enhance transmission inference
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative
Identifying where tuberculosis transmission occurs is an urgent priority for tuberculosis control programs. Yet
current genomic epidemiology approaches for inferring transmission linkages have limited resolution because
they sequence a single M. tuberculosis genome from each infected individual. We propose to develop
sequencing and transmission inference approaches to recover the M. tuberculosis diversity within infected
individuals and harness this diversity to reconstruct transmission linkages in a high-incidence setting.

application_id: 10877562
activity_code: R01
title: Neuronal and behavioral effects of an implicit priming approach to improve eating behaviors in obesity: Diversity supplement.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The overall goals of the parent project are to determine the impact of an implicit priming intervention, designed to alter food perceptions, on brain responses to food, food intake behaviors and weight-loss maintenance in individuals with overweight/obesity. The proposed supplement will add an assessment of early-life adversity to the parent project, to determine if early-life adversity impacts the neuronal response to food cues in brain regions involved in reward processing, and if this mediates effects of adversity on adiposity. This project will support research training for an incoming graduate student from a group underrepresented in health sciences.

application_id: 10840374
activity_code: K76
title: Developing an App-Based Voice Clinical Decision Support Tool to Augment the Sensitivity of the Bedside Swallow Evaluation in Older Adults
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Swallowing dysfunction is common in older adults, and leads to significant morbidity, including aspiration
pneumonia, malnutrition, and dehydration, and is the leading cause of death in older adults with AD/ADRD.
Unfortunately, the bedside swallow evaluation currently used by speech language pathologists and nurses to
detect swallowing dysfunction is unreliable, especially in individuals with AD/ADRD. We propose to develop a
sensitive smartphone-based clinical decision support tool that incorporates analysis of multiple characteristics
of a patient's recorded voice and cough to determine their aspiration risk.

application_id: 11235846
activity_code: K01
title: Functional validation of the SMOC1 matrisomal protein network in Alzheimers disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Large scale proteomic profiling of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) patients has identified an interconnected network
of proteins that change in levels as the disease advances. To determine the function of these proteins in the
brain and link them to specific causes of AD, this project will dissect the role of SMOC1, a key protein predicted
to drive changes in the network as a whole, and link individual network components to specific triggers that
cause AD. The completion of this study will identify targets for drug development, uncover biological processes
underlying AD, and elucidate the role of a predicted AD protein network with high translational potential.

application_id: 11074524
activity_code: F32
title: Designing addressable proteoliposomes by using viral transmembrane proteins and biomimetic cell-free synthesis techniques.
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
This research fellowship aims to design a useful tool for both gaining fundamental knowledge of
viral protein-cell membrane interactions and addressing current challenges in targeted drug-
delivery, especially those associated with carrier (delivery vehicle) -cell receptor specificity. By
developing adapted cell-free protein synthesis techniques, we intend to construct user-defined
proteoliposomes whose composition can mirror an exterior viral surface, used towards either
furthering our understanding of virus ligand-host cell interactions or repurposed for targeted
therapies exploiting the native fidelity of virus membrane proteins and for their respective cellular
receptors.

application_id: 11237043
activity_code: K01
title: Living beyond cancer: the short- and long-term cognitive effects of breast cancer and its treatment for cancer survivors
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dr. Peng proposes to comprehensively investigate breast cancer and its treatment (chemotherapy, radiation,
and hormone therapy) with global and domain-specific cognitive trajectory over time and to characterize cancer
hallmark pathways in brain tissues of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) patients and controls to identify intersecting
mechanisms for cancer and AD to inform targeted intervention. This research, together with Dr. Peng’s proposed
trainings in aging epidemiology, cancer survivorship, and advanced statistical modeling, will enable Dr. Peng to
conduct interdisciplinary research to study the deep connections between cancer and aging and to identify
interventions targeting common pathways of cancer and AD to prevent both diseases.

application_id: 10856906
activity_code: F31
title: Using Functional Neuroimaging and Smartphone Digital Phenotyping to Understand the Emergence of Internalizing  Illness
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: NARRATIVE A key challenge for psychiatry and clinical psychology is to discover, understand, and ultimately to treat the root causes of the most common and costly mental disorders. The proposed study will harness an innovative combination of state-of-the-art brain imaging techniques and longitudinal measures of symptom progression, function, and real-world experience to identify the neural and behavioral pathways that contribute to the development and recurrence of anxiety disorders and depression—mental illnesses that are common and challenging to treat. This work is a critical step toward the development of new, neurobiologically-grounded strategies for preventing or treating these debilitating emotional disorders.

application_id: 10846596
activity_code: K76
title: Leveraging Electronic Health Records to Measure and Reduce Harmful, Low-Value Care Among Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This NIA K76 Beeson Career Development Award resubmission application seeks to develop an electronic
health record (EHR) measure (“eMeasure”) of low-value proton pump inhibitor (PPI) prescriptions among older
adults, which would represent a timely and potentially transformative innovation in the quality of care field. The
proposal also aims to design, pilot test, and implement an intervention consisting of a behavioral economic
EHR-based clinical decision support tool to reduce low-value PPI prescriptions among older adults, informing a
future NIA R01 application for a multisite pragmatic trial. The long-term goal of the project is to develop state-
of-art and scalable methods to utilize the EHR to measure and reduce harmful, low-value care among older
Americans.

application_id: 11237594
activity_code: K01
title: Defining Host-pathogen Interactions in CAUTI to Guide Novel Drug Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTIs) are the most common cause of hospital-
associated infections and can result in severe disease and increased mortality. This proposal
seeks to define the host-pathogen interactions, and specifically the contribution of the virulence
factor urease, that facilitate CAUTI caused by Staphylococcus aureus in catheterized individuals.
Additionally, this research will provide the platform for the career development of a young
investigator focused on translating basic science research in host-pathogen interactions,
molecular microbiology, and novel drug development into clinically useful interventions to improve
patient care.

title: Integrating tuberculosis treatment into community pharmacies to improve TB/HIV outcomes in Uganda.
org_name: INFECTIOUS DISEASES INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
An estimated 3 in 10 people with tuberculosis (TB)/HIV in sub-Saharan Africa have a suboptimal TB treatment
outcome despite receiving effective anti-TB and anti-retroviral therapy (ART) regimens. Monthly health facility
visits to collect anti-TB drugs and ART refills present several challenges due to both direct and indirect costs,
resulting in poor healthcare access, continuity of care, and treatment adherence. The proposed studies will
evaluate whether integrating TB treatment into community pharmacies (a well-established differentiated ART
delivery model) can improve access to both anti-TB drugs and ART refills and enhance TB/HIV treatment
outcomes, hence contributing to the End TB Strategy goals (zero TB deaths, zero TB infection, and zero
catastrophic costs) and the ...

title: Development of an Alcohol, Cannabis, and Sexual Assault Risk Reduction Program for High Risk College Women
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sexual assault is a significant public health concern impacting 1 in 5 college women. Alcohol and cannabis are
known risk factors for sexual assault victimization, and women who experience nonconsensual sex are at a
greater risk of developing alcohol and cannabis use disorders. This project involves the development of a risk
reduction program for high risk college women that will integrate evidence- and theory-based alcohol and
cannabis prevention content into an evidence-based sexual assault risk reduction program.

org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Traumatic injury is the leading cause of death for Americans between the ages of 1 and 44, accounting for
nearly half of all deaths in this age group, as well as significant long-term complications for many who survive,
and the highly-specialized surgical care available at Level 1 trauma centers substantially improves patient
survival and recovery from traumatic injury when compared to less specialized treatment. Further advances in
the treatment of traumatic injury will require trauma surgeons to actively pursue the research necessary to
make new discoveries and develop new systems of care, which is facilitated by adding formal preparation in
the methods and application of scientific investigation to their clinical training. The program described herein
provides the meth...

application_id: 10996517
activity_code: F32
title: Daily and circadian regulation of respiratory plasticity in freely behaving rats
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Acute intermittent hypoxia, or repetitive periods of low oxygen, has shown great promise as a therapeutic in ongoing clinical trials to improve breathing (and non-respiratory) function in individuals with spinal cord injury and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. The proposed project will investigate whether behavioral and physiological factors stemming from an individual’s biological clock influence the effectiveness of acute intermittent hypoxia in improving breathing function. These studies will provide important new biological insights for the use of acute intermittent hypoxia as a therapeutic strategy in humans.

title: Development and Co-design of a Tailored Physical Activity Intervention for Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias (AD/ADRD) Caregivers

phr: Unpaid caregivers of people living with Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) and related dementias (AD/ADRD) face complex, role-related stressors which are associated with poor health outcomes. application_id: 11245707
activity_code: K01
title: Investigation of the Molecular Mechanisms Underlying Astrocytic CREB's Regulation of Cocaine Drug-Seeking
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Substance use disorder (SUD) is associated with long-term changes in gene expression. While it is well
established that upstream regulators, such as transcription factors and epigenetic modifications, mediate these
changes in neurons, how cocaine use impacts other cell types in the brain is unknown. The aim of this research
is to establish how transcriptional regulation in astrocytes changes the molecular landscape to support addiction
related behaviors, specifically cocaine drug-seeking.

application_id: 11245795
activity_code: K01
title: Analysis of C9ORF72-associated poly (GR) interactions on BAF chromatin remodeling complex structure and function
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative
Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) are two fatal
neurodegenerative diseases, and a repeat expansion mutation in the chromosome 9
open reading frame (C9ORF72) constitutes the most common form of familial FTD and
ALS. Repeat expansions can generate five different dipeptide repeat proteins (DPRs),
and we have found that these the DPR, poly (GR) can interact with the chromatin
remodeling complex BRG1/BRM-associated factor (BAF), inducing a decrease the levels
of this chromatin regulator. In this proposal, we will analyze how this interaction affect
complex structure and the capacity to modify chromatin status and gene expression in
patient-derived iPSCs derived neurons and its effects on neuronal survival and function.

application_id: 11016975
activity_code: K99
title: Quantitatively predicting drug-resistant mutations to improve precision oncology
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
My long-term goal is to build quantitative models that predict and circumvent cancer therapy resistance,
specifically focusing on protein kinases currently targeted by over 75 FDA-approved inhibitors. Unfortunately,
multiple kinase mutations have been found to resist known inhibitors and drastically reduce patient survival rates,
and it remains unclear why these mutations induce such dramatic drug-resistance. Through a combination of
high-throughput experiments, physics-based simulation, and machine learning based data-science methods, I
will identify the mechanistic impact of clinically observed kinase mutants and build models that predict if a drug-
resistant mutant is newly sensitive to alternative clinical therapeutics.

application_id: 11250051
activity_code: K01
title: Elucidating Molecular Mechanisms Linking Fructose to Cholesterol Metabolism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiovascular disease (CVD), the number one killer in men and women, is associated with the prevalence of
nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD). Consumption of fructose, primarily found in sugar-sweetened
beverages and processed foods, is a major risk factor for development of both NAFLD and CVD. The primary
goal of this proposal is to determine the mechanisms by which fructose increases cholesterol levels, which will
yield insight into novel therapeutic options for NAFLD and associated CVD risk.

application_id: 10856920
activity_code: T32
title: UW Surgical Oncology Research Training Program (UW-SORT)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARATIVE Despite a long history of groundbreaking discoveries, the number of funded surgeon-scientists continues to drop precipitously. Evidence supports a two-year research experience as part of general surgery residency to increase the likelihood that surgeons will pursue academic careers, and the integration of PhD-scientists into Departments of Surgery as a means of promoting surgical research productivity. Utilizing these two approaches, the University of Wisconsin Surgical Oncology Research Training (UW-SORT) Program aims to train post-doctoral fellows who will pursue multidisciplinary, translational surgical oncology research and ensure our continued advancement in the care of patients with cancer.
terms: Research Training;Surgical Oncology;Training Programs

application_id: 11251238
activity_code: K01
title: A new generation of misleading tobacco marketing: Assessing the evolution of misleading combustible tobacco marketing features and detrimental implications for vulnerable youth and young adults
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
This research will apply a mixed-methods approach to assess the use and impact of a new generation of
misleading descriptors and imagery in combustible tobacco marketing. Research activities (including focus
groups, a discrete choice experiment and eye tracking) will focus on identifying the effects of such marketing
for cigarettes and cigarillos on attention, product appeal, and risk perceptions of current and susceptible youth
and young adult tobacco users, and will inform future research and appropriate regulatory remedies.

activity_code: K01
title: Energy expenditure, cognitive function, and biomarker features of Alzheimer's disease
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This integrated training and research proposal focuses on the longitudinal associations of two physical activity-
related measures of energy expenditure with cognition and Alzheimer’s disease (AD) biomarkers among older
adults who are cognitively normal, as well as those with mild cognitive impairment and mild AD dementia.
Potential biological and genetic factors that may modify and/or mediate these associations will also be examined.

application_id: 11256860
activity_code: K01
title: Strengthening Clinical Support for Providers Working with Women Living with HIV to Manage Menopause
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Although symptoms associated with the menopausal transition (MT) disproportionately affect
cisgender women with HIV (WWH) and impact adherence to antiretroviral treatment, the MT is
under-researched among WWH and HIV care providers have few tools to support their patients
going through this transition. The proposed study aims to examine the relationship of
menopausal phase and symptom severity with HIV care and treatment outcomes, and develop
and pilot test an intervention to consistently implement menopause treatment support for midlife
WWH in a clinical setting. Findings will support the development of a multi-level menopause
support intervention for both HIV care providers and their patients to be empirically tested in an
R01.

application_id: 11257251
activity_code: K01
title: Mobile health applications in adolescent marijuana use: assessing the interplay among sleep, pain and inhibitory control
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Pediatric chronic pain is a common, complex and highly debilitating developmental health concern
that has been associated with multiple risk factors for heavy and frequent marijuana use.
However, the longitudinal interplay among chronic pain and two key risk factors, sleep
disturbances and decreased inhibitory control, as it relates to adolescent marijuana use, remains
minimally explored. This study aims to map the unfolding of pathophysiological processes using
a combination of mobile health assessment tools, in order to implement personalized, early
intervention in these vulnerable adolescents.

application_id: 11257295
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of Resistance Exercise Training for Improved Muscle Insulin Sensitivity
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/LAY SUMMARY
Resistance exercise training not only enhances muscle mass and strength but also improves insulin sensitivity,
thereby mitigating complications associated with type 2 diabetes. Our preliminary data suggest that skeletal
muscle protein synthesis, post-translational modifications, and upregulation of the transcriptional co-activator,
PGC-1α4, play pivotal roles in mediating the beneficial effects of resistance exercise training on metabolic health.
This proposal could help identify key mechanisms through which resistance exercise training improves insulin
sensitivity in people with insulin resistance, thus providing the foundation for future therapies, drugs, and exercise
prescriptions to effectively combat type 2 diabetes.

title: Evaluating the implementation and impact of standard-of-care delivered oncology financial navigation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: The proposed research is relevant to public health because it will provide foundational data on the utility of oncology financial navigation implementation strategies and their effect on implementation and patient outcomes in a real-world clinical setting serving patients with cancer. application_id: 11262865
activity_code: K01
title: FUNCTIONAL VALIDATION OF A PLECTIN-ASSOCIATED ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE PROTEIN NETWORK
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Large scale proteomic analysis of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) patients has identified a connected network of
proteins associated with AD. To determine whether the M4 protein network and its key hub proteins, including
Plectin (Plec) modulates AD risk, this project will dissect Plec’s function in the nervous system and validate the
protein network structure using cross-species analysis. My results will determine the function of a predicted AD
target in the adult brain, its role in the protein network, and validate protein network structure with the goal of
elucidating novel AD pathophysiological mechanisms and develop novel therapeutics.

title: Adolescent exposure to nicotine and tobacco content on social media: incorporating adolescent experiences and social media analytics to inform exposure assessment
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Using nicotine and tobacco products in adolescence is associated with future tobacco use and
increases risk for adverse health effects. Perceptions, intentions, and use behaviors may be
influenced by exposure to and engagement with pro-nicotine and tobacco product messaging
on social media. This research will use qualitative insights and advanced data science methods
to advance our understanding of youth experiences with pro-nicotine and tobacco content on
social media and help inform prevention activities related to digital marketing of products to

title: Advancing Cardiovascular Health Among Adolescents with Intellectual Disabilities: Co-Development of Technology Interventions to Enhance Physical Activity
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Despite significant investment in Medicaid services (~$53 billion), people with disabilities continue to experience pronounced health issues, which increases their risk of poor health outcomes and results in higher morbidity and shorter life expectancies compared to the general population. Among adolescents with intellectual disabilities (ID), the intersection of inactivity, motor impairments, and access challenges contributes to a higher risk of preventable chronic conditions such as cardiovascular disease and obesity. A solution-oriented approach that includes actively testing, advancing, scaling, and implementing innovative evidence-based physical activity interventions that address poor health outcomes among adolescents with ID is necessary.

application_id: 11285243
activity_code: K01
title: Investigating the functional roles of CTSH and PGM1 in beta-cells during autoimmune diabetes development
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Type 1 diabetes is caused autoimmune destruction of the pancreatic beta-cells, but currently there is no
therapy that can provide long-lasting improvements in beta-cell function. The aim of this project is to
understand the mechanistic roles of two type 1 diabetes loci, CTSH and PGM1, in beta-cells. The proposed
experiments will inform us whether these gene products could be targeted to enhance endogenous beta-cell
function in type 1 diabetes.

Substance use disorders remain a serious problem in this country. The interdisciplinary training program
proposed here addresses that problem by preparing individuals with the skills necessary to advance

application_id: 11145840
activity_code: K23
title: Geospatial food access as a driver of environmental oxidant stressors and early obesity
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: The consequences of early childhood obesity are lifelong, with substantial cardiovascular risk across various population groups. This study integrates longitudinal geospatial, chemical, and psychosocial data to investigate how limited geospatial food access in pregnancy may increase oxidative stress and childhood obesity risk through mechanisms that involve chemical and psychosocial stressors. Understanding the currently unknown role of geospatial food access in driving oxidant stressors starting in utero would inform built environment investments and neighborhood strategies to promote health for all Americans across the life course.

application_id: 10944972
activity_code: K99
title: Complement in the cellular microenvironment during cardiac renewal
org_name: TEXAS HEART INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Heart attacks result in permanent loss of cardiac muscle cells, leading to increased morbidity and mortality with limited treatment options. To regenerate cardiac muscle cells, special populations of fibroblasts and immune cells are required, yet their precise functions and contributions to cardiac renewal are not well understood. In support of the NIH mission to promote better treatment and prevention strategies, my research project will elucidate the interplay between these non- muscle cells and the cardiac muscle cells, which may lead to novel therapeutics promoting cardiac renewal.

application_id: 10783523
activity_code: K99
title: How to make a microtubule organizing center
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Microtubules of the mitotic spindle orchestrate equal segregation of chromosomes during cell division. This work will reveal how mitotic spindles are generated during mitosis in animals and trypanosome parasites. By determining how different species make their spindles, we will better understand not only mitosis, a critical event in the lifecycle of the cell, but also can develop new treatments for parasitic infection.

application_id: 10693869
activity_code: F30
title: Modeling Hidden Myosin Structural States to Predict Drug Specificity and Disease Phenotypes
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Myosins are molecular motors responsible for generating much of the force in the human body, including the heartbeat and the movement of limbs. Small changes to the cardiac myosin gene can cause cardiomyopathies, the leading causes of sudden cardiac death in young adults. This proposal aims to combine molecular simulations with experiments to understand how these subtle changes to the myosin gene create such large effects in protein function and how we can develop novel drugs to treat cardiomyopathies and other common heart conditions.

application_id: 11103125
activity_code: F31
title: Nicotinic modulation of excitatory synapse development in the prefrontal cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In the US, 1 in 14 pregnant people smoke cigarettes during pregnancy; prenatal nicotine exposure leads to an increased risk of developing several neurobehavioral disorders (e.g., ADHD and autism spectrum disorders). Pressingly, the cellular and molecular mechanisms by which the nicotinic cholinergic system modulates circuit and synapse maturation, and how these are dysregulated by nicotine exposure during early brain development, remain poorly understood. Characterizing the role of the nicotinic cholinergic signaling in circuit and synapse maturation will expand our knowledge of brain development and elucidate therapeutic targets to mitigate the adverse outcomes of prenatal nicotine exposure.

activity_code: DP1
title: Harnessing Proteins as Drugs: the Protectome of Cancer- and Aging-Prevention Proteins
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Public Health Relevance Statement
In this project, it is proposed that an important class of proteins exists universally in all
organisms, that prevents damage to molecules, and that, in humans, these proteins constitute
natural cancer- and neurodegenerative disease-prevention proteins. These protection proteins
and their genes were not hypothesized or identified previously, in part because of inadequate
genetic methods for finding and understanding them. The project includes—use of new methods
capable of finding and defining the functions of these proteins; the development of new
technology for increasing production of any specific natural protein to act as a drug for
prevention of cancer and other age-related maladies; and creation of a simple blood-pressure-
like general technology for ...

title: Digital Mindfulness Meditation-enhanced Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT-MM) for Binge Eating Disorder
org_name: YESHIVA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
I propose to develop a novel digital treatment, Mindfulness Meditation-enhanced Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
(CBT-MM), that integrates MM and CBT for the treatment of Binge Eating Disorder (BED) to target the two
important mechanisms in the development and maintenance of BED: dietary over-restriction and emotion
dysregulation. The current K23 proposal involves developing digital CBT-MM for BED and conducting a pilot
randomized control trial to evaluate its feasibility, acceptability, and within-group changes from pre-to post
treatment on indices of: binge eating remission, binge eating frequency, weight, quality of life, depressive
symptoms, emotion dysregulation, dietary over-restriction, and trait mindfulness. This innovative research
project has the potential to advanc...

application_id: 11317458
activity_code: K01
title: Public Policy & Health for Substance Exposed Infant-Mother Dyads
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Criminal justice-oriented policies enacted to protect newborns from prenatal substance exposure are associated with unintended negative consequences for both the mother and infant, such as lower rates of maternal treatment utilization and higher rates of neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome. Connecticut has implemented a unique policy strategy to support this population through early identification and assessment of mother-infant dyads. This proposed project applies a theory of health services utilization to examine this policy’s effect on three key outcomes: (1) maternal substance use treatment utilization, (2) child safety, and (3) child protective services involvement.

application_id: 11324438
activity_code: K01
title: Metabolic Vulnerability and Effects of APOE in Human Neurons with Impaired Endocytic Recycling
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Neurodegenerative dementias including Alzheimer’s disease afflict many elderly people in our society, and
effective treatments to prevent neuronal death are severely lacking. Metabolic stress and endosomal dysfunction
are early features of neurodegenerative disease, but how these cause neuron death, and strategies to maintain
neuron resilience, remain elusive. This proposal will significantly advance our understanding of how endosome-
associated risk genes for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease contribute to metabolic vulnerability and
proteostatic functional decline in human neurons, and will explore genetic and metabolic restorative approaches.

application_id: 11324621
activity_code: K01
title: Molecular mechanisms of incubation of methamphetamine craving
org_name: TEXAS A&M UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There are currently no FDA-approved therapeutics for psychostimulant use disorders, and our understanding
of the neurobiological changes underlying the intensification of methamphetamine craving over increasing
periods of abstinence is limited. The role of key projections from the nucleus accumbens in mediating the
incubation of methamphetamine craving will be delineated. This project will also use state of the art molecular
techniques to identify cell type-specific changes in gene expression and chromatin accessibility in the rat
nucleus accumbens associated with the incubation of methamphetamine craving.

application_id: 11015946
activity_code: DP2
title: Understanding diapause and its ability to suspend and preserve life
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY NEW YORK STONY BROOK
phr: Understanding diapause and its ability to suspend and preserve life
Project Narrative
As a natural form of “suspended animation”, diapause is a dormant state which can suspend embryonic
development and preserve life for an extended time without compromising following health and life. However,
our knowledge of how diapause works, especially in a more complex vertebrate system, remains limited. Here
we study diapause through two complementary angles – signaling pathways and functioning mechanisms –
using a naturally short-lived African killifish for its remarkable ability for staying in diapause to survive the
drought for up to years.

activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the genetic basis of protective immunity to tuberculosis in diverse hosts
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Genetic diversity is a key factor underlying heterogenous disease states of tuberculosis. However, current
mouse models fail to incorporate both genetic and phenotypic diversity. This proposal leverages diverse but
reproducible Collaborative Cross mouse strains, bacterial reporters of infection and novel host-pathogen
genetic mapping strategies to mechanistically define loci underlying disease tolerance. Overall, these novel
and synergistic strategies will define new models of disease tolerance and set the roadmap for systematically
interrogating host-pathogen genetic interactions underlying tuberculosis.

application_id: 11085833
activity_code: K08
title: Understanding the mechanism of tropism specific immune restriction in HIV infection
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Efforts to develop a cure for HIV have been hindered by HIV’s rapid mutational rate and ability to evade immune pressure. During the early clinical stages of HIV infection, a specific subset of HIV, X4-tropic viruses, are naturally restricted by the innate immune, while R5-tropic viruses replicate freely. In the proposed study, we will investigate the molecular mechanism that mediate this differential viral restriction using banked patient samples, in vivo mouse models, and ex vivo primary human cell analyses, with the goal of generating foundational data for the future development of novel therapeutics that empower our immune system to clear R5-tropic viral infections.

application_id: 11354992
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of Flow-driven Transcriptional Control of Hematopoietic Stem Cell Development by YAP
org_name: VERSITI BLOOD HEALTH, INC.
phr: Project Narrative
Blood flow is essential for the production of hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) during embryonic development;
successful harnessing of the regulatory program initiated or maintained by this extrinsic regulatory factor may
facilitate growth and expansion of HSCs for therapeutic use. I propose to use zebrafish as an in vivo platform to
dissect the physical, cellular and molecular mechanisms by which hemodynamic forces intersect with YAP
transcriptional regulation to drive HSC production. Translation of this work to in vitro human hematopoietic
differentiation has potential to facilitate generation of clinically useful HSCs for the treatment of blood diseases.

title: Biomarker Identification to Increase Adjunctive Cryoablation and Checkpoint-Inhibitor Immunotherapy Response
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative This study aims to advance understanding of adjunctive cryoablation’s ability to increase checkpoint inhibitor response by identifying biomarkers predictive of response as well as mechanisms of resistance. Biomarkers will be identified in preclinical murine models with the aid of granzyme B PET imaging and also identified from clinical biopsy specimens obtained from a prospective clinical trial of cryoablation and pembrolizumab in metastatic urothelial carcinoma. This study will help to define which cancer patients on immunotherapy are most likely to benefit from the addition of cryoablation and how resistance to adjunctive cryoablation can be overcome.

title: Multisensory integration at the cell, circuit, and behavioral levels: How audiovisual signals drive dynamic courtship behavior in Drosophila melanogaster
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Individuals are capable of integrating multisensory information, such as audible words and visible facial expressions during social communication, and then responding with unique and appropriate behaviors in real time. In humans, inappropriate multisensory processing and real-time response occurs in many developmental disorders, such as autism, sensory processing disorder, ADHD, Tourette’s syndrome, and psychosis, as well as in older adults who experience falls and dementia. This study aims to reveal how brain cells and circuits give rise to dynamic behaviors in response to multisensory stimuli, laying the foundation for long-term understanding of normal and disordered sensorimotor function.

title: Single-cell in situ analysis of RNA modifications in intact tissues
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
RNA (the transcriptome) is central in the flow of biological information from DNA to protein, while chemical
modifications of RNA (the epitranscriptome) represent an additional layer of regulatory information to control cell
state and fate. Existing methods can only profile mRNA modifications from mixtures of millions of cells, limiting

single-cell and spatial resolutions, and further apply these new tools to reveal whether different cell types regulate

application_id: 11014625
activity_code: DP2
title: Discovery of cGAS-like signaling enzymes in innate immunity and disease
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Human cells use signals called “RNA second messengers” to stimulate the immune response, control cancer,
and guide correct tissue development. Surprisingly, our work has revealed that the best characterized gene
known to activate RNA second messenger signaling is part of a broad family of signaling enzymes and many
RNA second messenger pathways remain to be discovered. We have developed an innovative approach to
uncover the function of these enzymes and provide a detailed understanding of how RNA second messengers
can be harnessed to improve immune responses and prevent disease.

application_id: 11197964
activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement: Optically Promoting Cardiac Maturation Using Engineered Peptides
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Stem cell-based regenerative therapies or in vitro investigations of cardiovascular disease pathophysiology, drug screening, and toxicology studies heavily rely on stem cell quality. Here, peptide nanostructures will be engineered to enable cells to be light responsive in a wireless and non-genetic manner, which can ultimately be utilized as a strategy for converting optical cues to stimulatory signals that can improve the structural and functional maturity of human stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes. With this new approach, we hope to alleviate the major technical barrier of CM immaturity, and therefore, better enable stem cell-based cardiovascular research and related fields to flourish.

application_id: 11014629
activity_code: DP2
title: Mapping cell fate flow and feedback control on vertebrate embryonic landscapes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The research proposed here will provide crucial insights into how individual cells of vertebrate animals, such as
humans, differentiate into mature tissues. This work will promote better understanding of: (1) cellular principles
underlying fate restriction in development, (2) the genetic basis for embryonic failures, birth defects, and
developmental disorders, and (3) developmental mechanisms that impact how individual cells compete and
interact in embryonic tissues. Overall, the proposed research will accelerate our understanding of error-
correction processes that take place naturally in embryos, and will generate molecular predictions for how
these processes might be one day harnessed or modulated in human patients.

application_id: 11381093
activity_code: K01
title: Supporting Dementia Caregivers During Medicare Home Health: Developing the DECLARE Needs Assessment Intervention
org_name: VISITING NURSE SERVICE OF NEW YORK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Family caregivers often act as an extension of the care team during home health care for older adults, yet their
capacity and needs are not systematically assessed which limits their access to necessary support. This
proposal would develop, refine, and pilot test a self-assessment intervention for the home health setting to
improve communication and support between the care team and family caregivers to patients with Alzheimer’s
Disease and Related Dementias. Proposed research and training activities would support the career
development of Dr. Julia Burgdorf, whose career goal is to improve home- and community-based care for older
adults by developing pragmatic clinical interventions to advance provider-led engagement and support of family
caregivers.

application_id: 10931356
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating mechanisms of tissue polarity during development and disease
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Planar cell polarity (PCP) is a conserved biological pathway that is essential for tissue development and organ function. Alterations in PCP function result in severe developmental disorders such as spina bifida and congenital heart defects. We aim to uncover the underlying mechanisms that contribute to altered PCP function in developmental disorders and to further our understanding of how PCP established and regulated during vertebrate development.

activity_code: K01
title: Social and policy determinants and impacts on COVID-19 and influenza disparities
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
COVID-19 and influenza account for a substantial disease burden in the United States, and both show
substantial disparities. The proposed research uses descriptive, non-experimental, and agent based-modeling
to examine 1) neighborhood social determinants of COVID-19 and influenza acute care utilization
(hospitalizations and Emergency Department visits) under varying contexts, 2) effects of two policies (paid sick
leave and rent control) on these outcomes, and 3) develops hypothesis about the mechanisms linking two key
social determinants, occupational exposures and housing overcrowding, and influenza disparities. Findings will
advance our understanding of the social and policy drivers of infectious disease disparities and inform policies
and interventions that can mitigat...

activity_code: K99
title: Rare Airway Cell Function in Post-Viral Chronic Inflammation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Common viral respiratory infections, such as influenza and SARS-CoV-2, contribute to chronic lung
disease. Following viral infection, airway repair mechanisms lead to differentiation of unexpected airway
cell types within the lung, Microfold (M) and basal-like cells. This proposal will investigate the function of
rare airway cell types during post-viral injury repair.

application_id: 11397644
activity_code: K01
title: Contraceptive Autonomy: A Mixed-Methods Approach to Developing a Novel Family Planning Measure
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
Lack of contraceptive autonomy is associated with health inequities and adverse health outcomes ranging from
STIs to depression to low birth weight. However, without the tools to measure contraceptive autonomy,
researchers and policymakers are left without a clear idea of where and how contraceptive autonomy is
lacking, and which sociodemographic groups are most affected. The study proposed here will develop and
validate a standardized metric of contraceptive autonomy, documenting reproductive inequities and situating
the multidimensional construct of autonomy within cultural context.

application_id: 11412874
activity_code: K01
title: Effectively Linking Molecular Informatics and Network Analytics to Reduce Malaria (ELIMINAR-Malaria)
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative
Malaria kills over 600,000 people every year, and causes hundreds of millions of additional infections that
impose significant financial burdens on the individuals infected, their families, their communities, and the health
system. The Amazon region aims to eliminate the disease by 2030, but there are important gaps in our
understanding of malaria epidemiology that will prevent successful elimination. The objective of this proposal is
to address one of those gaps: how human mobility contributes to malaria population structure, importation, and
diffusion, and do so by leveraging data fusion to effectively link efficient, cost-effective survey and genotyping
methods.

title: A hospital discharge opioid taper support intervention to improve post-operative opioid prescribing
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Post-operative care is a time when excessive opioid prescribing occurs which can result in unnecessary harms, particularly to older and frail adults. Without a clear plan for reducing excessive opioid prescribing at hospital discharge after surgery such as with an opioid taper, opioids are often continued, which can result in unnecessary harms. This project will test the effectiveness of an EHR-embedded, provider-facing, patient-specific, discharge opioid taper support (DOTS) intervention to reduce excessive post-operative opioid prescribing.

application_id: 11360437
activity_code: DP2
title: Engineering Novel Precision Genome Editing Tools
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Half of all known human genetic variations that contribute to diseases such as sickle-cell disease, cystic
fibrosis, Hutchinson–Gilford progeria syndrome etc. are caused by point mutations [also known as single
nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs)]. Thus, there is a pressing need to develop precision genome editing tools
that are able to correct these SNPs with high efficiency and accuracy. Here we propose four innovative strategies
to design precision genome editing approaches that address the limitations of current genome editing tools and
have a wide range of biotechnological and biomedical applications: from broadening our understanding of how
human genetic variations impact health, through detection and treatment of genetic disorders, to crop and
livestock improvement.

activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of correlated neural activity in pair bonding using prairie voles
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Social relationships, which are intricately tied to our health and well-being, are necessarily reciprocal. The reciprocal interactions that underpin relationships are accompanied by a synchronization of neuronal activity across individuals, referred to as interbrain correlation, which scales as a function of relationship strength. In this proposal, I will measure and manipulate neural activity in monogamous prairie vole pairs to identify the functional contributions of correlated neural activity between animals within the context of pair bonding.

title: Integrated Nano-Opto-Fluidic System on Sapphire towards Single-Molecule Protein Sequencing
org_name: ARIZONA STATE UNIVERSITY-TEMPE CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Single-molecule protein sequencing (SMPS) is an emerging research direction that directly reads amino acids
sequence from individual protein or peptide molecules. However, it faces challenges in sensing system reliability,
slow readout, high noise, and high cost. Here we will design an on-chip integrated, electronic system that
incorporates all-sapphire, low-noise nanopores and multifunctional nanophotonic nanostructures to transduce
single-molecule protein fingerprints into electronic signals towards high-throughput, portable, and inexpensive
protein sequencing.

activity_code: DP2
title: Deciphering the mechanism of long-range gene regulation in vivo
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mutations leading to disease frequently affect distant-acting transcriptional enhancers, non-coding DNA
elements that regulate gene expression and that are often located hundreds of thousands of base pairs away
from their target genes. This project aims to determine how enhancers regulate their target genes over such long
genomic distances using novel genome editing, genomics, and imaging technologies, advancing an essential
foundation to understand the pathological consequences of single-nucleotide and structural variation in non-
coding regions of the human genome.

activity_code: DP2
title: Annotated lineage trees of murine development
org_name: DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
phr: Development starts from a single transformed cell, which ultimately divides into a mix of cells with many unique
roles in the body. It’s hard to fully understand this process as we currently do not have a global cell fate map
that describes this process. Here we propose inroads towards such a map, using improved versions of our
CRISPR lineage tracing approach with molecular recording technologies to track cell fate. We will create these
maps in generated mouse lines, providing new insight into how cellular roles are chosen and how overlapping
cellular programs shape diverse tissues and organs.

The proposed work seeks to fill a surprising knowledge gap: cardiovascular risks following wildfire smoke inhalation. Results from this work can be used to determine biomarkers associated with cardiovascular risk, guide emergency medical management, and inform policy decisions surrounding wildfire smoke exposure.

application_id: 11076224
activity_code: K01
title: Multimodal investigation of tau-related vasculopathy in prodromal Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative
Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a devastating, fatal illness that by far is the fastest-increasing cause of death in the
US and will affect as many as 1 in 45 Americans by 2047. The overall goal of this proposal is to study the
cerebrovascular dysfunction which is a critical component in AD pathogenesis using a multimodal MRI/PET
imaging approach. Successful completion of the current imaging study will yield much richer and more specific
information about underlying vascular pathology in AD and advance our understanding of AD pathophysiology
and evolution.
