application_id: 11077353
activity_code: K08
title: Endothelial cell-based therapy for pulmonary vascular disease using induced pluripotent stem cells
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pulmonary vascular diseases, such as pulmonary hypertension, are devastating disorders that are associated
with high morbidity and mortality with frustratingly limited treatment options. Endothelial cell dysfunction is a key
contributor to disease pathogenesis and stem cell-based therapies to replace diseased endothelial cells holds
immense promise for new therapeutic approaches to treat pulmonary vascular disease. This proposal leverages
a novel approach for induced pluripotent stem cell-derived endothelial replacement therapy to dissect the
mechanisms that promote cellular engraftment and to test the efficacy of this approach in genetic pulmonary
vascular disease models.

application_id: 11014665
activity_code: DP2
title: Unraveling the Telomere Black Box: A New Single-Molecule Approach to Define the Telomere Chromatin Landscape and its Functional Mechanisms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Narrative
Telomeres cap the ends of our linear chromosomes to protect our genome integrity; thus, their loss or
deregulation often leads to cancer or premature aging human diseases. While we understand the inputs and
outputs in telomere processes, we do not know what is going on inside the telomere “black box” - its chromatin
landscape. This proposal describes an innovative methodology using single-molecule DNA sequencing and
imaging technology to explore the telomere chromatin landscape and understand how it can regulate the
telomere functions.

application_id: 11173963
activity_code: DP2
title: Synthetic organogenesis: new paradigms in reconstituting human organ development in vitro
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Understanding how cells in the embryo interpret complex signals to create organs is one of the most challenging
and important questions in biology. We propose to develop in vitro models of organ development by combining
human stem cells, advanced tissue engineering, and genomic editing techniques that will allow us to study de-
tailed molecular mechanisms of organ formation in the human gut, from the lungs to the large intestine. The
proposed work will significantly advance the current paradigms in stem cell biology and will innovate technol-
ogies of recreating precise human organ tissues in a lab, and eventually enable high-throughput drug screening,
and physiological modeling of gut homeostasis, chronic inflammation, and infections.

application_id: 11144797
activity_code: DP2
title: Systematic Reconstruction of Epithelial-Mesenchymal Communication in Organ Development
org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
How the two major cell types (epithelial and mesenchymal cells) interact with each other to form different organs
in our body is a major question is biology. Answering this question is essential for understanding where different
organs come from and provides a crucial step towards engineering tissues for regenerative medicine. This
project will develop novel reconstitution-based approaches to systematically reconstruct the “communication
codes” between epithelial and mesenchymal cells, and to understand how distinct communication codes lead to
diverse cell types and tissue shapes among different organs.

application_id: 11064929
activity_code: DP2
title: Single cell dynamics on a whole organism scale
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite active cell proliferation, differentiation and apoptosis, mammalian organs tightly maintain their overall
size and function, indicative of a delicately balanced program that regulates cell population dynamics. This
proposal aims to develop powerful single-cell genomic approaches that enable a whole-organismal view of
single-cell spatiotemporal dynamics, and apply these methods to investigate how tissue homeostasis is
maintained and disrupted during aging at single-cell resolution, thereby contributing to the knowledge of basic
biology, as well as opening new therapeutic horizons for aging-related diseases.

application_id: 11075201
activity_code: K25
title: Agitation in Alzheimer's Disease: Identification and Prediction Using Digital Behavioral Markers and Indoor Environmental Factors
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Agitation is one of the most common and unmanageable neuropsychiatric symptoms among persons with
dementia, affecting 45-83% of this ever-growing population. Pharmacological treatment of agitation often has
adverse side effects, and time-constrained caregivers cannot reasonably be expected to monitor the patients
and take continuous note of the environment to identify precipitants of agitation. The research and training for
this Mentored Quantitative Research Development Award will be centered on identifying digital behavioral
markers both during and preceding agitation, identifying environmental precipitants of agitation, and building
risk prediction models of agitation using data collected from patients with later-stage dementia and their living
environment.

application_id: 11062756
activity_code: DP2
title: Uncovering the neural circuit and synaptic mechanisms underlying interval timing
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative
Temporal processing is central to cognition and behavior, and it has been hypothesized that memory deficits in
Alzheimer’s disease and Schizophrenia result from disrupted temporal coordination and temporal processing
within the brain. However, the neural mechanisms that underlie the brain’s ability to encode time remain largely
unknown. This proposal will address the question of how the brain perceives and stores memories of temporal
duration on the scales of seconds and minutes by leveraging cutting-edge optical imaging techniques in order to
monitor and manipulate neural activity as mice engage in virtual-reality based timing behaviors.

title: Linking genetics to cellular behavior and disease via multimodal data integration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
A large number of gene mutations that are associated with disease risk have been identified for diverse diseases.
Hypotheses about how these mutations confer disease risk and their effects on cells and tissues are needed in
order to prioritize therapeutic targets for intervention. This study proposes computational frameworks for
identifying gene mutations that affect cellular behaviors of individual human cells, therefore enabling prioritization
of therapeutic targets.

application_id: 10947702
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating the role of CSF flow in regulating CNS-adjacent bone marrow-derived myeloid cell egress after spinal cord injury
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Traumatic spinal cord injury (SCI) is a neurologic catastrophe resulting in varying degrees of motor, sensory, and sympathetic impairment, and a robust inflammatory response is a hallmark of this disease process. CSF is able to communicate with the CNS-adjacent bone marrow to stimulate hematopoiesis and myeloid cell egress back to the CNS border meninges and perivascular spaces, but the exact mechanism underlying this signaling remains unknown. In this proposal, we will determine if CSF flow rate is able to regulate myeloid egress, whether SCI disrupts this CSF flow leading to increased myeloid cells in the CNS after injury, and if manipulation of CSF flow to the bone marrow can ultimately improve recovery after SCI.

activity_code: F31
title: Understanding how senescent stromal cells contribute to mammary gland tumorigenesis
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Recent studies have revealed that senescent stromal cells can secret factors to modulate tumor immune surveillance and thus promote tumor progression. Given that such factors have been identified in human breast cancer tissues across molecular subtypes and immunotherapy response amongst breast cancer patient is far from ideal, it is critical to understand how senescent stromal cells contribute to mammary gland tumorigenesis. The aims proposed here will not only broaden our understanding in how senescent stroma shapes tumor microenvironment but also help to identify potential strategies to improve response to immunotherapy by targeting cellular senescence.

activity_code: DP2
title: How the 3D Architecture of the Brain Shapes Cancer Cell Fate Decisions
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Brain tumors have surpassed the leukemias as the leading cause of cancer-related death in children.
Therapeutic options for pediatric brain cancers have shown little expansion over the past few decades and the
prognosis for many tumor types (especially high-grade gliomas) remains bleak. The research proposed here will
identify tumor-associated contacts with the brain microenvironment that guide cellular state transitions, account
for tumor grade and can be weaponized to therapeutic ends.

activity_code: K25
title: Computational approaches to the mechanistic elucidation of the serrated pathway of human colon carcinogenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE AND RELEVANCE TO PUBLIC HEALTH
Colon cancer evolution from precursor lesions is described mainly by two pathways, the adenoma-carcinoma
sequence and the less understood 'alternative serrated pathway'. This application investigates the mech-
anisms of the serrated pathway of human colon carcinogenesis by applying computational approaches to
genomics and single cell/spatial transcriptomic data of a large cohort of precursor serrated lesions. Under-
standing the precursor potential to malignancy can considerably enhance prevention, diagnosis and the clinical
predictability of the serrated lesions and polyps.

title: Genomic tools for massively parallel recording of signaling activity at cellular resolution in a brain-wide manner
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Signaling pathways play an important role in mediating cell fate decisions during animal development but our
understanding of their functions at a tissue-wide and genomics-level scale are still lacking. This project will apply
single-cell RNA-sequencing and CRISPR-Cas barcoding to record signaling activity during zebrafish brain
development. Our technology will facilitate investigation of signaling and its effects on cell fate decisions at a
scale and depth that has not yet been achieved, and may inform strategies for translation into disease modeling
and regeneration.

application_id: 10949827
activity_code: K08
title: Adaptation and Testing of an Evidence-Based Sexual Health Communication Skills Training Intervention for Patients with Prostate Cancer
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Prostate cancer (PCa) is the most common non-cutaneous cancer in U.S. men and causes significant short- and long-term sexual side effects (i.e., sexual dysfunction) in up to 90% of patients. Although multiple evidence-based therapies for sexual dysfunction (e.g., psychotherapy, medications, surgery) exist, many PCa patients face significant barriers to communicating with their providers about sexual dysfunction, which impairs sexual recovery. We propose to rigorously adapt and pilot test an evidence-based sexual health communication skills training intervention for PCa patients to improve patient-provider communication about sexual health in PCa care and, thereby, facilitate improved sexual recovery and sexual health outcomes among PCa patients.

application_id: 11009704
activity_code: DP2
title: Defining the Chemical Perturbome of Neural Development and Activity
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Project Narrative
Chemicals can influence the building of a brain and its function, either as environmental contaminants or as
drug treatments for disorders such as autism and intellectual disability. Here, we provide new approaches to

neurodevelopmental disorders through discovering potential therapies and detecting neurotoxicants.

application_id: 11009685
activity_code: DP2
title: A Novel Approach to Crack Neuronal Mechanisms that Shape Computations in the Brain
org_name: MAX PLANCK FLORIDA CORPORATION
phr: Project Narrative
Our brains have an amazing flexibility to adapt and learn new behaviors. I will establish a novel
behavioral paradigm and experimental methods, linking molecules, neuronal activity, and
behavior, to identify brain areas and neuronal computations underlying such flexibility. Our
findings will provide a neurobiological basis to explain how dysfunction in adaptive learning
processes results in maladaptive behaviors observed in ADHD, OCD and other brain disorders.

application_id: 11009688
activity_code: DP2
title: How does neuromodulation shape the fluidity of spatial working memory?
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite the importance of working memory processing for healthy brain function, it remains poorly understood
how brains balance the inherent tension between mental flexibility and stability. This proposal combines a novel
approach with innovative methods to develop fundamental new knowledge about the circuit, cellular, and
synaptic mechanisms of working memory flexibility. An improved understanding of how the brain can dynamically
maintain and update internal representations is critical for top-down design of therapies for disorders which
disrupt cognitive processing, including ADHD, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 11184345
activity_code: DP5
title: Deconstructing aging with senolytic CAR T cells
org_name: COLD SPRING HARBOR LABORATORY
phr: NARRATIVE
Cellular senescence is a key but poorly understood determinant of organismal aging. Our proposal aims to
identify surface molecules of senescent cells in aging which will allow us to image, isolate and characterize them.
By harnessing cellular therapy directed against these surface targets we aim to flexibly interrogate senescence
biology in aging and develop a new treatment modality for age-related diseases.

activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement for: Impact of a Healthy Checkout Policy on Healthfulness of Grocery Environments and Sales
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: NARRATIVE Stores provide over two-thirds of calories and added sugar in the US diet, and advertising within stores influences consumer decisions. Results of this project can inform the development of healthy retail policies and programs and can facilitate dissemination of evidence-based policy.

title: Multi-modal sensory feedback mechanisms of fine and gross motor control in autism spectrum disorders
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS LAWRENCE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Atypical sensorimotor behaviors are common in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), and they appear to be mechanistically related to clinical and cognitive traits and distinct in autistic individuals with co-occurring intellectual disability (ID). The proposed studies will characterize the motor and neurophysiologic mechanisms of multisensory (visual and somatosensory) feedback processing during sustained fine and gross motor control in school-aged autistic children with and without ID. title: Sex chromosomal regulation of hippocampal microglial activation with Alzheimer's disease and aging
org_name: OKLAHOMA MEDICAL RESEARCH FOUNDATION
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
After decades of failed clinical trials for the treatment of Alzheimer’s disease (AD), there is an urgent need for

title: Poly-Nucleic acid complexes for chemokine modulation of the tumor microenvironment
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Nucleic acid-based therapies are poised to become the next generation of cancer therapies and can be readily

application_id: 11076330
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Environmental Context in Early Semantic Network Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The relationships between the words that toddlers know predict later language outcomes including vocabulary
size and speed of word recognition. The proposed project will advance our understanding of how toddlers use
environmental context as a cue to establish these semantic relationships between words and examine how
individual differences in this process might be related to differences in toddlers’ vocabularies. Findings will inform
theories of word learning and semantic development as well as provide insights into possible intervention targets
to improve language development outcomes.

application_id: 11078759
activity_code: K43
title: Delineating the kidney brain axis in children with severe malaria (KID-BRAIN)
org_name: MAKERERE UNIVERSITY COLLEGE OF HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project narrative
This K43 describes a career development plan for Dr Batte over 5 years to receive mentored research
training, undertake critical courses and training to develop into an independent researcher. Dr Batte will
delineate the mechanisms of kidney-related brain injury in severe malaria by determining the extent that
AKI phenotypes are associated with biosignatures of brain injury and recovery. Further, Dr Batte will
establish the extent that AKI and AKI phenotypes are associated with cerebral edema, and in addition
define the association between neurobehavioral outcomes, brain injury biomarkers and AKI phenotypes

application_id: 11127469
activity_code: K01
title: Developing and testing a multi-component intervention to improve Perinatal Mental Health
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Current approaches to prevention and treatment of depression and anxiety among pregnant and postpartum women often operate within larger health systems that 1) lack mental health providers, and 2) are not trustworthy for pregnant women, especially those who are from populations that experience health disparities. Scalable interventions that focus on improving perinatal mental health care are needed. We will develop and test an approach to linking the health system and the community to improve the reach and impact mental health care for pregnant and postpartum women with a particular focus on communities that are experiencing health disparities.

application_id: 11126610
activity_code: K76
title: Age-related changes in skeletal muscle and lower urinary tract symptoms in older adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because a majority of women and men will experience
lower urinary tract symptoms in their lifetime, but existing treatments have limited efficacy and important risks.
Understanding new age-related mechanisms of lower urinary tract symptoms will lead to more accurate
diagnosis and novel individualized treatments that improve or maintain health-related quality of life and
physical function in older adults. Thus, the proposed research is relevant to the NIA's strategic goal to
understand the dynamics of the aging process and to improve the health, well-being, and independence of
older adults.

title: Developing and testing a multi-component intervention to improve Perinatal Mental Health
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Current approaches to prevention and treatment of depression and anxiety among pregnant and postpartum individuals often operate within larger health systems that 1) lack mental health providers, and 2) are not trustworthy for birthing people, especially those who are from communities that have been marginalized. Scalable interventions that focus on improving perinatal mental health care for communities that have been marginalized are needed. We will develop and test an approach to linking the health system and the community to improve the reach and impact mental health care for pregnant and postpartum individuals with a particular focus on communities that have been marginalized.

application_id: 11145145
activity_code: K01
title: Phenotypic convergence at mitochondria in copy number variant disorders
org_name: VIRGINIA POLYTECHNIC INST AND ST UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The schizophrenia-associated copy number variants 3q29 deletion and 22q11.2 deletion produce strikingly
similar clinical phenotypes, but it is not known whether these high-risk alleles have convergent effects at cellular
and molecular levels. Our studies strongly indicate that 3q29 deletion produces mitochondrial phenotypes in the
central nervous system, which aligns with previous reports of mitochondrial phenotypes in 22q11.2 deletion. This
career development project is aimed at understanding the extent of these phenotypes in genetically controlled
human iPSC and mouse experimental model systems, with the goal of testing the hypothesis of phenotypic
convergence at mitochondria.

title: Identifying and Promoting Quality Low Back Pain Care by Chiropractors
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Low back pain affects 7-10% of the global population, is the leading cause of disability worldwide, and carries
significant direct and indirect costs. Recent initiatives have focused on improving the quality of low back pain
care being delivered, but challenges often remain to measure this in the clinical encounter. The proposed
research will quantify and promote the use of patient reported outcome measures as one element of high-
quality low back pain care by chiropractors through a clinical decision support tool while establishing a
foundation for future study assessing effect on clinical outcomes.

activity_code: F30
title: Investigating circuit-specific effects of high-frequency repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: NARRATIVE High-frequency repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (HF-rTMS) is a non-invasive method for clinical brain stimulation that can treat psychiatric disorders. HF-rTMS is thought to restore excitability in hypoactive circuits, but the mechanisms underlying the therapeutic effects of rTMS remain poorly understood. This project will determine how focal HF-rTMS induces plasticity in cortical circuits and whether particular classes of neurons underlie the behavioral effects of rTMS, leading to improved design of clinical treatments.

title: Metal-titanates, novel anti-caries catalysts for modulating the virulence of cariogenic biofilms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dental caries is an enormous health problem worldwide and there is a shift towards oral microbial community
dominated by cariogenic bacterium, eg: Streptococcus mutans, in caries-active subjects. Commensal bacteria
normally help to control S. mutans via bioactive products such as H2O2; however, high-sugar consumption,
which is frequently found in populations with high caries rates, could inhibit the generation of H2O2 through
carbohydrate catabolite repression and disrupt the homeostasis. This project will investigate if selective
photoactivated semiconducting metal titanates will produce extrinsic H2O2 from O2 reduction and other reactive
oxygen species (ROS), to inhibit S. mutans, while giving an advantage to commensal bacteria and thereby
maintain homeostasis in dental...

title: Decoding ensemble dynamics from cortico-amygdalar circuits during social choice
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY

application_id: 10949273
activity_code: K99
title: Enhancing the Interpretability and Applicability of Polygenic Scores through Multi-Omics Integration and Analysis of Family-Based Studies
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This proposal aims to create a series of new statistical methods to help us better utilize genetic scores in the study of the causes behind intricate human diseases. It will incorporate multi-omics data to facilitate molecularly- informed disease pathways and assess the impact of each pathway on the disease, and also use family-based studies to provide unbiased investigations of how multiple correlated environmental factors or biomarkers influence diseases. It will further provide easy-to-use software tools and a summary statistics database for researchers to analyze large-scale multi-modal data to accelerate future research, enabling a more comprehensive and unbiased understanding of how molecular changes contribute to human diseases.

org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE/RELEVANCE STATEMENT Innovations in medicine, particularly for diseases that disparately affect minority and underserved populations, are at risk because those entering the biomedical research career path continue to underrepresent certain historically excluded populations. The Duke Paired Undergraduate Mentoring Program (PUMP) in Uronephrology is a sustainable program that will develop a diverse pipeline and enhance success in academic careers by providing mentorship and skills-building, thereby preparing the next generation of scientists to advance urology and nephrology-related disease prevention, treatment, and cure. application_id: 11160743
activity_code: K23
title: Initial Evidence for a Brief Psychological Telehealth Intervention for Patients with Chronic Masticatory Muscle Pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chronic masticatory muscle pain disorders (MMPD) affect between 5-12% of the adult population and are the
most common cause of chronic non-odontogenic orofacial pain. Combining standard dental care with brief
telehealth psychological interventions offers hope for reducing negative pain outcomes and improving quality of
life in this population, but few such interventions exist. The proposed submission will generate data to support
a future full-scale Phase II efficacy trial of a brief telehealth psychological intervention for MMPD, potentially
leading to an efficacious, widely implementable, and disseminatable treatment option for patients with chronic
MMPD.

application_id: 10948413
activity_code: K99
title: Multi-omic investigation of the adiposity-lipid intersection and its role in cardiovascular disease risk
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE Anti-obesity medications designed to reduce excess body fat or “adiposity” contribute to weight loss and substantially reduce the risk of heart disease. The genetic impact of adiposity on lipid levels—both critical risk factors for heart disease—remains uncertain but holds considerable clinical and therapeutic potential. This proposal will leverage comprehensive clinical and multi-omics data from large-scale cohorts to uncover key biological components at the intersection of adiposity and lipid pathways, potentially advancing heart disease risk assessment and identifying new therapeutic targets for individuals with obesity.

application_id: 10904692
activity_code: K08
title: Impact of clonal hematopoiesis mutations on toxicity and outcomes following oncologic therapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) mutations are acquired mutations in the blood present in the majority of older individuals that may contribute to worse oncologic outcomes in patients with solid malignancy. In this project we will use individual genetic and clinical data to determine whether CH mutations and cancer therapies interact to result in worse cancer treatment toxicity and outcomes and to investigate whether there are clinical markers to determine which individuals may be most at risk for these adverse outcomes. Completion of this project will provide key insight regarding the importance of CH mutations in patients with solid malignancy and guide future investigations regarding whether knowledge of CH mutations in patients with solid malignancy can improve cancer care.

phr: The Summer Training as Research Scholars (STaRS) at Boston University is a summer Research Education Program for outstanding undergraduate and early medical school students. title: Harnessing multivariate patient- and population-level disease trajectories to predict major clinical events in scleroderma
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Scleroderma is a complex rheumatic disease with significant variability in clinical presentation, disease course,
response to therapy and prognosis. This proposal will develop methods to identify patients at risk of progression
who may benefit from earlier and more intensive disease monitoring and therapeutic strategies. This includes
approaches to generate personalized risk estimates of major complications from multivariate longitudinal data,

application_id: 10948951
activity_code: K01
title: Endothelial S1PR1 promotes lung repair in post-viral ARDS
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Viral respiratory infections, such as influenza and coronaviruses, frequently lead to acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), a condition with a mortality up to 46% where up to half of survivors continue to suffer from chronic respiratory disability due to persistent pulmonary fibrosis. Endothelial injury and dysfunction contribute to the severity of ARDS, persistence of lung injury, and dysregulated repair. The proposed studies will elucidate how the pulmonary endothelium promotes repair of the closely associated alveolar epithelial layer after viral infection-induced lung injury and identify novel translational targets to prevent morbid post-viral fibrotic outcomes.

application_id: 10861735
activity_code: K23
title: Novel Neurocognitive Markers of Risk for and Resilience to Cognitive Decline in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: RHODE ISLAND HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease will affect an estimated 14 million Americans by the year 2050, contributing to economic
burden and reduced quality of life for patients and caregivers. As the U.S. population ages, it is critically
important to identify who is at greatest risk for Alzheimer’s disease-associated cognitive decline. Studying
novel markers of risk for and resilience to cognitive decline in cognitively normal older adults may identify those
who would benefit most from early detection and treatment strategies for Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 11088017
activity_code: F31
title: Study the role and origin of hybrid insulin peptides in the pathogenesis of type 1 diabetes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative  Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition in which the insulin producing beta cells are selectively targeted for destruction. The specific autoantigens that are critical for disease onset have yet to be identified, however, we have identified autoantigens that may play a significant role in the development of disease. The current proposal aims to determine the origin of these autoantigens and to study if the level of these autoantigens can affect the pathogenesis of type 1 diabetes. Results from these studies may allow us to establish a disease-critical role of the antigens in the pathogenesis of diabetes.

phr: Project Narrative Object recognition lies at the heart of our ability to make sense of the visual world, engaging a series of hierarchical processing in the ventral temporal cortex (VTC) and medial temporal lobe (MTL) network, which coordinately transforms the visual inputs to semantic representation. title: Neonatal predictors of contextualized emotion processing and risk for anxiety
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project will examine how early variation in naturalistic emotion processing—how we attend to, interpret, and respond to emotional cues—are linked to risk for depression and anxiety in a large longitudinal sample of infants. Specifically, we will examine how neonatal activation to salient stimuli is linked to how infants attend to naturalistic emotions, and how this association is moderated by infant temperament. This proposal will therefore provide important insight to the etiology of depression and anxiety as well as important training opportunities in infant mental health methods.

application_id: 10772006
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Neutrophils in Ischemia/Reperfusion Injury following Acute Stroke
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A stroke is caused by a lack of blood flow to the brain, usually due to the blockage of blood vessels, resulting in damage to the brain tissue. Inflammation, the body's reparative response to tissue damage, can worsen the initial damage to the brain produced by the stroke when blood flow is restored. Therefore, a better understanding of inflammation in the brain after a stroke may improve the effectiveness of anti-inflammatory therapies.

application_id: 10877013
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the Mitochondrial and Nuclear functions of ATP Synthase Subunit ATP5A1 that Maintain Genome Integrity in Response to Oxidative Stress
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Maintenance of genome integrity is a fundamental function of all cellular life that depends upon robust antioxidant systems to defend against reactive oxygen species. I discovered a novel nuclear function for ATP synthase subunit alpha (ATP5A1) to respond to oxidative stress that involves suppression of RNA/DNA hybrids and protection from DNA damage. This project will further elucidate the role of ATP5A1 in coordinating oxidative metabolism with genome maintenance and lead to new research and therapeutic opportunities at the interface of oxidative stress, genome maintenance, and cancer.

title: Mural stem cells in normal development and germinal matrix hemorrhage in the preterm human brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Fundamental questions remain about the origin of brain blood vessel cells and their interactions with the developing neural tissue. This project will define how human brain mural cells contribute to angiogenesis and neurogenesis and build a suite of tools to interrogate specific signaling pathways in mural cells. title: Social Reward Processing Across the Lifespan: Identifying Risk Factors for Financial Exploitation Diversity Supplement
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: Project Narrative Risk for financial exploitation has both been associated with diminished cognitive abilities (e.g., reduced executive functioning), Alzheimer’s Disease, depressive symptoms, and a range of demographic variables (e.g., socioeconomic status, sex, and ethnicity). application_id: 11032108
activity_code: F32
title: Learning-induced synaptic plasticity in corticospinal output neurons of motor cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Corticospinal neurons serve as the output of motor cortex and coordinate muscle activation during voluntary movements. Using advanced imaging techniques in behaving animals, this proposal aims to address how synaptic plasticity in these output neurons can modify spike output in order to drive learned behaviors, thus revealing how the brain can be dynamically altered during motor learning. Findings generated from this proposal will elucidate a specific function for synaptic plasticity at the corticospinal node in the motor pathway, which is highly relevant in the context of spinal cord injury, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and other neurological diseases that affect learning and/or movement.

application_id: 10741804
activity_code: F31
title: Cellular mechanisms of perivascular fibroblast development and dependence on PDGF signaling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Perivascular fibroblasts (PVFs) reside within perivascular spaces of the adult murine brain, are identified by expression of Collagen-1 and Platelet-derived growth factor receptors (PDGFR)a and PDGFRb, and have almost exclusively been studied in the context of stroke and injury. However, almost nothing is known about PVF developmental origins and timing, or the cellular and molecular mechanisms involved in their development. This will be the focus of my investigations, and the results from this work will allow for novel tools and methods for studying the homeostatic and disease-related functions of PVFs.

title: Supporting Personalized Decision Making for Breast Cancer Screening among Older Women
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Whether to continue breast cancer screening after the age of 74 is a complex clinical decision, and the balance of benefits and harms may vary by patient. application_id: 11088861
activity_code: K23
title: Development of Emergent PET Tracers in Frontotemporal Lobar Degeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is a leading cause of dementia and one of the most common causes of early-
onset dementia. Diverse molecular pathologies cause FTD (collectively known as frontotemporal lobar
degeneration [FTLD] pathology), which has complicated the development of positron emission tomography
(PET) tracers for these diseases. This project will evaluate and validate new PET tracers, which could improve
FTD/FTLD diagnosis and differential diagnosis and facilitate drug development for these diseases.

application_id: 11082314
activity_code: T35
title: Training Medical Students in NIDDK Research
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
The mandate of NIDDK is to reduce the disease burden of diabetes and other hormonal diseases, obesity,
kidney and gastro-intestinal and liver diseases in the US and the world at large. We need to provide the
scientific manpower to lead this effort and this proposal aims to enhance the pipeline by interesting medical
students to do research in these fields. In the past 4 years we have supported 51 students in laboratories of
successful scientists who investigate the nature and treatment of these diseases.

application_id: 11184425
activity_code: K01
title: Contraceptive Autonomy: A Mixed-Methods Approach to Developing a Novel Family Planning Measure
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
Lack of contraceptive autonomy is associated with health inequities and adverse health outcomes ranging from
STIs to depression to low birth weight. However, without the tools to measure contraceptive autonomy,
researchers and policymakers are left without a clear idea of where and how contraceptive autonomy is
lacking, and which sociodemographic groups are most affected. The study proposed here will develop and
validate a standardized metric of contraceptive autonomy, documenting reproductive inequities and situating
the multidimensional construct of autonomy within cultural context.

application_id: 11143133
activity_code: R25
title: MSK Computational Biology Summer Program
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: R25 MSK Computational Biology Summer Program: Project Narrative
The MSK Computational Biology Summer Program (CBSP) aims to train the next generation of computer
science savvy students in cancer biology. Biologists are trained to form a hypothesis and provide data to
support it, but in the current big data era, many can’t find the insights they seek in the vast troves of data
they’ve collected. CBSP participants, through exposure to cancer biology topics, can use their skills to develop
new tools and mine big data towards finding the next generation of cancer therapeutics, patient diagnostics,
and furthermore, can uncover novel biological and mechanistic insights into cancer.

title: Impact of the Therapeutic Alliance on Reduction of Disparities in Latino End-of-Life Cancer Care
org_name: BECKMAN RESEARCH INSTITUTE/CITY OF HOPE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer is the leading cause of death among Latinos in the US, who now represent the largest minority group. Latino cancer patients engage in advance care planning less often and receive less value-consistent care at the end-of-life more often than non-Latino cancer patients. The proposed research addresses the pressing need to reduce Latino EoL care disparities by developing an intervention for improving the therapeutic alliance between oncologists and their Latino cancer patients.

title: Improving Patient-Centered Decision-Making for Stress Urinary Incontinence Treatment in Older Men
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Male stress urinary incontinence (SUI), a frequent result of radical prostatectomy for prostate cancer,
significantly reduces health-related quality of life among older men. Surgical treatment options are known to
provide near-term improvement in quality of life with high satisfaction rates but are underutilized and patients
have regret about treatment decisions. The goal of this K76 proposal is to improve goal-concordant treatment
decisions for SUI management in older men by identifying disparities in surgical care and addressing these
disparities by developing a geriatric-informed patient-centric decision support tool to promote individualized,
goal-concordant decision-making for SUI treatment decisions among older men, which will then be tested in an
R01 multi-institu...

application_id: 10917150
activity_code: K08
title: The impact of the aging immune system on periodontal disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Proper regulation of inflammation within bone is critical for preventing disease and healing from injury. A dysregulated immune response becomes more prevalent in elderly populations and results in a susceptibility to numerous conditions, including periodontal disease. The goal of this project is to understand the age-related perturbations that occur to the cellular and molecular regulators of the innate immune response in bone.

application_id: 10897782
activity_code: F32
title: Skin-like wearable biosensors for multimodal mental health biomarker monitoring
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mental disorders affect one in four people worldwide; however, the lack of quantifiable metrics for mental health
disorders contributes to the challenges of diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment. The goal of this research
proposal is to develop a skin-like wearable sensor system to monitor physical and chemical mental health
biomarker as an approach to quantify mental health state. I will design and fabricate physical biosensors to
monitor skin conductance and chemical biosensors to monitor sweat cortisol levels, using intrinsically stretchable
materials to provide high signal-to-noise ratio recordings and minimal discomfort without social stigma.

application_id: 10748277
activity_code: K01
title: Neuronal Gαi-GPCR targeting to primary cilia and impact on cAMP mediated transcription
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Extracellular stimuli such as, hormones, neurotransmitters and drugs, work by activating receptors to induce cell
signaling which leads to physiological responses. The proposed project focuses on neuronal receptors, key drug
targets for neuropsychiatry, that reside in specialized neuronal compartments, whose functions are unknown,
called primary cilia. This proposal will examine three important questions (i) how different neuronal receptors are
selected to be transported to primary cilia, (ii) how receptors signal in the cilium and (iii) what impact the signaling
has on neuronal gene expression changes that influence neuronal function.

title: Diverse Patterns of Activity-Dependent Alternative Splicing
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Cognitive and neuropsychiatric disorders have been linked to aberrant alternative splicing of various ion
channels. Defects in ion channel splicing can dysregulate ion channel contributions to neuroplasticity. A
mechanistic understanding of the activity-dependence of alternative splicing of ion channels would therefore
advance our knowledge of the underlying causes of abnormal physiology and brain disorders.

title: GLUCOSE BINDS RBPs TO REGULATE EPIDERMAL HOMEOSTASIS
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: GLUCOSE BINDS RBPs TO REGULATE EPIDERMAL HOMEOSTASIS  Project Narrative The maintenance of epithelial tissue is orchestrated by an interplay of regulatory factors that control transcription, RNA processing, translation and protein modification that together balance the decisions to divide, differentiate or enter senescence. Breakdowns in this regulatory system lead to skin diseases, such as psoriasis or cancer. In this proposal, we aim to elucidate the molecular mechanisms by which glucose regulates the activity of RNA- binding proteins and their interacting targets in the context of epidermal differentiation, with the ultimate goal of developing new therapeutic strategies for the treatment of skin diseases.

application_id: 11053130
activity_code: K99
title: The role of BNST PKCdelta neurons in compulsive ethanol intake
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In the treatment seeking population of Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) patients, periods of abstinence define a
highly vulnerable portion of the AUD cycle, as during this time individuals report heightened craving and
negative affect; however, neuroadaptations underlying these behaviors remain poorly understood. This
proposal is designed to test the hypothesis that abstinence from ethanol exposure induces changes in BNST
PKCδ neuron activity and their associated circuitry to drive compulsive ethanol intake. Completion of these
studies will yield novel information related to the regulation of extended amygdala circuitry in the context of
individual differences in ethanol behaviors, critical to the development of specialized therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 11089292
activity_code: F31
title: Contribution of Levodopa to Brain Iron Accumulation
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Parkinson’s disease (PD) patients suffer from elevated levels of brain iron with levodopa (L-DOPA) commonly
used by clinicians as a first-line antiparkinson drug. This project will research how our standard administration of
L-DOPA impacts critical properties of iron homeostasis with the goal of identifying key iron mechanisms in the
blood-brain barrier which will be confirmed by utilizing an in-vitro blood-brain barrier model and in-vivo murine
model. This research will allow for the understanding of the mechanism of iron transport after L-DOPA exposure
and target critical regulators to reduce brain iron accumulation and improve antiparkinson drug therapy
development.

application_id: 10862803
activity_code: K08
title: The Fibrosis-Immunity Interface in Corneal Transplantation
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS EYE AND EAR INFIRMARY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Corneal transplantation is the most commonly performed type of solid organ transplantation in the world and T cell-mediated alloimmune rejection is the most common cause of corneal transplant failure. We have recently demonstrated fibrosis in rejected corneal transplants, and in the proposed research we will investigate the interactions between T cell alloimmunity and fibrosis in corneal transplantation. These studies will provide fundamental insights in a previously unexplored area and have the potential to help identify novel therapeutic strategies for i) preventing transplant fibrosis and failure and ii) regulating T cell-mediated immune rejection.

application_id: 10975854
activity_code: F30
title: Metabolic Obesity Phenotypes and Obesity-related Cancer Survival
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Despite the overwhelming evidence that metabolic dysfunction and obesity increase the risk of multiple cancers, evidence on whether these exposures are associated with survival after a cancer diagnosis is limited. Using both a large prospective epidemiologic cohort and electronic medical record data from a comprehensive cancer center, we will examine the relationship between obesity-related cancer and metabolic dysfunction after cancer diagnosis using metabolic obesity phenotypes. Results will potentially inform clinical care for cancer patients at highest risk of metabolic dysfunction and improve their survivorship experience.

activity_code: F32
title: Microglia-Mediated Astrocyte Activation in the Acute-to-Chronic Pain Transition
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Preventing acute pain from becoming chronic is a promising therapeutic target. Astrocyte activation is an important component of the chronic pain state. This application proposes to use a combination of molecular, imaging, and behavioral approaches to investigate how astrocyte activation is modulated by microglia and the role of microglia-mediated astrocyte activation in chronic pain. The results from the experiments proposed in this study will elucidate the role of microglia in astrocyte activation and determine if microglia-derived differences in astrocyte state contribute to pain behaviors, providing new therapeutic targets.

activity_code: K23
title: A Fully Remote Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial to Investigate the Impact of Insomnia Treatment on Crohn’s disease
org_name: DARTMOUTH-HITCHCOCK CLINIC
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Symptoms of insomnia are common in people with Crohn’s disease and may predict greater likelihood of disease flare, surgery, hospitalization, and reduced quality of life. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) is highly effective for treating insomnia and may reduce chronic pain and inflammation, both significant problems in Crohn’s disease. In the proposed mixed methods study, we seek to investigate the feasibility, acceptability, and impact CBT-I in 60 adults with Crohn’s disease in order to improve understanding of treatment of insomnia, pain, and inflammation and inform the development of a rigorous NIH R01 application.

activity_code: F32
title: The Role of SMARCAL1 Depletion on Increased Immunogenicity in ALT+ Gliomas
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE.
SMARCAL1 is a novel tumor specific therapeutic target for high grade gliomas (HGGs) that utilize alternative
lengthening of telomeres (ALT). We will investigate the effects of targeting SMARCAL1 on inflammation
within tumor cells and the ability of immune cells to recognize and respond. These studies will provide novel
insight into the mechanisms governing tumor-intrinsic inflammatory signaling and how they can be targeted
for glioma immunotherapy.

The Graduate Research Training Initiative for Student Enhancement at the University of South Dakota (G-
RISE USD) will develop a biomedical doctoral training program that produces a highly qualified, diverse pool of
biomedical scientists and prepares them for a variety of potential career paths. The program will seek to train
students from diverse populations that reflect South Dakota and the surrounding region, emphasizing
American Indians, Latinos, 1st generation college students, economically disadvantaged students, and students
from rural backgrounds. These trainees will be prepared for careers in the public and private sector through
professional development workshops and access to graduate level classes in the fields of business, law,
communication, and education.
terms: <South Dakota><Universities>
---
application_id: 10840792
activity_code: T32
title: Viral Persistence and Pathogenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Viral diseases remain a leading cause of death worldwide, necessitating an increase in training in virology.
The complexity and diversity of persistence and pathogenesis mechanisms utilized by viruses pose major
challenges to the development of effective vaccines and chemical or immune-based treatments of viral
diseases. A goal of the training program is the recruitment of undergraduates and recent Ph.D. graduates in
related disciplines (biochemistry, biological sciences, cellular and molecular biology, etc) into advanced studies
in mechanisms of viral persistence and pathogenesis.
terms: <Pathogenesis><Viral>
---
application_id: 10903009
activity_code: F31
title: How does the brain accumulate cognitive maps?
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative Cognitive decline during aging involves impairments in using stored information to support learning in new environments, leading to decreased behavioral flexibility. This proposal will investigate whether the similarity between cognitive maps in the dorsal CA1 region of the hippocampus changes as mice accumulate multiple cognitive maps across different age groups. Understanding how similarity between cognitive maps aids in behavioral flexibility is critical to understanding the neural signaling alterations that underlie cognitive decline and can pave the way for therapeutic interventions that will improve the quality of life for an aging population.

application_id: 11068460
activity_code: F31
title: A multimodal examination of Alzheimer's disease pathology and synaptic loss
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Synaptic loss is strongly associated with cognitive dysfunction in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and autopsy studies show that loss of synapses is closely linked with tau pathology; however, this has been difficult to measure in living people. The proposed project will examine the extent to which tau pathology is associated with synaptic loss using neuroimaging and blood biomarkers of AD, which will inform on the progression of AD and potential windows for treating this disease. This project will also support the training of a promising pre-doctoral student to address the nation's biomedical, behavioral, and clinical research needs in AD.

application_id: 10744784
activity_code: F32
title: The role of mitochondrial redox stress in the impaired Nrf2 response to contraction in aged muscle
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative Aging organisms show impaired redox homeostasis and a blunted ability to respond to redox stressors. This is associated with declining stress resilience and increased risk for age-associated diseases. We propose to manipulate the chronic mitochondrial redox stress in models of acute and chronic exercise in young and aged mice to test whether mitochondrial dysfunction underlies the decreased adaptive response with age that drives the decline in muscle function and healthspan.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE HIV continues to be a major cause of death and suffering worldwide. Our program supports training of early career stage investigators to become independent, patient-based and translational scientists addressing the highest impact research questions in HIV that are designed to reach affected populations. application_id: 10839469
activity_code: T32
title: The UConn/JAX-GM Training Program in Genomic Science
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT SCH OF MED/DNT
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Since the rapid drop in cost for genome sequencing, the field of genomics has experienced a
bottleneck in computationally trained scientists capable of interpreting and transforming this data
into actionable information to improve human health. We propose a new training program, the
UConn Health/JAX-GM Genomic Sciences Training Program, to help fill this gap and provide pre-
doctoral scientists with the tools they need to accomplish this goal.

title: Mechanism of mitochondria-induced myokine activation and implications for healthy aging.
org_name: UPSTATE MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to investigate the activation mechanisms of myokine signaling in
response to mitochondrial protein import stress. We will validate the role of a specific
myokine in promoting body mass homeostasis and cardiac health. This research holds
the potential to advance our understanding of cellular stress adaptation and its
implications for metabolic and cardiovascular health, opening new avenues for targeted
therapeutic interventions to reduce the burden of metabolic and cardiovascular
diseases.

application_id: 10995481
activity_code: F31
title: Exploring the Role of Microglia SYK Signaling in Tauopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE More than seven million Americans are currently living with Alzheimer's disease (AD) and related tauopathies, and although significant progress has been made in delineating critical innate immune receptors involved in disease progression, much of the downstream signaling molecules modulating this immune response remain inadequately described. In our preliminary studies using a tau-based model of AD and frontotemporal dementia, we have identified an integral role for the immune signaling kinase, SYK. This project aims to further characterize the role of SYK in the exacerbation AD pathology through the regulation of microglia activation and to also evaluate if delayed SYK deletion is therapeutically efficacious in limiting tauopathy onset and progression.

Alzheimer’s disease is a debilitating neurodegenerative condition with major contributions to risk and

future Alzheimer’s risk is not well understood at the individual phenotypic level. The proposed research will use

and clinician understanding of modifiable Alzheimer’s risk factors and provide insight into the neuroimmune

We are proposing an innovative new training program to encourage surgical residents to pursue

title: Functional epitranscriptomic profiling of m6A RNA modifications in Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) is the most prevalent neurodegenerative disease and is characterized by amyloid beta
(Aβ) accumulation, microtubule associated protein tau (MAPT, tau) aggregation, and synaptic deterioration. N6-
methyladenosine (m6A)-modified RNA has been shown to increase in AD, interact with pathological Aβ and tau
in AD, and regulate synaptic function; however, it is not well understood which m6A-modified RNAs interact with
Aβ and tau and contribute to synaptic degradation in AD. Using a novel m6A sequencing approach, this proposal
will elucidate the m6A-modified sites and transcripts in AD, distinguish the Aβ- and tau-driven m6A modification
changes on RNA, and uncover the role of aberrant m6A RNA modifications in synaptic dysfunction, contributing
fundamenta...

application_id: 10835960
activity_code: T32
title: Research Training in Non-Pharmacological Interventions for Cognition in Aging, MCI, and Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
This competing continuation of a 15-year predoctoral training program is focused on research training for the
design and evaluation of cognitive interventions for older adults. The program has three substantive
emphases: (a) behavioral interventions (e.g., cognitive training, exercise, mindfulness) for unimpaired older
adults; (b) multi-component compensatory interventions for persons with cognitive impairment; (c)
neuromodulation/stimulation interventions. We propose to continue this successful program with six pre-
doctoral slots a year, reserved for students beyond the first year of doctoral study.

application_id: 10998809
activity_code: F31
title: Synergistic contributions of cerebrovascular disease and neuroinflammation to Alzheimer's disease in adults with Down syndrome
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This study will examine the independent and interactive contributions of cerebrovascular disease, neuroinflammation, and blood-brain barrier (BBB) disruption to cognitive decline and the progression of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) pathophysiology in adults with Down syndrome (DS). Virtually all adults with DS develop AD and progress to dementia by age 60, but the mechanisms are poorly understood. Through rigorous examination of the relationships of cerebrovascular disease, neuroinflammation, and BBB disruption with AD pathophysiology and cognitive outcomes, using neuroimaging, biofluid, and neuropathological markers, this study has the potential to contribute to our understanding of the pathways underlying AD in adults with DS and the neurotypical population.

title: Trafficking and translation of mRNA in osteocyte dendrites
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative: Osteocytes are bone-embedded cells that extend long projections to connect to other cells in bone, which allows them to control bone remodeling and contribute to bone strength. To better understand how osteocytes form and maintain these dendrite-like projections, we will examine gene and protein expression within the projections themselves. Discovery of new mechanisms that control osteocyte dendrites may lead to new therapeutics targeting osteocytes to improve bone strength and reduce fractures.

activity_code: F30
title: Defining mechanisms of perianal fistula inception in African and European ancestry Crohn's disease patients
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Perianal fistulae are a complication of Crohn’s disease with poor treatment response that affects patients of African ancestry more frequently and with greater morbidity. These studies will explore differences in protein and gene expression between African and European ancestry patients with perianal fistula at the single cell level, and establish an in vitro rectal organoid model to characterize the lipid and cytokine mediators that contribute to fistula inception. The combination of these approaches will better define gene modules that distinguish perianal fistulizing Crohn’s disease between ancestry groups and inform the discovery of novel targets for the treatment of perianal fistula.

title: Investigating the role of calorie restriction-induced blood factors in rejuvenating the aged hippocampus
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The research described in this proposal will investigate the role of calorie restriction (CR)-induced blood factors in rejuvenating the aged brain. The proposed study will delineate cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the rejuvenating effects of CR blood plasma on learning and memory processes in the aged hippocampus and identify individual circulating blood factors as potential therapeutic targets. application_id: 11070830
activity_code: F31
title: Deciphering the function of cohesin and CTCF in R-loop-mediated DNA repair at telomeres
org_name: NORTH CAROLINA STATE UNIVERSITY RALEIGH
phr: NARRATIVE Cohesin and its cofactor, CCCTC-binding factor (CTCF), have been shown to 1) localize to telomeres, the protective ends of linear chromosomes, and 2) directly bind R-loops, which promote DNA repair through poorly understood mechanisms. While R-loop accumulation at telomeres is linked to telomere shortening and accelerated aging, many questions remain about how telomeric R-loops are regulated and how they function in the telomere DNA damage response. Based on our recent discoveries of cohesin’s role in R-loop-mediated DNA repair at genomic sites, we will explore a potential functional link between telomeric R-loops, cohesin, and CTCF in the telomere DNA damage response.

activity_code: F31
title: Effect of Mucins and Dolosigranlulum pigrum on Staphylococcus aureus nasal colonization
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Staphylococcus aureus infections are a major healthcare threat responsible for over 80,000 hospitalizations every year, and the majority of these infections originate from S. aureus carried in the nose. This project will address how mucus in the nasal passages and colonization by a potentially beneficial bacterium, Dolosigranulum pigrum, can prevent S. aureus colonization in the nose and therefore prevent serious infections.

title: Impact of T cell receptor signaling on memory CD8+ T cell stemness
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 CD8+ T cells regulate the adaptive response to infection, antitumor immunity, and autoimmunity through

application_id: 11121885
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms of HIV-1 Hypersensitivity to Islatravir
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Drug resistance mutations from lack of patient adherence to daily dosing regimens reduce the effectiveness of
HIV-1 treatment; therefore, potent inhibitors with the potential to be long-acting are needed to combat this
problem. Islatravir (EFdA) is a promising potent, long-acting antiviral that targets HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, an
essential enzyme in the HIV-1 replication cycle. I will study a drug mutation that causes resistance to one type
of reverse transcriptase inhibitor but increases susceptibility to EFdA, which will aid in developing optimized
combination treatments for future HIV-1 therapies.

application_id: 11237082
activity_code: F31
title: Structural and Biochemical Effects of Capsid-targeting Molecules on HIV-1 Capsid Assembly
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
For the tens of millions of individuals living with human immunodeficiency virus 1 (HIV-1), antiretroviral therapies
are life-saving medicines that prevent the onset of acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). Our lab
previously reported compounds with anti-HIV-1 activity that belong to a unique class of antiretrovirals not
currently used in clinical practice. Here, I aim to determine how these compounds modify the assembled
structures of the HIV-1 virion and to establish the biochemical parameters of compound binding to lay the
groundwork for the next generation of HIV-1 therapeutics in order to help outpace antiviral drug resistance.

application_id: 11258923
activity_code: F31
title: The Molecular Mechanism and Pathophysiology of Robust STAT3 Signaling During Oral Salmonella Typhimurium Infection
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium, one of the most common causes of bacterial gastroenteritis, has a
repertoire of secreted protein effectors that promote virulence. The effector SarA hijacks host gene expression
but how this impacts oral infection is unknown. Through integrating biochemical, cellular, and mouse infection
models, this proposed research will elucidate the signaling mechanisms of SarA and reveal how this effector
shapes disease in the gut.

title: UC Davis Comparative Oncology Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
The UC Davis Comparative Oncology T32 program will provide an outstanding environment for biomedical
research training. The goal is to train 1) veterinarian- and physician-scientists and 2) graduate students

application_id: 11128671
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the Role of Retinal Microglia and Infiltrating Monocytes on Photoreceptor Cell Death in Retinal Detachment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Retinal detachment is an ocular emergency that can lead to permanent blindness due to photoreceptor cell
death. Detachment of the retina can occur in all ages, from newborns to adults, and is characterized by active
infiltration and activation of local microglia and peripheral monocyte/macrophages. Through the successful
execution of this proposal, we will gain new mechanistic insights to effectively target these populations and
develop novel neuroprotective strategies to prevent vision loss.

application_id: 10745650
activity_code: F31
title: Progression and differential control of postural and kinetic tremor with deep brain stimulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Narrative While deep brain stimulation (DBS) does offer symptom relief for most patients with Essential Tremor (ET), DBS therapy is not as effective at reducing tremors during voluntary movement and has been shown to lose effectiveness over time. Understanding of the neural, muscular, and failure mechanisms underlying tremor control and progression with DBS could help combat these therapeutic challenges and result in improved patient outcomes. This project will integrate high-field magnetic resonance imaging, patient-specific pathway activation models of DBS, quantitative sensors, and comprehensive electromyography measurements to further understand the mechanisms behind tremor control, tremor progression, and side effect induction with DBS.

activity_code: R00
title: Mutant Tubulins Confer Resistance to Pathological Tau
org_name: SEATTLE INST FOR BIOMEDICAL/CLINICAL RES
phr: Project Narrative
Abnormally folded, phosphorylated and aggregated tau accumulates in several
neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's Disease. We have discovered mutations in
tubulin genes that suppress neuronal tau-induced toxicity in Caenorhabditis elegans model. The
aims within this proposal will elucidate the mechanisms underpinning mutant tubulin-mediated
suppression of tau toxicity, tau-microtubule interactions, and aid in the discovery of new
therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 11127682
activity_code: R00
title: Circuit Dynamics of Structuring Episodic Memories in Humans
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
Transforming rich and continuous experience into concise and temporally associated event sequences (i.e.,
event segmentation), a fundamental process for episodic memory, relies on the coordination between the
hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, and substantia nigra. This proposal aims to reveal the underlying circuit-level
neural dynamics using direct neural recordings (at both the single-cell and population levels), computational
modeling, and electrical stimulation. The expected outcomes of this study will provide valuable insights on event
segmentation and its influence on episodic memory in humans, which will shed light on developing neural
prosthetics to help patients with memory-related disorders.

activity_code: K08
title: Determining the role of the creatine pathway regulation of intestinal goblet cell development in colitis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Inflammatory bowel disease is a disease notable for intestinal energetic stress, such as down regulation of the machinery required to use creatine. We will evaluate the impact of creatine transporter loss on the mucous producing cells of the intestines and the impact that loss has on the bacteria and energetic factors in the intestine. This is significant because disruption of energy, bacteria and barrier function all contribute to inflammatory bowel disease activity and understanding their interactions could provide more targeted therapeutic targets.

title: How does mechanosensation contribute to choroid plexus function in health and disease?
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: Project Narrative The proposed project will investigate how choroid plexus epithelial cells might sense and respond to changes in intracranial pressure (ICP). The results of this project will deepen our understanding of how the brain is able to dynamically balance blood, brain, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) volumes to regulate ICP, which can be pathologically elevated after traumatic brain injury, stroke, hemorrhage, tumor, hydrocephalus, and during seizures. The data collected in the proposed experiments could help inform therapeutic strategies for stabilizing ICP across several neurological conditions.

title: Investigating the Genetic Determinants of Host-Specificity and Potential for Further Adaptation of Powassan Virus
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT ALBANY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Investigating host-driven substitutions and potential adaption to novel transmission cycles is essential in understanding the current spread and reemergence of the tickborne flavivirus, Powassan virus (POWV). application_id: 10928085
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Formation and Function of Subgenomic Flavivirus RNAs During Flavivirus Infection of the Mosquito Vector
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative: Arthropod borne flaviviruses, such as Dengue, produce non-coding RNAs that function to benefit these viruses during infection. Specifically, these non-coding RNAs (termed sfRNAs) have been shown to be involved in pathogenicity, interferon repression, RNAi repression, along with many other functions, indicating that they are important to understand in order to better arm ourselves with potential new therapies. This study will focus on unveiling the specific coordination observed in relevant tandem xrRNA structures located within the flavivirus 3′ UTR, as well as how these sfRNAs attenuate the mosquito RNAi immune response.

application_id: 11090469
activity_code: T32
title: Postdoctoral Training in Vision Research
org_name: SMITH-KETTLEWELL EYE RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: Public Health Relevance
The proposed postdoctoral training program in vision and rehabilitation research will have a positive and direct
impact on public health. The program is designed to train fellows to gain a deep understanding of vision and
visual impairment to develop novel models of visual function that provide insights into disease prevention,
monitoring and treatment, interventions for binocular vision disorders, rehabilitation techniques and assistive
devices. Postdoctoral fellows emerging from the program will be able to launch their own independent research
careers. Because research at Smith-Kettlewell is motivated by clinical problems, it is highly likely that our
fellows will make significant contributions to vision health during and after their training.

title: Sex Differences in T cell metabolism
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative It has long been observed that disease severity and outcomes are different in males and females, yet we don’t know why. The goal of my proposed project is to answer this question by looking at differences in T cell metabolism between males and females, and how they relate to viral infection outcomes. Understanding the biology of sex differences in T cells, metabolism, and infection will offer context to sex differences in immunity as well as new sex-specific therapies for infection.

title: Can a subset of patients with HER2+ metastatic breast cancer be cured?
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Some patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer do exceptionally well, remaining on first-line therapy
indefinitely without evidence of disease progression. Tracking minimal residual disease in the blood may help
guide treatment in patients with HER2+ MBC and allow us to explore whether some patients might be cured.

title: Exploring Crystallin Deamidation as a Causative Agent of Cataracts

application_id: 10823409
activity_code: F31
title: Redox Regulation of Host Ribosomal Proteins during Helicobacter pylori infection
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Helicobacter pylori is a gastric pathogen that colonizes the gastric mucosa of more than half of the global population and is a major risk factor for several gastric pathologies including peptic-ulcer disease and gastric adenocarcinoma. The goal of this project is to determine how reactive oxygen species, a hallmark of H. pylori infection, oxidize host ribosomal proteins and impact their functions. The results of this study will further our knowledge of how H. pylori infection modulates host cellular events and identify signaling axes that could be leveraged for the prevention and treatment of H. pylori-induced gastric pathologies.

application_id: 10863347
activity_code: F32
title: Homeostatic Maintenance of Neocortical Excitation-Inhibition Balance by Ciliary Neuropeptidergic Signaling
org_name: BRANDEIS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Signaling through neuronal primary cilia plays a crucial role during early development, but primary cilia persist into adulthood and their role in mature neurons is largely undefined. Recent research has unveiled that ciliary signaling via the neuropeptide somatostatin (SST) can modulate neocortical circuits to potentially stabilize activity by providing network-wide homeostatic feedback to modulate excitatory synaptic strengths. Here I will test this hypothesis by manipulating SST signaling and determining the impact on neocortical function and further provide valuable insights into the underlying cause of cognitive impairment in disorders arising from cilia dysfunction.

application_id: 10863630
activity_code: K08
title: Multi-scale Analysis of the Role of Skeletal Muscle Actin Mutations in Dilated Cardiomyopathy
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative The heart and skeletal muscle contract when myosin protein pulls on actin filaments and mutations in these and associated proteins can lead to dilated cardiomyopathy through different mechanisms. This study will use methods from single atoms to whole hearts to explore a unique mutation in an actin protein that normally causes skeletal muscle disease but, in this case, causes cardiomyopathy. The findings from this study will establish a new way to study actin proteins in cardiomyopathy and lay the foundation for new heart failure therapies.

application_id: 11115855
activity_code: T32
title: Training in the Molecular Basis of Autoimmunity and Autoinflammation
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In the past two decades, we have seen a paradigm shift in our understanding of the mechanisms underlying
autoimmune and autoinflammatory diseases. To continue generating key insights and developing novel
therapeutic strategies will require the next generation of scientists to have broad, multidisciplinary understanding
and training in the latest advanced technologies. The Training in the Molecular Basis of Autoimmunity and
Autoinflammation develops predoctoral investigators in these crucial areas by bringing together highly skilled
and collaborative training faculty from multiple disciplines, with the ultimate goal of developing effective
treatments, and eventually cures, for these debilitating diseases.

activity_code: T32
title: Research Training in Experimental Immunology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Immunology is the study of the body’s immune system and how it responds in states of health and disease. Many human conditions involve alterations of the normal function of the immune system including cancer, autoimmune diseases, infectious diseases, and transplantation. The purpose of this training grant is to instruct the next generation of scientists in the critical thinking and technical skills necessary to provide mechanistic insights into how the immune system functions, and how to manipulate the host immune response to the benefit of human health.

title: TRIM 14 is a master regulator of STAT3 activity during the macrophage innate immune responses to Mycobacterium tuberculosis

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), the causative agent of tuberculosis, successfully evades host defenses to make it one of most infectious pathogens in the world.
