application_id: 10863125
activity_code: K99
title: The molecular determinants of atrial fibrillation
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common serious arrhythmia in human populations, but current treatment strategies only function to reduce symptoms, instead of treating underlying causes. The studies outlined by this proposal will expand our understanding of the genes required by atrial muscle cells to maintain heart rhythm, and how the expression of these genes is maintained at the appropriate level. We will examine a set of genes implicated in human AF and in two mouse models for their requirement to maintain heart rhythm and for their effectiveness as gene therapies to prevent AF and reverse established disease.

application_id: 10998526
activity_code: F31
title: How ribosomal silencing promotes chronic infection of the gastric pathogen Helicobacter pylori
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA CRUZ
phr: Project Narrative Helicobacter pylori is a globally prevalent pathogen that contributes to the burden of disease by maintaining life-long chronic infections that lead to severe gastrointestinal diseases and gastric cancers. Currently recommended therapies are approximately 75-80% effective, which has led both the World Health Organization and the NIH to categorize H. pylori as a high research priority pathogen. This proposal aims to elucidate growth mechanisms that facilitate disease causing H. pylori infections by characterizing the role of RsfS and ribosome silencing in chronic H. pylori infections.

title: The Development of Youth Resilience to Neighborhood Disadvantage
org_name: MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Neighborhood disadvantage (e.g., poverty and exposure to violence) is a robust indicator of youth adjustment difficulties across most domains of development, yet there are youth who thrive despite significant neighborhood adversity. This project investigates: (1) how resilience changes as youth reach important developmental milestones and freely explore their environments, (2) whether these developmental changes differ by resilience domain (psychiatric, social, emotional, academic), and (3) how warm and involved parenting as well as neighborhood protective factors might shape their development. In doing so, the proposed project can identify specific and modifiable protective factors that promote resilience to chronic neighborhood risk.

application_id: 11116875
activity_code: T32
title: Building an interprofessional and diverse workforce in substance use and pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Our proposed training program, “BUilding an InterprofessionaL and Diverse workforce in
substance Use and Pain” (BUILD-UP), will train the next generation of researchers at the
intersection of substance use and chronic pain, a cutting-edge field with few current training
options. BUILD-UP will prioritize recruitment of postdoctoral fellows from diverse groups
including individuals who identify as being from a group excluded based on ethnicity and race,
being from a disadvantaged background, having a disability, or being LGBT+, and will reduce
barriers and promote equity in participation by allowing fully remote training. Therefore, BUILD-
UP will have the important public health impact of both expanding and diversifying the workforce
in this critical area.

application_id: 11369769
activity_code: F31
title: How ribosomal silencing promotes chronic infection of the gastric pathogen Helicobacter pylori
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA CRUZ
phr: Project Narrative
Helicobacter pylori is a globally prevalent pathogen that contributes to the burden of disease by maintaining
life-long chronic infections that lead to severe gastrointestinal diseases and gastric cancers. Currently
recommended therapies are approximately 75-80% effective, which has led both the World Health
Organization and the NIH to categorize H. pylori as a high research priority pathogen. This proposal aims to
elucidate growth mechanisms that facilitate disease causing H. pylori infections by characterizing the role of
RsfS and ribosome silencing in chronic H. pylori infections.

title: Host-pathogen interactions during GBS diabetic wound infections
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Group B Streptococcus (GBS) is mainly associated with neonatal meningitis but is also the third leading cause of death from skin and subcutaneous infection. In fact, GBS is increasingly associated with diabetes as a common colonizer of diabetic wounds, but little is known about GBS factors that allow colonization and persistence in the highly inflammatory wound environment. This proposal seeks to understand how GBS metal homeostasis and methylglyoxal detoxification contributes to GBS survival during diabetic wound infection.

title: Defining the impact of Extracellular Vesicles on inflammation during pneumonic plague

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Yersinia pestis, the etiologic agent of the plague, has refined mechanisms to manipulate the host immune response and suppress inflammation. title: Structure-based Antiviral Design against HTLV-1 Protease
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Project Narrative Human T-cell leukemia virus type-1 (HTLV-1) is an oncogenic human retrovirus which has infected over 20 million people worldwide but lacks any vaccines or direct-acting antivirals. Vibrio cholerae is a pervasive human enteropathogen and the etiologic agent of cholera, a severe human

microbial community of the gastrointestinal tract, the gut microbiota, contributes to differences in host immune
responses to oral cholera vaccination, provide high-resolution insight into host microbiota-immune interactions,
and identify novel microbial candidates to be used in the precise and rational targeting of the gut microbiota,

org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Timely management of dementia remains a challenge for primary care providers despite their central role in
the care of patients with dementia. The goal of this proposal is to adapt and implement a practical intervention
to facilitate high quality, comprehensive post-diagnosis care for dementia in traditional primary care settings.

and clinician-centered strategies to improve the care of older adults with dementia.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA RIVERSIDE
phr: PROJCT NARRATIVE Vibrio cholerae is a pervasive human enteropathogen and the etiologic agent of cholera, a severe human diarrheal disease that results in 100,000 deaths worldwide per year. Although several oral cholera vaccines have been developed for prophylactic treatment, they demonstrate variable efficacy as a function of human population. The proposed studies will examine how interindividual differences in the composition of the microbial community of the gastrointestinal tract, the gut microbiota, contributes to differences in host immune responses to oral cholera vaccination, provide high-resolution insight into host microbiota-immune interactions, and identify novel microbial candidates to be used in the precise and rational targeting of the gut microbiota, specifically in compleme...

activity_code: T32
title: Advanced Training in Oral Biology
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Advanced Training in Oral Biology program at the School of Dental Medicine at the University at Buffalo
offers a wide range of research-intensive interdisciplinary graduate and post-doctoral programs to train the
next generation of oral health biomedical scientists. The goal of the program is to attract pre-doctoral and post-
doctoral trainees into oral biology research careers. This proposal requests continuation of support for both our
Ph.D. program to train dentists (post-D.D.S.) and non-dentists (pre-doctoral), and for our post-doctoral program to
train post-doctoral fellows.

application_id: 11252309
activity_code: F31
title: The role of antigen-independent T cell activation in controlling M. tuberculosis infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Our lack of understanding of the mechanisms of T cell mediated protection against Tuberculosis, a disease
responsible for 1.6 million deaths in 2021, has hampered our ability to develop new vaccine strategies. Antigen-
independent T cell activation, termed bystander activation, has shown protective effects in response to M.tb
infection but has not been fully explored or characterized. Here, we will comprehensively analyze the role of
bystander activation in two T cell subsets in the context of M.tb infection to extensively explore this protective
pathway as a possible vaccine target.

title: Craniofacial Oral-biology Student Training in Academic Research (COSTAR)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Oral diseases remain highly prevalent and greatly affect the health of the US population. application_id: 10863957
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular basis for substrate discrimination by transporter protein MexY of the MexXY-OprM efflux pump in Pseudomonas aeruginosa
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE The ability of bacteria to actively export antibiotics out of their cells through efflux pumps is a prominent mechanism contributing to the escalating global antibiotic resistance health crisis. My research will investigate how specific efflux systems belonging to a family of pumps found in many pathogenic bacteria accomplish the essential processes of antibiotic uptake and recognition for export out of the cell. In addition to providing me the interdisciplinary training necessary to become an independent researcher, my results will enhance our understanding of efflux pump activity/ function and could support development of new pump inhibitors or guide redesign of current therapeutics to reduce their export while retaining antibiotic efficacy.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA CRUZ

New technologies have identified translation from previously overlooked short open reading frames.

title: Early Post-Stroke Biomarkers for Motor Rehabilitation Outcomes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative Stroke is a common cause of adult disability, and many stroke survivors suffer from chronically impaired arm function. This proposal seeks to improve future post-stroke rehabilitation therapies by identifying early biomarkers post-stroke that can be used to predict motor control impairments in the chronic stage. This information will then be used to develop and test patient-individualized rehabilitation therapies and may lead to better recovery of motor function after stroke.

activity_code: F31
title: Within and between host dynamics in influenza A virus antigenic evolution
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The rapid evolution of seasonal influenza A viruses results in new variants that require our vaccines to be
updated each year. There is currently a lack of understanding of how these variants arise amongst individual
hosts to then go onto circulate globally. This research aims to address this gap with the goal of improving our
ability to anticipate the evolution of influenza A viruses when designing seasonal vaccines.

title: Cardiovascular Surgery Research Training

Cardiovascular diseases are the major causes of morbidity and mortality in the United States. This

application_id: 10813878
activity_code: K23
title: Identifying Novel Diagnostics and Mechanisms for Cardiac Allograft Vasculopathy
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiac allograft vasculopathy is a pan-arterial disease that impacts 50% of patients within ten years of heart
transplantation and is the cause of death for up to 1/3rd of patients. Currently our understanding of the
mechanism of CAV and the standard modalities to diagnose CAV are insufficient. Achieving our aims will set
the stage for a change in the diagnostic paradigm for cardiac allograft vasculopathy and advance our
understanding of the pathophysiologic mechanism for the disease that impacts the majority of heart transplant
recipients.

application_id: 10909850
activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing pulsatility during cardiopulmonary bypass to reduce acute kidney injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiac surgery is effective for improving the health of patients with cardiovascular disease, but many patients
experience complications such as acute kidney injury (AKI) that result in an increased risk of death after surgery.
Maintaining normal pulsatile blood flow with the heart lung machine during cardiac surgery may prevent AKI after
cardiac surgery even though increased mechanical pulsatility damages red blood cells. This trial will identify to
best level of pulsatility during cardiac surgery for minimizing red blood cell damage while preventing AKI.

application_id: 10819218
activity_code: K23
title: Reducing Reintubation Risk in High-Risk Cardiac Surgery Patients with High-Flow Nasal Cannula -the "I-CAN" study
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hundreds of thousands of adult patients undergo cardiac surgery every year in the United States, each of
whom has a significant risk of reintubation after surgery. A better understanding of interventions that can
effectively decrease the reintubation rate could help decrease short- and long-term mortality, cost, and hospital
length of stay. Therefore, we propose the “Reducing ReIntubation Risk in High-Risk Cardiac Surgery Patients
with High-Flow Nasal Cannula” (I-CAN) Study, to assess whether this simple, relatively non-invasive technique
can help decrease postoperative reintubation rates.

title: A Plan to Reduce Non-Communicable Disease-Related Morbidity and Mortality After Natural Disasters
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Hurricanes Irma and Maria in 2017 killed over 1000 people in the U.S. territories of Puerto Rico and the US
Virgin Islands. One third of deaths after natural disasters have been attributed poorly managed chronic
diseases such as diabetes and heart disease. This proposal will develop an emergency preparedness plan to
reduce morbidity and mortality due to diabetes, hypertension, and heart failure after natural disasters in the
U.S Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico.

application_id: 10925178
activity_code: K23
title: Chronic Pain Diagnosis and Treatment in Torture Survivors
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
27 million people worldwide are estimated to suffer chronic somatic pain as a result of systematic torture,
which is likely undetected by evaluators due to confounding psychiatric illness and a lack of diagnostic tools.
This gap in the detection of pain results in exclusive psychological treatment rather than multimodal therapies,
substantially limiting rehabilitation, placing vulnerable individuals at higher risk of drug abuse, and increasing
healthcare expenditures. The objectives of this study are to improve the diagnosis of pain in torture survivors
(SA 1), to qualitatively characterize acceptability of somatic pain treatment (SA 2), and to assess the feasibility
of recruiting and retaining subjects in a subsequent clinical trial (SA 3).

application_id: 10821452
activity_code: K23
title: Toward optimizing digital mental health interventions: A clinical trial aimed at understanding what drives patient engagement.
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mobile app mental health interventions are accessible and effective when used at the recommended dose; but
when implemented in routine care, patients do not stay engaged long enough to see clinical benefits. The
proposed research evaluates what drives patient engagement and tests the impact of two strategies—
automated motivational push messaging and coach support—to improve engagement with an evidence-based
mobile app intervention for depression and/or anxiety. Findings will lay the foundation for a program of
research focused on optimizing mobile app interventions for engagement and effectiveness.

title: Social Media Use and Mechanisms of Suicide Risk in Adolescents
org_name: RHODE ISLAND HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Nearly all adolescents use social media sites, yet for a vulnerable subset of teens, social media use may
increase risk for suicide. Given rising suicide rates among adolescent girls in recent years, it is critical to
examine the reasons why social media use may be related to suicidal thoughts and attempts. Findings of this
study will have the potential to inform future suicide intervention and prevention efforts that address the unique
needs of adolescents in the digital age.

title: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy: Pathways to Future Cardiovascular Disease
org_name: THE CHRIST HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite the substantial cardiovascular risk burden associated with the development pregnancy related-
hypertensive disorders, surprisingly little is known regarding the mechanisms that drive the development of
these hypertensive disorders of pregnancy in some women or the subsequent cardiovascular disease (CVD)
outcomes in these vulnerable women. We hypothesize that: (i) the stress of pregnancy augments pre-existing
metabolic and inflammatory derangements in certain women, making them more susceptible to hypertensive
disorders of pregnancy; (ii) in this setting, affected women are prone to developing coronary microvascular
dysfunction (CMD), a previously unrecognized driver of CVD risk; and, (iii) women affected by a hypertensive
disorder of pregnancy, especially in the s...

application_id: 10863971
activity_code: F30
title: Sex differences and metabolic responses to chronic stress
org_name: COLORADO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Individuals suffering from metabolic dysfunction are at an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and depression, all of which correlate with increased mortality and years lived with disability and, importantly, are influenced by factors such as chronic stress. This proposal will determine whether activation of a specific frontal cortex to brainstem circuit mitigates the effects of chronic stress on glucose homeostasis and Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal axis activation. Determining the neurocircuitry that regulates blood glucose during prolonged stress is vital to advance our understanding and, potentially, address the increasingly prevalent stress-related metabolic disorders that burden our society.

Development Award is to obtain further training and initiate a mentored research project to establish a

application_id: 11052490
activity_code: K01
title: Proteomic and epigenetic alterations associated with plant-based diets and CVD
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative
Plant-based diets are healthy dietary patterns that are consistently associated with a lower risk of
cardiovascular disease (CVD) and CVD risk factors. The proposed research seeks to uncover the mechanisms
underlying plant-based diets and CVD by comprehensively characterizing the proteome and methylome, and
integrating multiple omics data from 3 large prospective cohorts. The proposed research will yield novel
evidence on protein markers and epigenetic markers of plant-based diets, and pathways linking plant-based
diets to CVD, thus providing a basis to formulate CVD prevention and treatment strategies.

activity_code: R25
title: Training in Cardiovascular Sciences for Under Represented Students
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The CURS program addresses the lack of diversity in biomedical research, which is rooted in the limited number of under-represented students entering the field of science, ultimately affecting the goals and direction of research. Underserved communities suffer from a lack of representation at the decision-making level in science, in terms of policy issues and the determination of priorities for conducting research. Therefore, the CURS program addresses the critical need to increase the pipeline of highly qualified trainees into cardiovascular science.

application_id: 10863899
activity_code: T32
title: Mechanisms of Trauma-Induced Coagulopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative The fundamental objective of our Trauma Research Center Training Program is to inspire the next generation of surgical trainees to pursue a career that includes basic science and translational investigations. Our secondary objective is to recruit capable surgeons as surgical investigators to the field of academic trauma surgery, in which lack of research has become a national crisis. As trauma continues to be the leading cause of death for individuals younger than 45 years of age, and the most common cause of preventable death is uncontrolled bleeding, training the next generation of clinician investigators in this field is our fundamental goal.

application_id: 10928821
activity_code: R42
title: An Interactive Education Program to Reduce High Risk Behavior in Adolescents
org_name: KLEIN BUENDEL, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research will fill a gap by completing the adaptation of iTRAC, a web-based emotion regulation
sexual health intervention, and testing its efficacy in promoting safer sexual cognitions and delaying sexual
initiation among early adolescents. This application addresses the Office of Disease Prevention and Health
Promotion's Healthy People 2030 indicator FP-08, to increase the proportion of adolescents who get formal
sex education before age 18 years (currently less than 53%). It also uses a novel approach, emotion regulation
skills training, to address indicator FP-04, to increase the proportion of adolescents who have never had sexual
intercourse, and indicator FP-06, to increase the proportion of adolescents who used condoms at last sexual
intercourse.

title: Oral and Craniofacial Sciences Health Research Training
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The UCSF Oral and Craniofacial Sciences training program educates exceptional students to apply scientific
approaches to solve the most challenging problems in dental, oral and craniofacial health. The goal of this
training grant is to provide the financial resources for education and training of DDS/PhD, PhD and
postdoctoral fellows to become influential leaders in academic and industrial biomedical research, and other
fields related to dental, oral and craniofacial science.

application_id: 11044367
activity_code: K23
title: Vitamin D Genetics and Racial Differences in Pediatric Chronic KidneyDisease-Mineral and Bone Disorder
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pediatric Chronic Kidney Disease-Mineral Bone Disorder is a comorbidity of CKD that impacts the skeletal and
cardiovascular health of pediatric patients. Unfortunately, despite the importance of this entity, our current
treatment guidelines do not enable individualized therapy despite evidence that a one-size fits all approach is
inadequate. This proposal seeks to identify genetic variants that predict skeletal behavior in pediatric CKD-
MBD and thereby allow the individualization of therapy.

application_id: 10864668
activity_code: K99
title: Dissection of specialized metabolism in plants using multiplexed perturbation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Plants are the origin of many FDA-approved drugs and, as food, deliver a daily dose of drug-like compounds into our bodies. This project establishes an efficient strategy to decode the genomes of dietary and medicinal plants by activating and identifying hundreds of different biochemical programs in multiplexed assays. This work will help illuminate what molecules a plant can synthesize, allowing us to (1) make informed dietary decisions, (2) engineer the metabolic content of our food, and (3) produce high-value medicinal compounds.

application_id: 10890147
activity_code: K99
title: Double-stranded RNA dictates SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid condensation temperature
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: I determined how SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid (N) protein recognizes viral RNA to undergo condensation in an RNA sequence, RNA structure, and temperature dependent manner. I will uncover new modes of condensation function by identifying how RNA-RNA interactions are modulated by condensation as well as identifying which N role(s) in viral replication require condensation. Lastly, I will design and test novel inhibitors of N condensation including synthetic RNA-binding proteins and small molecules with the goal of eventually developing treatments for SARS-CoV-2.

application_id: 10864956
activity_code: T32
title: Neurobehavior, Neuropathology, and Risk Factors in Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Alzheimer's disease (AD) and related dementias affect a growing number of people as the population ages. Currently, nearly 6 million persons in the US have AD; it is estimated that by the year 2050, that number will be 14 million. With this increase in the number of AD patients, there is a corresponding growth in the number of family members and other caregivers affected by this devastating disease, as well as dramatic social and economic costs. The goal of our research training program is to prepare a new generation of investigators to translate mechanistic scientific discoveries into effective tools for diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of AD and related dementias.

org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The importance of myelination is highlighted by the devastating neurological symptoms that accompany diseases such as leukodystrophies and multiple sclerosis. Successful completion of this proposal will help uncover key molecular mechanisms that drive CNS myelination, potentially leading to new therapeutic targets.

title: Understanding the role of community determinants in opioid use disorder and program implementation factors influencing patient adherence to opioid agonist therapy
org_name: GEISINGER CLINIC
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The proposed research will elucidate how community contextual factors related to economic, social, and physical conditions work in conjunction with individual-level genetic risk factors, healthcare factors, and comorbid medical conditions in generating or mitigating risk for opioid use disorder. It will also identify program implementation factors at the individual, interpersonal, and organizational levels that influence adherence to medication-assisted treatment among patients with opioid use disorder. The research results will inform investment in preventive interventions where community conditions may increase risk for opioid use disorder as well as how health systems can increase retention in treatment programs for opioid use disorder.

title: Defining the role of perineuronal nets in Alzheimer's Disease pathology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder resulting in dementia primarily in the elderly populations. The age-dependent increasing plaque load in AD has been correlated with an exacerbated loss of extracellular matrix structures called perineuronal nets (PNNs). Thus, it is imperative we determine the consequences of PNN loss in AD to gain deeper insight into pathology progression as well as the therapeutic potential PNNs may hold.

activity_code: K23
title: Cytomegalovirus-specific Immune Reconstitution after Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation in the Era of Modern Antiviral Prophylaxis
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection causes an increased risk of complications and death after hematopoietic cell transplantation. Recent studies suggest that new medications for the prevention of graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) after transplant may promote the development of immunity against CMV; however, it is unclear whether these new GVHD preventative drugs can promote CMV immunity in the era of modern CMV prevention. This proposal will seek to answer this question, using cutting edge technology and analytical methods, in order to inform management of CMV in the context of new and future cancer treatments.

application_id: 11129761
activity_code: F31
title: Characterization of JT-4-173, a Potent Antiviral that Inhibits HIV-1 by a Novel Mechanism of Action
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Tens of millions of individuals living with HIV-1 are taking highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART), a long-
term and life-saving treatment comprising the use of multiple antivirals that prevent HIV-1 infection by targeting
essential enzymes involved in the viral replication cycle. Our lab previously reported a potent non-nucleoside
reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI), JT-4-173, that binds at the typical NNRTI-binding site but inhibits the
virus through a previously undescribed process that is unique to this compound. Here, I aim to characterize the
novel mechanism of action of JT-4-173, which can both further our understanding of HIV-1 reverse transcriptase
biology and aid in the development of future generations of HAART.

application_id: 10747343
activity_code: F32
title: NRSA application: Characterizing acetylcholine, noradrenaline, and dopamine diffusion through the extracellular space in three subregions of macaque neocortex
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Understanding how neuromodulators diffuse through the extracellular space (ECS) from release site to target receptor is a crucial component in understanding neuromodulator signaling. Prior computational work has studied diffusion dynamics over spatiotemporal scales that are too small for neuromodulation and lack the capabilities to incorporate factors that only become relevant at larger distances. Thus, this proposal seeks to combine small-scale mechanisms modeled by MCell with algorithms of large-scale mechanisms such as bulk diffusion to evaluate how factors like granularity and anisotropy shape diffusion of acetylcholine, noradrenaline, and dopamine through structurally different regions of macaque cortex.

application_id: 10747354
activity_code: K22
title: Identification of therapeutic vulnerabilities in prostate cancer bone metastasis
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative  Metastatic prostate cancer is the second-leading cause of cancer-related deaths in men, ranks third in national cancer expenditures and represents significant morbidity to patients due to bone metastasis and side effects of treatment. This proposal will perform preclinical studies to identify previously unrecognized therapeutic vulnerabilities of prostate cancer bone metastasis and treatment-resistant tumors. These studies may ultimately result in novel treatments that can potentially improve survival and reduce morbidity of this lethal disease.

activity_code: F32
title: Neural sensitivity to social feedback following adversity: Implications for adolescent mental health
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although theories of adversity suggest that childhood threat exposure sensitizes neural and emotional response systems in ways that both increase risk for psychopathology and promote adaptation, most research has focused on negative health outcomes (i.e., psychopathology) among adversity-exposed youth. This study will examine how threat exposure predicts neural sensitivity to negative and positive social-emotional information and how this sensitivity interacts with peer experiences to shape changes in negative (i.e., depression) and positive (i.e., social-emotional wellbeing) health outcomes. Results will improve knowledge of the ways adolescents leverage adversity-linked sensitivity to social-emotional cues in order to benefit from environmental support and will identify...

application_id: 10924714
activity_code: F31
title: Development of soluble and membrane bound immunogens to shepherd HIV-1 MPER specific BCR maturation
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: Project Narrative. The induction of broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) by vaccination is a global health priority as a means to prevent HIV infection, and the membrane proximal external region (MPER) of the HIV-1 envelope protein is a site of particular interest. Vaccine induction of bnAbs will likely require sequential immunization with additional immunogens designed to shepherd affinity maturation of the B cell receptor (BCR) to acquire neutralization breadth and potency. This project seeks to develop soluble and membrane-bound boost immunogens that present the MPER epitope in a more native-like context with the aim of maturing primed BCRs to acquire bnAb features.

application_id: 11258893
activity_code: F31
title: Cis-regulatory mechanisms governing RORgt expression in type 3 lymphocytes
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Type 3 lymphocytes—including group 3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3),
IL-17-producing γδ T cells (Tγδ17), and
T
helper 17
(
TH17) cells—are crucial for a range of functions from mucosal barrier defenses against extracellular
pathogens to secondary lymphoid organ formation. Their dysregulated function has also been implicated in
various autoimmune diseases, such as multiple sclerosis and Crohn's disease. The proposed study will further
our understanding of the regulatory mechanisms governing the development and differentiation of type 3
lymphocytes.

title: Characterizing a novel Plasmodium cytoskeletal protein
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The malaria parasite exercises morphological changes to complete its life cycle. We identified a protein which associates with the parasite cytoskeleton in two life stages and is hypothesized to be essential for parasite replication. We aim to characterize its functional role in the blood stages of malaria infection and map its interactions to explore the parasite cytoskeleton and inform the rational design of antimalarial drugs.

application_id: 10747394
activity_code: F31
title: Structural analysis of protein-protein and protein-lipid interactions of lens membrane proteins.
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative: Cataract is the primary cause of blindness in the world and, with surgery as the most common treatment, it costs the US healthcare system billions of dollars each year. Major lens membrane proteins, AQP0 and Lim2 are essential for lens transparency and an understanding of the roles that these proteins play in cataract formation will allow therapies to be developed to prevent, treat or delay its onset. This proposal focuses on structural analysis of these two lens membrane proteins using advanced mass spectrometry techniques with the goal of elucidating the role that protein-protein and protein-lipid interactions play in cataract development.

application_id: 10822500
activity_code: F30
title: Genetic resiliency to disease progression in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A subset of individuals exhibits resilience to Alzheimer’s disease, as indicated by significant Alzheimer’s disease neuropathology on autopsy despite intact cognition. Underlying pathways that engage this resilience remain largely unknown, making it difficult to develop targeted therapies that harness these naturally-occurring pathways. My goal is to investigate genetic mechanisms of natural resilience, so that we can design strategies that leverage these underlying mechanisms to therapeutically confer resilience to Alzheimer’s disease progression.

activity_code: F30
title: Delayed wound healing in diabetic corneal epithelia: reduction in protein response after injury and uncoordinated cell-cell communication
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Injury to the cornea can be painful and compromise vision, especially in people with diabetes. For a cornea to heal cells need to talk to one another using signals from proteins that act like sensors, a process that does not work well in diabetic corneas. The goal of this proposal is to look at the interactions between two important sensing proteins to see why cells have a hard time talking to each other in diabetes.

title: Targeting breathing limitations to improve functional outcomes in HFpEF
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
More than 80% of patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) are obese and obesity is
considered a primary comorbidity in HFpEF, yet, the role of obesity in provoking symptoms of dyspnea on exertion
(DOE) and exercise intolerance in HFpEF patients has, thus far, been neglected. Obesity-related mechanical
ventilatory constraints ultimately 1) increase the oxygen (O2) cost of breathing and 2) impose a mechanical ceiling
on ventilation (V̇E) during exercise, which could provoke DOE and reduce exercise capacity. The overall aim of
this K99/R00 proposal is to 1) investigate the O2 cost of breathing and examine how this impacts DOE and peak
exercise capacity in patients with HFpEF, and 2) reduce obesity-related mechanical ventilatory constraints to
pote...

application_id: 10973693
activity_code: K00
title: Epilepsy Mechanisms and the Path to Intervention
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research will further our understanding of SLC13A5-associated epilepsies by establishing and
characterizing mouse models for the disease. These studies will provide evidence supporting the complexity of
SLC13A5 disease mechanisms as well as guiding the planned gene therapy. Using our novel animal models
and recombinant proteins, our multidisciplinary approach will determine seizure properties, neuronal properties,
metabolic markers, histological changes to characterize phenotype-genotype relationships and molecular
mechanisms underlying SLC13A5 disease.

application_id: 11361465
activity_code: R00
title: RBPMS, a novel RNA splicing regulator of cardiac function and disease
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: Project Narrative
RNA posttranscriptional modifications emerged as a key regulatory component of cardiac gene expression and
function, influencing various cardiovascular diseases. RBPMS is a novel regulator of RNA posttranscriptional
modification in the heart, modulating heart function and disease progression. Through the study of RBPMS, the
proposal aims to gain insights into the fundamental mechanisms whereby RNA binding proteins regulate cardiac
function and develop new potential therapeutics for cardiovascular disease.

application_id: 10832971
activity_code: R01
title: MRWeight: Medical Residents Learning Weight Management Counseling Skills -- A Multi-Modal, Technology-Assisted, Spaced Education Program
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: 8. Project Narrative Overweight and obesity affect a large majority of Americans, increasing their risk for multiple chronic illnesses. It is recommended that physicians provide weight management counseling (WMC) to patients having overweight or obesity, but physicians have limited WMC training. Residency is an ideal time to teach physicians WMC skills, but residency programs lack an evidence-based curriculum to support this need. Our study addresses this gap by testing MRWeight, an innovative technology-assisted spaced education program designed specifically for teaching WMC to medical residents.

application_id: 11291792
activity_code: DP1
title: Harnessing Highly Networked HLA-E-Restricted CTL Epitopes to Achieve a Broadly Effective HIV Cure
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The high level of HIV variability and the polymorphic nature of MHC-Ia alleles are major challenges to the
development of a broadly effective CTL-based vaccine. This proposal aims to overcome these obstacles by
leveraging the conserved MHC-1b allele HLA-E and mutation-resistant HLA-E-restricted CTL epitopes in a
rationally designed, therapeutic CTL-based vaccine for HIV. Thus, the proposed work is relevant to the NIH’s
mission to seek and apply knowledge to lengthen life and reduce illness.

application_id: 10992383
activity_code: R01
title: Empowering Latinas to Obtain Guideline-Concordant Breast Cancer Screenings
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Latinas suffer disproportionately from breast cancer, relative to non-Latina Whites, including late-stage diagnoses and worse mental health during diagnostic evaluation, treatment, and survivorship. This Diversity Supplement aims to extend the Parent Grant (R01CA261638), by providing insight into factors that contribute to Latinas' decisions to seek and provide social support after receiving abnormal mammogram results through a prospective qualitative study. Further, the Diversity Supplement will train a bicultural, bilingual Latina trainee seeking a Master's of Social Work degree in health communications and qualitative research methods, which will enhance the diversity and research impact of the nation's workforce of emerging clinician scientists.

title: Deciphering the Role of Noradrenergic Receptors during Neuromodulation in Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a degenerative brain disorder imposing an increasing societal health burden and currently presents no effective options for clinical treatment or prevention. application_id: 10928120
activity_code: R01
title: Impact of a Novel Community-Based Biobehavioral Chronic Pain Team Training Program (4PCP) on Practitioner and Patient Outcomes
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Drugs may help the sensory part of chronic pain and the anxiety and depression that follow, but they do not address its core emotional, cognitive and behavioral roots, which require a biobehavioral approach, typically foreign to most primary care practitioners (PCPs). Our prior work showed that PCPs skillfully appropriate and implement this approach with 4PCP, a training program for PCP's to manage chronic pain effectively, resulting in greater work satisfaction and improved patient outcomes. Here we propose to train 27 practices in 4 cities using an intensive, codified and dissemination-ready version of the training and measure whether over the ensuing 2 years: (1) patients have less pain and (2) PCP's are happier with their work, and (3) fewer opioids are prescribed.

application_id: 11000719
activity_code: RF1
title: Diversity Supplement for a STEM diversity trainee in the lab
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA CRUZ
phr: Project Narrative
Understanding the neural circuit organization at great precision is a prerequisite to understand
the brain functions underlying complex animal behaviors. In this proposed research, we will
develop and validate two novel trans-synaptic virus tracer systems to delineate the neural
connectivity orthogonally and rapidly. Our proposal has high levels of public health relevance
because utilizing our methods to study brain connectivity will enhance understanding of neural
circuit organization in both normal and diseased brains, which aligns with NIH’s mission to identify
the circuit-specific therapeutic targets to cure brain disorders.

application_id: 11128813
activity_code: R00
title: Capturing the Holistic Glycocode through Systems Glycobiology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Molecular interactions at the cell surface are paramount to many biological processes, yet our knowledge of the
cell surface architectures that dictate these interactions remains incomplete. This proposal introduces novel
technologies to capture glycoprotein features across the cell surface proteome, where glycan and protein
components contribute holistically to unique molecular surfaces that relay biological information (i.e., the
glycocode). Using these developments, we will generate a human glycocode atlas across multiple cell types to
explore the role of cell surface glycocode heterogeneity in specialized cellular functions, and we will study
dynamic glycocode reprogramming as cells transition from sedentary to migratory phenotypes known to drive
numerous pathologies.

activity_code: K00
title: SIRT1 Regulates RNA Stability to Promote Breast Cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative Highly aggressive forms of breast cancer cells produce and release exosomes, a major class of extracellular vesicles (EVs), as a mechanism for intercellular communication that promotes cell growth, therapy resistance, as well as invasive activity and metastasis. I have recently discovered a novel mechanism by which the downregulation of the NAD-dependent deacetylase Sirtuin 1 (SIRT1) in aggressive breast cancer cells regulates RNA stability, leading to the increased production of exosomes enriched in proteins that strongly promote cancer cell invasion. I plan to further examine the broader consequences of this mechanism, as well as investigate the importance of EVs in mediating the interplay between aging and cancer progression, with the goal of identifying novel therapeu...

title: Sickle Pan-African Research Consortium (SPARCO)- Tanzania
org_name: MUHIMBILI UNIVERSITY/ ALLIED HLTH SCIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
SPARCO-Tanzania will contribute data on sickle cell disease (SCD) in Tanzania into the SCD in Sub-Saharan
Africa Network database, advance SCD standards of care, enhance the skills in clinical care and research on
SCD and generate data on the current status of SCD and effectiveness of interventions that will inform
policy change. These activities will lead to an increased understanding of the disease in Africa and improve the
effectiveness of care and treatment programs, leading to the reduction in the morbidity and mortality due to
SCD in Sub-Saharan Africa.

title: Self-Assembling Spike-EBR Nanoparticles as a Vaccine Platform Technology Against SARS-CoV-2 and Future Pandemic Coronaviruses
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: Project Narrative
The COVID-19 pandemic represents the 3rd outbreak caused by zoonotic transmission of a beta-coronavirus
(beta-CoV) in the last 20 years, and new vaccine strategies are urgently needed to ensure rapid global
distribution, lasting protection against variants of concern (VOCs), and prevention of future CoV pandemics.
The proposed research is based on the EBR nanoparticle (NP) technology that modifies membrane proteins
such as the SARS-CoV-2 spike (S) protein to self-assemble into virus-resembling NPs that bud from the cell
surface. The EBR technology will be applied to accomplish three goals: i) Design a hybrid mRNA vaccine that
combines attributes of mRNA and protein NP vaccines by encoding the self-assembling S-EBR NP construct
to enhance vaccine potency against SARS-CoV-2...

application_id: 11015947
activity_code: DP2
title: AGING AS A SELECTIVE PRESSURE THAT DRIVES TUMOR PROGRESSION
org_name: H. LEE MOFFITT CANCER CTR & RES INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Globally, the population aged 65 and above is growing faster than all other age groups posing a significant
problem to the healthcare system, the exponential increase of many age-related diseases; one such disease is
cancer, whose risk of incidence increases 11-fold as one gets old. Despite this, and contrary to other age-related
diseases such as Alzheimer’s or diabetes, cancer research currently has virtually no framework that factors in
the aging process. Here, I propose a new conceptual framework to precisely map how the aging process imparts
into cancer progression and to identify novel therapeutic strategies for older cancer patients that factors in the
reprogramming that occur with aging.

application_id: 11186384
activity_code: R43
title: Impedance-based bone health characterization for predictive osseointegration and patient-specific treatment
org_name: RYTEK MEDICAL, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Comprehensive assessment of patient-specific bone quality is critically needed to improve dental implant
treatment planning and ensure predictable osseointegration for long-term success in a rapidly aging population
of increasing bone quality variability. We propose to develop a novel impedance-based bone assessment
technology capable of interrogating trabecular bone microarchitecture in order to inform patient-specific
treatment planning, implant selection, use of adjunctive procedures, and postoperative protocols. An objective
metric able to interrogate the mechanical properties at the osteotomy site could be transformative for improving
osseointegration predictability, ultimately maximizing outcomes while expanding treatment accessibility for aging
patients.

application_id: 11073083
activity_code: R00
title: Measuring treatment response and residual disease in leukemia with personalized, sensitive, and quantitative genomic methods
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Better methods are urgently needed to measure therapeutic response and detect early relapse, especially in
acute myeloid leukemia (AML), where 50 percent of patients that have a complete response to initial treatment
go on to relapse within 2 years. Current methods for measuring minimal residual disease in AML are not sensitive
enough, do not apply to all patients, and do not fully capture the genetic heterogeneity of the disease. In this
project, we propose that utilizing personalized genetic mutations associated with a patient’s leukemia will result
in more sensitive, quantitative, and general measurements of minimal residual disease, allowing for better
evaluation of treatment efficacy and earlier detection of relapse, ultimately improving patient outcome in AML.

application_id: 11159629
activity_code: R00
title: Improving Novice Driver Roadway Hazard Identification Through a Parent-Focused Intervention
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative
Novice drivers share a disproportionate amount of crash risk compared to experienced drivers, which is
thought to be largely driven by teens’ inexperience in identifying potential hazards on the roadway. Parents
serve as the primary instructor of teens’ driving under the learner phase of licensure, yet existing literature
indicates that parents do not regularly engage in instruction on how to anticipate and respond to potential
roadway hazards. This research will address priorities set forth by NICHD to access and prevent traumatic
injuries by improving parental instruction of teens’ ability to anticipate hazards on the roadway via a parent-
focused teen-driving intervention.

particle size, source, and composition exposure variations, and will also evaluate the
potentially modifying effects of diet and health oppo...

Deprescribing is an essential component of safe and effective health care for older adults. This proposal seeks
to fund the next phase of the US Deprescribing Research Network, a national research network that aims to
expand the quality, quantity, and translational impact of research on deprescribing for older adults. Through a

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT STORRS
phr: Project Narrative
Forty percent of children with Cerebral Palsy have hemiplegia, characterized by impaired movement skills and
muscle strength on one side of their body, leading to significant limitations in daily function and participation.
This project proposes an innovative, child- and family-friendly, community-based training program using
modified, commercially-available, joystick-operated ride-on-toys to promote self-initiated use and movement
skills in the affected upper extremity in young children with hemiplegia between 3 and 8 years. Study findings
have implications for the adoption of age-appropriate, highly enjoyable, and easy-to-operate modified toys by
families as an adjunct to conventional therapy to increase children's functional use of their affected arm

title: Data Science and Medical Image Analysis Training for Improved Health Care Delivery in Nigeria - DATICAN
org_name: LAGOS STATE UNIVERSITY TEACHING HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative In the battle against Africa’s dual burden of infectious and chronic non-communicable diseases, there is an urgent need for more scientists highly skilled in conducting data science-driven medical research. Our program will increase the number of highly skilled data scientists in Nigeria and build repositories of ‘big data’ for future research and education efforts, leading ultimately to improved health care for vulnerable populations in sub-Saharan Africa and around the globe.

activity_code: R00
title: Deep Learning Approaches to Decipher the Impact of Mobile Element Insertion on Alternative Splicing in Neurological Disorders
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Mobile element insertions (MEIs) represent a largely undefined component of the genetic architecture of neurological disorders, as a number of MEIs have been associated with alternative splicing in these disorders but large-scale genome-wide functional characterization has not been systematically performed across tissues. This program study will functionally characterize the impact of MEIs on alternative splicing from whole-genome sequencing and transcriptome sequencing in large cohorts using new developments in deep learning models. These results will enhance our understanding of the etiological role and pathogenic mechanisms associated with MEIs in neuronal development and human neurological disorders.

title: Couple-based prenatal contraceptive education program for families
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TENNESSEE KNOXVILLE
phr: Planning Together, a contraceptive education program, aims to improve consistent desired contraceptive use postpartum. Planning Together targets multiple levels of factors impacting contraceptive use, including (a) harnessing existing social support, (b) reducing challenges with transportation, healthcare, and knowledge gaps, via (c) providing a flexible delivery method to increase availability and scalability. This innovative couple-based, multi-level intervention has the potential to redesign existing family planning education modes to improve consistently desired couple contraceptive usage, with the longer-term goal of reducing short interpregnancy intervals (SIIs).

phr: To bridge gaps in the genomics workforce for different population groups, Meharry Medical College is seeking to establish a Meharry Center for Genome Research (MCGR). The Center will realize our vision to promote genomic research through infrastructure building and the formation of basic, clinical, and computational science research teams that are interdisciplinary and community-engaged. It will also facilitate a sustainable partnership among institutions such as MMC, Fisk University, and Tennessee State University in Nashville to train the genomics workforce.

application_id: 10984593
activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement
org_name: GEORGE WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
 This proposed diversity supplement will extend the parent grant's research on magnesium
(Mg) supplementation in patients with diabetes mellitus (DM) and heart failure (HF), examining its
differential impact across diverse racial, ethnic, and gender groups. In this research, we will
examine the differential effect of Mg supplementation and replacements on incident HF in patients
with DM and on mortality and hospitalization in patients with HF by race, gender, and ethnicity.
We will also study providers’ and patients’ perspectives on Mg supplementation.

application_id: 11033809
activity_code: RF1
title: High-resolution bidirectional optical-acoustic mesoscopic neural interface for image-guided neuromodulation in behaving animals - Diversity Supplement
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: NARRATIVE The high tissue penetrability of ultrasound (US) waves presents untapped and exciting opportunities for accessing structures throughout the mammalian brain. In this supplement to our RF1 project, we will further advance methods pioneered in our labs to extend the depth penetration and resolution of optoacoustic imaging to support whole-brain functional imaging of distributed neuronal activity in scattering tissue, with a focus on the olfactory bulb in awake, behaving animals. These developments would break new ground in the field of brain function in large mammalian brains.

application_id: 11062590
activity_code: R01
title: Diagnosis of Cancer in the Emergency Room - Explaining Persistent Disparities - Diversity Supplement
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Cancer is a frequent diagnosis in the emergency department (ED); ED cancer diagnoses are more common among racial minorities and low-income patients and result in poor outcomes, including lower survival. Evidence about emergency diagnosis of lung cancer in the United States (U.S.), and how to prevent it from occurring, is limited. This supplement will generate much needed evidence about emergency diagnosis of lung cancer, focusing on associations and outcomes among patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and predictors of survival to help identify modifiable drivers of disparities in patients with the poorest prognoses.

activity_code: R01
title: Local Flavor Policies to Enhance Equity in Tobacco - A Diversity Supplement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project seeks to examine whether innovative local tobacco control policies restricting sales of flavored tobacco products can reduce tobacco use and disparities in tobacco and, ultimately cancer. The study will examine trajectories of flavored tobacco use in a large longitudinal national cohort of youth and young adults among those exposed and not exposed to policy. It will also examine youth and young adult non-tobacco user exposure to tobacco marketing and subsequent tobacco use outcomes in real-time in eight communities with different flavor policy variants and will have a particular focus on assessing differences in policy impact across vulnerable group status.

application_id: 11136921
activity_code: R33
title: Cognitive Training for Emotion Regulation in Psychotic Disorders
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA
phr: Project Narrative
Prior studies have implicated problems with emotion regulation in the emergence of hallucinations and
delusions in people diagnosed with psychotic disorders. We are evaluating whether a cognitive training
program administered via a mobile app can improve these difficulties with emotion regulation by enhancing
brain activation in the prefrontal cortex. If effective, a new, low cost treatment option will be available that can
be employed in everyday life via smart phones.

application_id: 11146878
activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement to R01 DC018042
org_name: MONELL CHEMICAL SENSES CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chemosensory loss contributes to malnutrition, depression, and reduced quality of life in affected patients. In this project, we will investigate the role of inflammation in taste loss using both a transgenic approach and a respiratory viral infection model to determine the relationship between inflammation, taste loss, and taste bud regeneration to provide mechanistic insights for future therapies aimed at correcting or preventing immune- related taste dysfunction. This supplement funding will directly enable the technical and conceptual development of Mr. Elias Mimouni, a Black U.S. citizen, as he works toward pursuing a career as a physician scientist in the biomedical field.

application_id: 10886937
activity_code: R01
title: Perceived racism, cardiovascular disease risk, and neurocognitive aging: Diversity Supplement
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: AWARDED PROJECT NARRATIVE Although Black Americans are twice as likely to have Alzheimer’s disease than White Americans, little is known about the impact of social and societal determinants of health on neurocognitive integrity of the medial temporal hippocampal memory and prefrontal-executive systems, the primary brain systems impacted in Alzheimer’s disease. The objective of this application is to investigate the impact of interpersonal and institutional/structural racism on neurocognitive integrity of these brain systems in Black adults aged 65 years and older and to examine cardiovascular disease risk as an underlying biological mechanism. Addressing this knowledge gap is critical for developing new health policies and biobehavioral and social interventions for healthy aging.

application_id: 10985597
activity_code: R01
title: Theory and Modeling of Functional Conformational Changes of RNA Polymerases_Diversity Supplement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: The computational methodologies we develop will be applicable to a broad range of conformational changes of RNA polymerases with biomedical relevance. In particular, oxidative DNA lesions cause transcriptional mutagenesis of RNA Polymerase II that contributes to tumor growth in skin cancer, and mechanistic insights from this work will help uncover a route to address the adverse effects of these DNA lesions. In addition, antibiotics against Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) often target the dynamic motion of its RNA polymerase, and this work will provide mechanistic insights to facilitate the rational design of new antibiotics fighting drug resistance of Mtb.

application_id: 11090776
activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement for Tran: High-resolution Infrared Thermal Imaging (ITI) for Simultaneous Functional Mapping of the Entire Craniotomy in Awake Patients
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Due to neurovascular coupling, neural activity creates a reliable increase in blood flow and hence local temperature, which can be measured in the operating room with an infrared thermographic camera. While an awake patient performs tasks, this new method of monitoring the patterns of temperature fluctuation can be used to create a map of brain function, that once validated the surgical team can use to avoid damage to key functional areas. This can lead to safer, faster, and more effective surgeries for patients with drug-resistant epilepsy, glioma, or neurovascular malformations.

application_id: 11100770
activity_code: R35
title: Destruction of noncoding RNAs - R35 Diversity Supplement
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: NARRATIVE When cells mature or respond to changes in their environment, they turn on some genes, turn off others, and destroy gene products that are no longer needed. When this process goes awry, cancer and other diseases can occur. This proposal focuses on identifying how cells destroy two classes of gene products—microRNAs and circular RNAs—that are important for controlling the expression of other genes.

application_id: 10987614
activity_code: R01
title: Building a Complete, Predictive, Data-Driven Model of Action Selection During Olfactory Navigation: Diversity Supplement for Postbaccalaureate Training
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA BARBARA
phr: Project narrative We do not yet understand why animals behave the way they do, why the same sensory input (e.g., a smell or a sound) can elicit vastly different behaviors across individuals. To study how animals interpret sensory information and turn this information into decisions for survival, we examine a simple creature –a fly larva– as it performs one of the most important survival tasks: finding a food source. By quantifying, modeling, and then controlling the nervous system of a larva while it converts information from an ambiguous world into accurate navigational decisions, we aim to reveal the inner workings of a simple yet remarkably powerful brain and build new theories of how our own brain works.

application_id: 10948643
activity_code: R01
title: Forecasting Trachoma Control - Diversity Supplement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The WHO currently recommends annual mass azithromycin distribution in trachoma-endemic areas until the district-level prevalence of trachomatous inflammation-follicular (TF) drops below 5%. Since there is no true gold standard test to assess the prevalence of active disease, we will use a model which assumes a true, but latent or hidden state of infection. We anticipate that the results of this study will provide will show modestly improved precision of detecting the true state of trachomatis infection in a hypoendemic and hyperendemic communities.

title: Diversity Supplement to R01 Parent Grant HL150624
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE We hypothesize that mutant p53 enhances inflammatory stress via activating the NLRP1 inflammasome and increasing the secretion of proinflammatory cytokines, thereby generating a chronic inflammatory microenvironment that inhibits wild type (WT) hematopoietic stem and progenitor cell (HSPC) fitness in a paracrine fashion. title: Diversity Supplement - Environmental surveillance of albendazole resistance markers in human infective helminths using dual amplicon metabarcoding
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: NARRATIVE – PARENT GRANT Intestinal parasites, including soil-transmitted helminths (STH), infect over 1.5 billion people worldwide, resulting in a massive global burden of disease. Mass drug administration (MDA) to high risk populations is the cornerstone of the global strategy for controlling STH infections; however, varied MDA program success indicates that re-infection from environmental reservoirs is common. This proposal leverages a novel, sensitive and specific molecular assay to detect STH DNA in large volumes of soil within a multi-country community-wide MDA randomized controlled trial to define, for the first time, the impact of an MDA intervention on soil reservoirs of STH, and to evaluate the potential for using soil STH DNA as a non-invasive surveillance tool to monitor human ...

application_id: 11064719
activity_code: U54
title: Green Diversity Supplement: Predicting 5-ALA Fluorescence Status in High Grade Gliomas Based on MRI Features
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ARIZONA
phr: Narrative High-grade gliomas, including the particularly aggressive brain tumor known as glioblastoma, present significant challenges for treatment due to their invasive nature and tendency to recur after surgery. Our project aims to assist with surgical planning and improve surgical outcomes by combining advanced imaging techniques with a method called fluorescence-guided surgery, which helps surgeons more accurately identify and remove tumor tissue. By analyzing how brain tumors look under combined imaging modalities, we plan to develop a predictive model that can inform surgeons of tumor characteristics in advance of surgery.

application_id: 10939124
activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement: Optically Promoting Cardiac Maturation Using Engineered Peptides
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE (of Parent Grant: R01HL164348)  Stem cell-based regenerative therapies or in vitro investigations of cardiovascular disease pathophysiology, drug screening, and toxicology studies heavily rely on stem cell quality. Here, peptide nanostructures will be engineered to enable cells to be light responsive in a wireless and non-genetic manner, which can ultimately be utilized as a strategy for converting optical cues to stimulatory signals that can improve the structural and functional maturity of human stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes. With this new approach, we hope to alleviate the major technical barrier of CM immaturity, and therefore, better enable stem cell-based cardiovascular research and related fields to flourish.

title: Retinal circuit disassembly in primate glaucoma - Diversity Supplement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: NARRATIVE Recent studies in mice suggest that specific types of retinal ganglion cells, the neurons that degenerate in glaucoma, are more susceptible to injury. However, translating these findings to the human condition is not straightforward since the mouse and human eye differ in several major ways, including the ocular structures and types of retinal ganglion cells found in the two species. Here we propose a detailed multi-modal assessment of the mechanisms underlying ganglion cell susceptibility in a highly clinically relevant model of glaucoma, which will lead to improved understanding of the disease, novel targets for earlier diagnosis and treatment, and in turn, better options for glaucoma patients to preserve and potentially improve their vision.

application_id: 11034413
activity_code: R35
title: Diversity Supplement to Genetic Dissection of Signaling and Cilia
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Primary cilia are microtubule-based projections on virtually every cell type and are linked to multiple signaling pathways. Among the best studied is Sonic hedgehog (Shh), which requires the cilium for signal transduction. Here we use a series of mouse models to genetically uncouple ciliogenesis and vertebrate Hh signaling, and in the process paint a clearer mechanistic picture of each. These proposed experiments have great potential to give us the fundamental knowledge we need to develop strategies to treat diseases related to abnormal cilia or Shh signaling.

application_id: 11082842
activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement: Neuropsychological Norms for Bilingual Spanish speaking Hispanic/Latinos
org_name: FLORIDA ATLANTIC UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative There is inconsistent evidence indicating that Bilingualism might increase Cognitive Reserve and or resilience (CR/R). We propose a multidisciplinary longitudinal study to compare Bilinguals and Monolinguals with amnestic Mild Cognitive Impairment from an equivalent cultural background in neuropsychological and neuroimaging data. Our findings will advance understanding of the complex interactions between neural, environmental, and sociocultural factors and the role of Bilingualism in CR/R in MCI, paving the way for new targets for interventions and providing fundamental insight into the role of language(s) in the aging brain.

title: Forecasting Trachoma Control - Diversity Supplement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO

title: Diversity Supplement for Development of an Oscillated Insertion tool to Eliminate Surgically Induced Neurodegeneration for Optical Neuroimaging of Cognitive Aging and Dementia
org_name: ACTUATED MEDICAL, INC.
phr: Project Narrative: Relevance – Age related cognitive decline creates substantial emotional and financial burden as well as reduced quality of life for patients and their families. Better tools are needed for study of these conditions in preclinical models. Implanted devices generate a chronic inflammatory state caused by vascular perforation and crushing of brain tissue which leads to encapsulation of the implanted device by scar tissue and loss of neural density. This SBIR project develops the Optical Neuroscience Insertion Tool (ONIT) for reduced insertion force and tissue damage during placement of optical imaging systems (fiber optic cannula, GRIN lenses) in preclinical studies.

title: Diversity Supplement: Quantitative Detection of Coronary Microvascular Dysfunction in Long COVID Patients using a Comprehensive, Rapid, Free-Breathing Cardiovascular MRI
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: This study seeks to discover the reason why patients with long COVID suffer from persistent chest pain symptoms. Using advanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), this study will determine: (a) whether MRI accurately and precisely detects blockage in small blood vessels supplying oxygen to the heart in long COVID patients; (b) whether the said blockage is the cause of persistent chest pain symptoms in long COVID patients; (c) whether the said blockage resolves better with medicine. phr: Project Narrative Cells must inherit the right number of chromosomes every time they divide. application_id: 11065149
activity_code: R35
title: UG Diversity Supplement: Epithelial cell-cell junction remodeling in response to cell- and tissue-scale forces
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cells in epithelial tissues are connected by cell-cell junctions that sense mechanical forces and respond by activating signaling pathways, recruiting mechanosensitive proteins, and remodeling their connections to the cytoskeleton. Disruption of cell-cell junctions contributes to cancer cell metastasis and barrier function diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease. The proposed research will strengthen our understanding of how epithelial cells sense and respond to physiological forces to maintain, reinforce, and remodel their cell-cell junctions; therefore, this research will help us better understand the pathogenesis of cancer and barrier function diseases and potentially identify new targets for treating these diseases.

application_id: 11181026
activity_code: U54
title: Career Enhancement Program
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE – CAREER ENCHANCEMENT PROGRAM 
The Developmental and HyperActive Ras Tumor (DHART) SPORE Career Enhancement Program (CEP) supports faculty at the Instructor and Assistant Professor levels to conduct translational research focused on developing treatments for tumors characterized by NF1 driver mutations. The CEP funds innovative pilot research projects by early-stage investigators and provides them with access to scientific expertise and and structured mentorship from SPORE scientists. The overall goal of the CEP is to accelerate the career development of talented young scientists who advancer the field of cancer biology and therapeutics related to NF1.

application_id: 11159811
activity_code: U54
title: Yale Center for the Study of Tobacco Product Use and Addiction (YCSTP)
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE - OVERALL
The Yale Center for the Study of Tobacco Product Use and Addiction (YCSTP) will examine how ingredients in
tobacco products, including nicotine and chemicals that produce cooling and sweetness, contribute to product
initiation, continued use, and addiction. We also will investigate if these ingredients may help people switch
from smoking cigarettes to using non-combustible products like e-cigarettes and oral nicotine pouches. Career
development activities and collaborations will develop the next generation of tobacco regulatory scientists.

application_id: 11145014
activity_code: DP1
title: Fusion of nanomagnetic and viral tools to interrogate brain-body circuits
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Narrative
This project aims to develop a broadly applicable platform for targeting and modulating activity of neurons and
other electrogenic cells anywhere within the body. This approach will be further applied to interrogate circuits
connecting the enteric (gut) neurons to the midbrain pathways delivering insights into the role of gut-brain
connections in modulating dopamine-dependent behaviors. Our technologies and findings would pave way for
development of organ-centric therapies for neurological and mental conditions.

activity_code: DP1
title: Framework to Accelerate Substance Use Disorder Genetic Studies through Customizable, EHR-Based Precision Phenotyping
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research is relevant to public health because substance use disorders (such as opioids,
tobacco, and alcohol) are common, costly, and contribute to poor health outcomes. We are proposing the
development of a method that helps researchers identify individuals with complex variations of these disorders
in large data sets linked to genetic data in hopes of finding novel prevention and treatment interventions. The
proposal is relevant to the National Institute on Drug Abuse’s emphasis on “…supporting and conducting basic
and clinical research on drug use…and the underlying neurobiological…mechanisms involved.”

org_name: STEELWORKER CHARITABLE AND EDUCATIONAL ORGANIZATION
phr: The Tony Mazzocchi Center (TMC) will conduct 1,920 courses that will reach 29,950 DOE site workers, Tribal members, and community residents with 372,900 hours of training over the proposed five-year grant from 2026-2030. application_id: 11285191
activity_code: DP1
title: The neuropharmacology of brain activation during stages of drug abuse
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative
Dopamine signaling is key to the rewarding effects of stimulant drugs, but much less is known about the
involvement of other neurotransmitters and receptors in response to repeated drug exposure. Whole-brain
multimodal imaging will be used to non-invasively measure the interaction between neurotransmitter systems,
specifically neuroadaptations occurring at dopamine-glutamate targets. This study will reveal the importance of
multi-receptor interactions and the potential role of glutamate and other targets in the treatment of drug addiction.

application_id: 10907110
activity_code: DP1
title: Engineering Bacteriophage T4 as a Targeted Gene Therapy Drug for in vivo HIV Cure
org_name: CATHOLIC UNIVERSITY OF AMERICA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE More than 40 million people across the globe are now living with HIV-1, of which the substance users constitute a disproportionate and “key population”, yet there is no cure or vaccine on the horizon. Using our recently developed bacteriophage T4 artificial virus vector technology, this proposal will establish a novel and affordable in vivo HIV cure platform that can be easily administered to substance users through one or two infusions. The HIV cure “drug” will target stem cells and introduce genetic modifications that confer resistance to HIV infection and then, by repopulating the body with HIV-resistant blood cells including resistant T cells, it replaces the HIV reservoir and leads to functional HIV cure.

title: High-throughput identification and characterization of conserved regulators of drug-induced plasticity using single-neuron resolution atlases of the complete Caenorhabditis elegans nervous system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative Chronic exposure to drugs such as psychostimulants produces long-lasting molecular and functional changes in the brain, which in vulnerable individuals result in behavioral changes that further predispose them to loss of control in their drug use and progression of Substance Use Disorder (SUD). These drugs produce remarkably similar behavioral outputs and act on similar molecular targets in the nervous system (brain equivalent) of the simple yet powerful model organism Caenorhabditis elegans. In this proposal, we leverage the capabilities for high-resolution, large-scale, and rapid genetic analysis of the entire nervous system in C. elegans to identify candidate regulators of long-lasting drug-induced changes and open up novel avenues of therapeutic interventions for SUD.
