application_id: 11184312
activity_code: DP1
title: Elucidating chronic liver disease pathways using somatic genetics
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There are no high throughput genetic methods to uncover mechanisms that drive chronic liver disease's
progression to cirrhosis, limiting our ability to develop new therapies. Recently, we showed that some somatic
mutations within diseased livers can exert adaptive effects that might benefit tissue health. Using new
technologies developed in my lab, we propose to exploit somatic mosaicism as a genetic strategy to 1) identify
adaptive genetic pathways and 2) understand how mutant clones expand within chronically damaged tissues,
and potentially protect from disease.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Female genital mutilation/cutting (FGM/C) affects girls and women throughout the world, with 3 million girls cut each year, and resulting in significant reproductive, sexual, and mental health needs for impacted patients. The scientific goal of this project is to study the effects of a community-based music training program on neurodevelopment among Hispanic children from low-income communities. application_id: 10730933
activity_code: R15
title: Biasing immunological development with early life microbial colonization
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MASSACHUSETTS BOSTON
phr: Project Narrative
Studies designed to probe the equilibrium model of immunity may be key to making improvements in preventative
medicine and health outcomes for wide range of health challenges; in particular, we utilize an emerging fungal
disease system. We will develop innovative tools, including the Xenopus gnotobiotic system, Fusarium infection
model, custom NanoString immune gene panel, and a training program focused on the inclusion of
undergraduate trainees in a Course-based Undergraduate Research Experience. We will advance the study of
immunology and provide a clear test of the central hypothesis that immune equilibrium is established in early
development and education of the immune system can be biased by hysteresis from previous host
encounters with microbes.

activity_code: DP1
title: Placental models to support embryogenesis in vitro
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: This research will shed light on a poorly understood but critical period of mammalian development
that spans the embryo's implantation into the uterus, the period when many human pregnancies
terminate, and thus will yield clinically relevant discoveries in developmental and reproductive
biology. The systems being developed will form the basis of approaches to decipher how
pathogens, toxins and teratogens affect early development, and so will have environmental and
drug safety value. Moreover, as this research utilizes stem cell biology to drive the formation and
development of synthetic embryo-like structures, it is expected to inform regenerative medicine
applications for a wide range of diseases.

application_id: 11180366
activity_code: DP1
title: The Role of the Macrophage in Bacterial Vaginosis Mediated HIV Risk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
Bacterial Vaginosis is mediated by a non-optimal vaginal microbiome, is characterized by vaginal inflammation,
and increases HIV risk by mechanisms that are not completely understood. This project aims to elucidate the
role that macrophages play in increasing HIV risk in women who have BV, using multi-omic analyses and
machine learning. The knowledge obtained from this proposal will contribute to mechanistic understanding of
BV-mediated HIV transmission, which will provide insight necessary to improve strategies for vaginal health
and HIV prevention.

title: Surgical Simulator for Improving Skill Proficiency and Resilience
org_name: KITWARE, INC.
phr: Project Narrative
Virtual simulators are powerful tools for training surgeons in skill-intensive minimally invasive
procedures, however, to date most available simulators focus mainly on new surgeons in formal

application_id: 11145969
activity_code: DP1
title: Emergence of activity patterns in the cerebral cortex and their influence on brain circuit development and function
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The project presents a new approach to studying mammalian brain development. The proposal is to apply
modern molecular tools and new behavioural assays in a mouse-sized marsupial that is born at stage 18
(equivalent to human seven-week gestation) and develops further in their mother's pouch. Longitudinal studies
in this model will provide unprecedented access to study the functional and behavioural outcomes of fetal
disruptions to circuitry and could be applied to test therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 10738746
activity_code: DP2
title: Revealing the Biophysics of the Germinal Center Microenvironment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS MED BR GALVESTON
phr: Project Narrative The fact that a small subset of HIV infected individuals develop broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) capable of neutralizing 98% of HIV viruses gives substantial promise that an HIV vaccine is possible. However, there has been a lack of understanding of the biophysical constraints of the germinal center microenvironment (GCME), which is the arena in which B cells compete to develop into bnAb producing B cells. We aim to uncover the composition of the extracellular space within the GCME, with the goal of identifying novel biophysical constraints and metabolic pathways that may be recruited during vaccination to elicit generation of bnAb producing B cells.

This study will examine the efficacy of a gait training program in chronic stroke survivors who cannot walk

application_id: 11141741
activity_code: DP2
title: Characterizing Macrophages as "Hide-Outs" for Chronic Pathogens
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Both HIV and Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) establish life-long infections that, despite the development of
antiretroviral therapy and antibiotics, cause millions of deaths every year, emphasizing the need to develop a
cure. This proposal aims to define the methods by which HIV and Mtb evade immune-mediated clearance by
characterizing their common target cell, the macrophage, and how this cell acts to shield these pathogens from
immune responses. Insight into these mechanisms will help identify macrophage-specific pathways that can be
exploited to make these infected cells more susceptible to immune-mediated clearance, potentiating the
development of cure-based strategies for HIV and Mtb infection.

application_id: 10754502
activity_code: DP2
title: MOLECULAR ANALYSIS OF MALARIA MITOCHONDRIAL GENE REGULATION
org_name: IDAHO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: In the human malaria parasite, Plasmodium falciparum, the lack of knowledge regarding most essential processes and regulatory pathways, particularly in the mitochondria, is a critical barrier to identifying and developing new drug targets. Ideally these targets are highly conserved and should be resistant to mutation or have limited ability to change in order to evolve resistance. This proposal outlines methods to identify and define previously unknown and uncharacterized aspects of parasite mitochondrial biology, with the goal of advancing rational drug design against this deadly parasite.

application_id: 11176089
activity_code: R36
title: Molecular Mechanisms of VMAT2 Function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Narrative The vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) is essential for loading neurotransmitters such as dopamine, serotonin and norepinephrine into synaptic vesicles before their release into the synapse. Amphetamine has been shown to alter VMAT2 function, causing release of neurotransmitters from synaptic vesicles. In this proposal, we aim to study the structure and function of VMAT2 and the differences that occur as a result of amphetamine in order to generate new therapies to treat neuropsychiatric disorders and amphetamine abuse.

application_id: 11092303
activity_code: DP2
title: Differential regulation of T cell plasma membrane proteins by N-glycan branching and clathrin-mediated endocytosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The N-glycan branching pathway controls immunity and inflammation by controlling turnover rates of proteins
on the surface of T cells. However, which proteins are regulated in this manner, and how this pathway
promotes expression of some proteins while inhibiting expression of others is unknown. We propose using a
quantitative proteomic approach to analyze all surface proteins simultaneously and to examine the hypothesis
that these extracellular glycans cooperate with intracellular endocytosis machinery to differentially regulate cell-
surface protein turnover.

title: Tolerance-programming biomaterial-based Intranasal ASIT for the treatment of autoimmunity
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative:
As many as 23.5 million Americans suffer from autoimmune dieses and current therapies are
non-curative and often systemically immunosuppressive, increasing the risk of opportunistic
infections and cancer. The aim of this proposal is to develop an antigen-specific immunotherapy
that upon intranasal administration binds to nasal mucus, passes via paracellular transport to the
nasal immune system, and actively programs dendritic cells to generate autoantigen-specific
regulatory T cells that suppress autoimmunity. Completion of this project will demonstrate the
efficacy of a clinically-viable cure for autoimmunity in a preclinical model of autoimmune
myocarditis and establish a platform technology that can be easily modified to treat various
autoimmune and allergic dieses as well as...

application_id: 11136939
activity_code: DP2
title: Real-time predictive modeling for public health departments to control infectious diseases
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Public health departments increasingly use predictive models to make decisions on the best policies to reduce infectious diseases in the United States. Yet there is limited evidence on how these predictive models are used by public health departments and whether their use translates into policies that reduce infectious diseases. The
proposed research aims to develop predictive modeling tools for California public health departments to allocate targeted vaccines to their highest-risk populations, and then study the implementation and public health impact of using these predictive tools to guide real-time vaccination policy.

application_id: 10898034
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the mechanism of herpesvirus genome packaging
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Herpesviruses are important human pathogens that can have devastating effects in neonates and immunocompromised individuals, yet there is no cure for any herpesvirus and treatment options are lacking. Here, I dissect the mechanism of an essential feature of the herpesviral lifecycle - viral genome packaging - by exploring the role of a unique accessory factor required for packaging and by developing an assay to monitor packaging in vitro. Detailed mechanistic insight is essential to understand the mechanism of current antiherpesviral drugs and to facilitate the identification of new targets for antivirals.

phr: Project Narrative
The intestine exerts the important function of maximizing nutrient uptake from the diet ingested during feeding
periods. Maintenance of intestinal health requires the precise regulation of local immune responses against
microbes and dietary components. However, there is a major gap in our understanding of how dietary-derived
signals affect intestinal immune responses during feeding. Gut neurons have emerged as important regulators
of intestinal immune responses, since they interact with immune cells and are quickly activated by the presence
of food in the intestine. Our studies of how diet modulates gut neurons that control intestinal immunity are
expected to uncover dietary and neuronal therapeutic targets to gut disturbances associated with dysregulation

application_id: 11136265
activity_code: DP2
title: Emerging mechanisms of viral gene regulation from battles between host and SARS-CoV-2
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Coronaviruses, including the pandemic SARS-CoV-2, have posed severe threats to global health and led
to substantial economic loss. However, the limited knowledge of coronavirus biology and pathogenesis greatly
hinders the development of effective antiviral treatments. This work aims to understand how the dynamic
interplay between SARS-CoV-2 and host factors controls viral gene expression with the goal of revealing novel
druggable targets for innovative strategies against these highly pathogenic coronaviruses.

application_id: 11140355
activity_code: DP2
title: The HEART Study: Measuring the Impact of Public Health Programming
org_name: BOSTON COLLEGE
phr: Improving population health is a central goal of public health and a key outcome of federal initiatives. Yet public health professionals currently lack standardized tools to assess whether programs are equipped to produce consistent, high-quality outcomes. This project addresses that gap through the development of the HEART Tool, a validated and pragmatic measure of public health program capability. By enabling the earlier identification of implementation challenges and supporting program refinement, the HEART Tool will contribute essential knowledge to strengthen HIV-related care and prevention efforts in the United States, thereby improving outcomes across a range of public health conditions.

application_id: 11173706
activity_code: DP2
title: Essential functions of the mitochondrion in malaria parasites
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In the face of increasing resistance to frontline antimalarials, there is a critical need for new drugs that
can act against both human and mosquito stages of malaria infection. The highly divergent mitochondrion
of the malaria parasite is an attractive drug target, but harnessing its full potential requires in-depth
understanding of the organelle’s biology and metabolic pathways. This project aims to identify core
functions of the mitochondrion that are likely to be essential across all life stages of the parasite.

application_id: 10951271
activity_code: DP2
title: Deciphering the immunogenicity of cell death using systematic genetic tools
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative The core function of immunity is to distinguish self from non-self, yet in the process of sterile immunogenic cell death, our immune system recognizes our own dead cells as foreign. This process can thus lead to autoimmunity, though it can also be useful in cancer treatment if induced in the right time and place. This proposal seeks to apply breakthrough functional genomic technologies to interrogate the molecular basis of immunogenic cell death and potentiate new treatments in autoimmunity, cancer, and other diseases of the immune system.

activity_code: DP5
title: Computational Methods for Enhancing Privacy in Biomedical Data Sharing
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Rapidly-growing volume of biomedical datasets around the world promises to enable
unprecedented insights into human health and disease. However, increasing concerns for
individual privacy severely limited the extent of data sharing in the field. This project draws upon
cutting-edge tools from cryptography and information theory to develop effective privacy-
preserving methods for collecting, sharing, and analyzing sensitive biomedical data to empower
advances in genomics and medicine.

activity_code: DP5
title: Revealing tissue microstructure in the brain gray matter in Alzheimer's disease using in vivo high-gradient diffusion MRI
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
More than five million people in the United States living with Alzheimer’s disease, and this number grows
substantially due to the population aging, resulting in burdens on the economy, families and caregivers of patients.
While volumetric MRI biomarkers are useful to assess the risk of Alzheimer’s disease, their diagnostic value is
still limited since the association between biomarkers and neuropathological changes remains unclear. With the
help of advanced high-gradient MRI system, this project will establish an in vivo diffusion MRI microscopy
technology targeting tissue microstructure in the brain gray matter, which will expand our understanding of
underlying mechanisms of disease progression, and may provide prognostic and confirmatory biomarkers and
aid in the asses...

application_id: 11170029

title: Identifying transcriptomic and epigenomic convergence in intellectual and developmental disorders
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) have poor health outcomes, and their life
expectancy is 20 years shorter than the rest of the U.S. population. The limited arsenal of efficacious treatments
for these disorders exacerbates this public health emergency. This proposal will develop a framework for

mechanism-based therapies.

application_id: 11169718
activity_code: DP5
title: Therapeutic Potential of a Novel MGMT-Dependent DNA Interstrand Crosslinking Agent in the Treatment of DNA Repair Deficient Cancer
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
DNA repair abnormalities occur frequently across human cancer types, presenting the potential for targeted
tumor treatment via synthetic lethal approaches. We recently discovered a novel DNA modifying agent which
generates DNA interstrand crosslinks specifically in tumor cells lacking the DNA direct repair enzyme O6-
methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase (MGMT) and is effective in temozolomide (TMZ)-resistant glioma
models. This proposal seeks to understand the therapeutic potential of this compound in a broader array of
MGMT-deficient cancers, specifically by targeting additional defects in interstrand crosslink repair and by
circumventing TMZ resistance.

application_id: 10932139
activity_code: DP5
title: Deciphering the lipid composition of primary cilia in human metabolic disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Obesity currently affects 42% of U.S. adults, costing 173 billion dollars in health care costs annually. Human genetics links obesity to defects in an organelle called the primary cilium, where G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) transmit extracellular information across the cell membrane to direct cell signaling events. This project will decipher how the primary cilium membrane controls GPCR activity and how it responds to dietary lipid consumption with the long-term goal of finding better strategies to treat metabolic disease.

title: Unlocking microglia targeting for neurotherapeutics
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Microglia, the immune cells of the brain and spinal cord, are the next frontier for treating neurological diseases. application_id: 11169055
activity_code: DP5
title: MDMA as a Treatment for Social Deficits in Schizophrenia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
The goal of this project is to test the prosocial compound ± 3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) as
a novel treatment for a debilitating symptom of schizophrenia, impaired social motivation. This project will
proceed in two phases; the aim of Phase 1 will be to establish the tolerability of MDMA in this patient
population, and the aim of Phase 2 will be to test the effects of MDMA on social motivation. Results from these
studies will be foundational in identifying effective psychopharmacologic and combined psychopharmacologic-
psychosocial therapies for social deficits in schizophrenia.

application_id: 11195024
activity_code: DP5
title: Leveraging cell-derived bioparticles for macromolecular delivery
org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: 8. PROJECT NARRATIVE
The ability to deliver macromolecules into cells within the body is required for many emerging therapeutic
modalities, including genome editing therapies, but there are few available methods for safe and efficient
macromolecular delivery. The proposed work will explore the use of cell-derived bioparticles for therapeutic
macromolecular delivery, with a focus on (1) understanding cellular factors that influence bioparticle formation
and (2) developing new strategies for highly efficient bioparticle production. These advances would overcome
the key limitations that currently prevent the widespread use of cell-derived bioparticles, unlocking the potential
of these bioparticles to serve as next-generation therapeutic delivery vehicles to treat a wide range of
diseases.

application_id: 11195543
activity_code: DP5
title: A hierarchical examination of the neural and computational mechanisms underlying loneliness
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
The United States currently faces a public health epidemic of isolation and loneliness—with roughly half of U.S.
adults reporting sometimes or always feeling lonely—but the neural and computational mechanisms that
underlie forming and maintaining interpersonal relationships at the individual-level are still poorly understood,
which will be crucial for generating new policies, programs, health care, and preventative measures. This
proposal aims to characterize how and when computations along a cognitive hierarchy of social information
processing (e.g., perception, learning, decision-making) impact social connection, and consequently,
loneliness. Outcomes of this work will elucidate the interplay between mental health outcomes and
computations underlying interpersonal relat...

title: Understanding data-driven models for neural dynamics
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
To extract mechanistic insights into brain function and its disorders from large-scale neural recordings, modern
systems neuroscience demands new theoretical approaches and data analysis tools. The proposed project aims
to build rigorous foundations for data-driven recurrent neural network modeling. It will elucidate the fundamental
domain of validity of this increasingly popular paradigm for making sense of high-dimensional neural data.

application_id: 10989935
activity_code: K00
title: Characterizing CAFs in Metastasis as a Function of Breast Cancer Cell Phenotype
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative After metastasis to the lungs, breast cancer cells are exposed to unique forces and matrix proteins that are not present at the primary tumor. My previous findings indicate that cyclic stretching, mimicking breathing dynamics within a 3D model of the early metastatic niche, can drive tumor cells into a state of growth arrest without widespread cell death. Because stromal cells are the key contributor of matrix composition and architecture, my goal is to evaluate the unique fibroblast subtypes conditioned by breast cancer cells at different stages of the metastatic cascade and characterize how the tissue changes they produce alter the metastatic progression of disseminated breast cancer cells.

activity_code: K00
title: The Impact of SARS-CoV-2 Infection and Vaccination on the Risk of Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposed research will determine the effect of SARS-CoV-2 infection on Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) risk via brain structure changes and evaluate how vaccination status and timing of SARS- CoV-2 infection affect cognitive outcomes and ADRD risk among COVID-19 survivors. The findings from this research could improve knowledge of ADRD in the post-COVID-19 era, and facilitate planning and appropriate resource allocation to care for an aging population and reduce the burden of ADRD. The long-term goal of this line of research is to decrease the global burden of ADRD by advancing our understanding of how major infectious diseases influence long-term ADRD risk.

application_id: 11021046
activity_code: R00
title: Fast motion-robust fetal neuroimaging with MRI
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Subject motion is the fundamental impediment to reaping the full benefits of fetal-brain magnetic resonance
imaging, as it frequently produces images with dramatic artifacts.
The goal of this project is to exploit innovations in deep learning and integrate them into the acquisition pipeline
to overcome the challenges posed by motion in fetal neuroimaging studies.
This will be achieved by using fast, automated scan prescription of standard anatomical planes and by adaptively
updating the acquisition as motion happens in the scanner, based on sub-second navigator scans interleaved
with the imaging sequence.

activity_code: DP1
title: Multiscale Insights into the Epigenetic Basis of THC Consumption
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project narrative
With the advent of legalization, cannabis availability and use are at record highs which has raised concerns
regarding increased incidence of cannabis use disorder (CUD). The experiments in this proposal will fill gaps in

title: Genetic dissection of fluid flow signaling in Left-Right patterning of zebrafish
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: Project Narrative Extracellular fluid flows act signals, either by providing mechanical force or directing chemicals across cell surfaces, however, few proteins that sense and transduce fluid flows signals have been identified. The purpose of this research is to use the zebrafish left-right patterning system to characterize novel proteins that sense and transduce fluid flows. Through this work we will gain insight into diseases caused by aberrant fluid flows like polycystic kidney disease, heterotaxy, and congenital heart defects.

application_id: 11076183
activity_code: F31
title: Brain-wide neural mechanisms enhancing maternal behavioral response to infant cues
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Crying is the only way infants can communicate their needs – unfortunately, maladaptive responses to
infant sounds have been associated with neurological and hormonal pathologies impacting motherhood, such
as those that lead to postpartum depression. Disentangling the neural circuitry that supports adaptive maternal
responses is essential for supporting infant well-being and maternal mental health outcomes. In this proposal, I
will take advantage of cutting-edge whole-brain imaging and genetic tools available in mice to uncover the brain-
wide neural mechanisms that enhance the maternal response to auditory cues from their offspring, with the
potential of identifying ways we can predict and intervene in cases where these responses may go awry.

application_id: 11065809
activity_code: F31
title: Leveraging Embedded Mobile Device Sensors for Detection of Children's Mobile Screen Use: Examining Feasibility and Performance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA AT COLUMBIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE With the ubiquity of mobile devices, the accurate measurement of mobile screen use is critically important for understanding its impact on health-related outcomes in children. Objective measures of mobile screen use, or passive sensing applications, would be most optimal to capture intermittent bouts of screen use longitudinally, however, these applications do not lend themselves to capturing who is using the device at specific time points, introducing a critical limitation in mobile screen use measurement. The proposed study will evaluate the preliminary accuracy of machine learning models trained on sensor data from the built-in sensors on mobile devices, such as accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer, to identify the unique child user of a device.

application_id: 11076659
activity_code: F31
title: Assessing Design Trade-Offs in an Orthosis with Enhanced Functionality and Customizability
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The objective of this project is to assess various options when weighing the goals of dorsiflexion assistance
and energy recycling in a Decoupled Energy Storage and Return Variable-Stiffness Orthosis (DESR-VSO).
Doing so will shed light on how the DESR-VSO can impact the mechanics of foot drop, a condition caused by a
variety of disabilities which can lead to falling, gait compensation, and injury. The rigidity of current ankle-foot
orthoses (AFOs) limits range of motion, which compromises ankle push-off power, and the DESR-VSO seeks to
combat that technological weakness.

application_id: 10928249
activity_code: K23
title: Understanding the Associations between Romantic Relationship Conflict, Psychophysiological Responding and Alcohol Misuse among Emerging Adults
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Emerging adulthood (EA) is a developmental phase characterized by increased risk-taking behaviors including heavy episodic drinking (HED). Romantic relationship conflict may be a particularly salient social influence in EA drinking behavior and a promising target for prevention and intervention, but these associations have not yet been explored. The proposed award will support the candidate’s transition to independence in patient-oriented alcohol research and address this critical gap in the literature by examining the associations between controlled alcohol administration, conflict communication behaviors, and the psychophysiological responses that may underpin this bidirectional relationship.

activity_code: F31
title: Understanding the Role of NAD Deficiency in a Mendelian Disorder of Tryptophan Metabolism
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Kynurenine 3-monooxygenase (KMO) deficiency is a new genetic disorder that disrupts the nicotinamide
adenine dinucleotide (NAD) synthesis pathway, which is critical for proper prenatal and postnatal development.
The proposed studies will utilize two mouse models to investigate birth defects and neurodevelopmental
abnormalities in KMO deficiency. These studies will also dissect the role of NAD deficiency and excess
kynurenine and related metabolites in this disorder and provide insights into the role of NAD and kynurenine
metabolism in normal development.

title: The Impact of Cognitive-Motor Interference on Balance Control in Children with Cerebral Palsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Due to abnormal responses to both expected and unexpected balance challenges, children with cerebral palsy are at an increased risk of falls and resulting injuries. application_id: 11161401
activity_code: K23
title: Understanding the Associations between Romantic Relationship Conflict, Psychophysiological Responding and Alcohol Misuse among Emerging Adults
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Emerging adulthood (EA) is a developmental phase characterized by increased risk-taking behaviors
including heavy episodic drinking (HED). Romantic relationship conflict may be a particularly salient social
influence in EA drinking behavior and a promising target for prevention and intervention, but these associations
have not yet been explored. The proposed award will support the candidate’s transition to independence in
patient-oriented alcohol research and address this critical gap in the literature by examining the associations
between controlled alcohol administration, conflict communication behaviors, and the psychophysiological
responses that may underpin this bidirectional relationship.

title: The effect of genetic variation on mRNA splicing in chondrocytes responding to cartilage matrix damage
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Osteoarthritis affects over 250 million people worldwide, and despite extensive research, a lack of under- standing of the molecular mechanisms underlying its risk factors has hindered the development of effective treatments. title: Identifying the Effects of Race-Related Stressors on Laboratory- Induced Stress and Craving among African Americans with Alcohol Use Disorder
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: Social stress (SS) is related to increased risk for alcohol use disorder (AUD). Understanding how SS affects alcohol use and the risk of developing AUD can inform more effective, tailored interventions. title: Neural mechanisms of anxiety during early and protracted abstinence in alcohol use disorder
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative: Alcohol use disorder is a debilitating neuropsychiatric illness that often presents with
significant anxiety, increasing the risk of relapse. The overall goal of this K23 proposal is to launch the career
of the Principal Investigator, A. Benjamin Srivastava, MD, as an independent translational researcher who can
combine clinical and cognitive neuroscience methods in order to understand the neural mechanisms
underlying anxiety during early and protracted abstinence in alcohol use disorder. Ultimately, this patient-
oriented research program will lead to both improvements in diagnosis and the identification of new treatment
targets aimed improving outcomes in alcohol use disorder and co-occurring psychiatric illnesses.

application_id: 10897328
activity_code: K23
title: Improving the Safety of Treatment for Hospitalized Patients with Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alcohol withdrawal syndrome is a common, potentially fatal condition in hospitalized patients with two dominant therapies (benzodiazepines and phenobarbital), but no rigorous evidence to inform best practice. This K23 award will investigate how and why providers choose benzodiazepines or phenobarbital for management of inpatient alcohol withdrawal syndrome and the risk of adverse events associated with either treatment in different groups of hospitalized patients. This knowledge will be used to design and pilot an embedded pragmatic clinical trial to identify the safest first-line therapy for inpatient alcohol withdrawal syndrome: benzodiazepines or phenobarbital.

title: Schools as health-related assets: The role of school physical, educational, and social environments in shaping children's resilience to temperature and fine particulate matter exposure in New York
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Children spend much of their lives in schools, which represent both a physical and social environment. Previous work has demonstrated how the neighborhood built and social environments modify pediatric exposure and susceptibility to ambient heat and air pollution—however, schools have not been evaluated in this way. temperature and air pollutants, this work can be used to inform school-level interventions to protect pediatric health.

application_id: 11245119
activity_code: F31
title: Biochemical Silencing of Maladaptive Inflammation in Heart Failure
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF LOUISVILLE
phr: Project Narrative
Heart failure is a complex disease with many different causes, symptom, and prognoses, yet common
features to many presentations are inflammation and oxidative stress. This proposal seeks to determine
the ability of the endogenous histidyl dipeptide carnosine to quench pro-inflammatory products of
oxidative stress. Cardiac-specific overexpression and deletion of carnosine synthase will be used to
determine carnosine-mediated changes to these products, as well as cardiac function and remodeling in
failing hearts.

title: The Pathogenic Role of IL-33 in the SARS-CoV-2-Infected Lung
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), the causative agent of coronavirus disease
2019 (COVID-19), remains a significant threat to global public health. The alarmin cytokine IL-33 drives patho-
genesis in a mouse model of COVID-19 and has been associated with disease severity in human patients. The
proposed research will seek to elucidate this therapeutically relevant target by determining the mechanism
through which IL-33 impacts pulmonary pathology of SARS-CoV-2 infection.

title: CSHL Statistical Methods for Functional Genomics Course
org_name: COLD SPRING HARBOR LABORATORY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Biologists study the behavior and activities of cells at many different scales. For example, a biologist may
study how the activities and behaviors are inherited when a cell divides, or when they have been disrupted
through mutation or environmental insults in cancer tissues. Technological advances in the past two
decades have produced a variety of high-throughput methods for generating vast amounts of data about
cells, data that must be analyzed statistically due to the sheer volume and complexity of the measurements
involved. The primary objective of the course “Statistical Methods for Functional Genomics” is to train
both experimental and computational scientists in statistical methodology for analyzing high‐throughput
data sets, so that meaningful biological inference...

application_id: 11056679
activity_code: F31
title: The role of cell surface glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) in PTHrP bias signaling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
The parathyroid hormone (PTH)-related protein (PTHrP) is a key paracrine and autocrine regulator of bone
formation and remodeling, tooth eruption, mammary gland development, placental calcium ion transport, and
the main cause of hypercalcemia and osteolysis in patients with malignancy. The proposed research will
investigate a novel component, heparin/heparan sulfate, in PTHrP signaling via its PTH type 1 receptor (PTHR).
The findings will advance our fundamental understanding of the key cellular and molecular mechanisms
underlying PTHrP signaling that are essential for the development of new therapies targeting PTHrP associated
diseases.

application_id: 11117687
activity_code: K99
title: Determining how cancer cachexia influences gluconeogenesis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a high aggressive cancer associated with a peripheral wasting
condition called cancer cachexia that impacts quality of life. There are long-standing questions in the field of
cachexia such as where and how amino acids are metabolized during cachexia, and how does this change in
metastasis. This proposed research will test whether amino acids contribute to liver gluconeogenesis in both
primary and metastatic PDAC, which will provide scientific evidence that can facilitate therapeutic development
and improved standard of care for patients.

application_id: 11045691
activity_code: R25
title: A Clinical Immersion Program to Train Biomedical Engineers to Identify Indiana's Urban Health Needs
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The development of innovative medical devices and technologies depends on a biomedical engineering
workforce capable of identifying unmet clinical needs and working in teams to develop design solutions. Clinics
in urban environments present unique health care issues, and this clinical immersion and team-based design
program will challenge students to engage in engineering design in this context, with socioeconomic
considerations in mind. The project will implement the novel INdiana Summer Clinical Residency in Innovation
for Biomedical Engineers [(IN)SCRIBE] Program at Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis, in
collaboration with the IU School of Medicine, to prepare biomedical engineering students for clinically-relevant,
team-based design in an urban setting.

application_id: 11238411
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanism and Regulation of Replication-Coupled Asymmetric Histone Incorporation in Drosophila Male Germline Stem Cells
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proper maintenance of “immortal” cell populations like adult stem cells is important to prevent cell overproliferation
(cancer) or cell degeneration and loss (premature aging). Adult stem cells in both humans and flies undergo asymmetric cell
division to maintain stem cells while creating differentiating cells, and epigenetics underlie these different cell fate decisions.
Here I propose studying the role of chromatin and DNA replication timing on asymmetric histone incorporation to better
understand how epigenetic asymmetry can be established and regulated to produce substantially different cell fates and
ensure proper cell maintenance and homeostasis.

application_id: 11140988
activity_code: F31
title: A novel approach to exercise as treatment for injury-induced impairments in cerebrovascular function among adolescents with concussion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Prescribing aerobic exercise as medicine after concussion is best practice for reducing
patient symptom severity, but existing methods to determine the appropriate exercise prescription for individual
patients are impractical and may not adequately treat the physiological systems also impacted by concussion.
Our research laboratory has expertise in conducting exercise-related clinical trials and measuring physiological
function in patients with concussion. Thus, our study will explore clinically feasible post-concussion exercise
recommendations optimal for recovery of impaired physiological systems.

application_id: 10998126
activity_code: F31
title: Improving Global Measurement of Intimate Partner Violence
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Intimate partner violence (IPV), the most common form of violence worldwide, is responsible for a variety of physical and mental health problems for victims and exposed children that can persist throughout the life course. Despite increased awareness of the negative societal impacts of IPV and an international commitment to reducing IPV enshrined in the Sustainable Development Goals, numerous challenges exist in commonly used questionnaires to measure IPV. Improving the precision, validity, and cross-cultural consistency of IPV measurement is a prerequisite for accurately assessing the scope of the problem, evaluating efforts to prevent and reduce IPV, and effectively allocating resources to address this pervasive global health threat.

activity_code: F31
title: Leveraging Robot-Based Haptic Dyads to Improve Community-Based Stroke Rehabilitation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Limited healthcare resources in low resource settings in USA and developing countries necessitate practical solutions such as community-based rehabilitation and affordable robotics that enable caregivers and stroke survivors to interact and support rehabilitation in a community setting. title: iCAREFOR: Interhospital Care Fragmentation in Older Adults with Dementia
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Interhospital fragmentation of care is an understudied risk factor for poor patient outcomes,
including increased mortality, higher costs, and increased healthcare waste. This study focuses
on potential causes of and solutions to interhospital care fragmentation among older adults with
Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias. Findings from this study will provide additional

novel approaches for directly reducing care fragmentation as well as mitigating the negative

title: From Crest to Bone: Understanding neural crest skeletogenic capacity along the neural axis
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARATIVE
Studying the molecular mechanisms that drive neural crest stem cells to differentiate into bones is of critical
importance due to its implications for developmental disorders like retrognathia and cleft palate, as well as neural

a unique model system that resembles the

crest-derived osteogenesis. Thus, the results of this proposal will provide important insights into molecular
mechanisms crucial for normal neural crest-derived bone development and maintenance, including head

application_id: 10825433
activity_code: F31
title: A multi-modal, brain-wide atlas of astrocyte diversity across developmental stages and model species
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Astrocytes are a heterogenous population of glial cells that play critical roles in neuronal circuit assembly and function in health and disease, but the molecular drivers of their heterogeneity remains poorly understood. This proposal seeks to characterize astrocyte molecular, structural, and functional diversity across development stages and brain regions in the rodent and primate brain using cutting-edge techniques. Detailed characterization of astrocyte subpopulations will enable their more precise manipulation for basic and translational research.

application_id: 11006332
activity_code: K23
title: Association of Perceived Stress and Cognition
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
As the elderly population in United States continues to grow rapidly, Alzheimer's disease poses
a major public health challenge, and the disease may disproportionately burden older African
Americans. Our proposal is designed to reduce the knowledge gaps regarding the role of
modifiable risk factors such as perceived stress and how it may differentially impact cognitive
function in high-risk groups. Findings from our proposal would lay the foundation of
understanding the association of stress and cognitive impairment, mechanistic pathways, and
targeted interventions to reduce Alzheimer's disease disparities.

application_id: 11041052
activity_code: K23
title: ADRD Prevention Messaging to Increase Smoking Cessation Attempts in Older Adult Smokers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Older adult smokers are at elevated risk for cognitive decline and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias
(ADRD) development and, unless they can successfully quit smoking, their prevalence will continue to rise.
Unfortunately, older smokers are half as likely to attempt to quit smoking compared to younger adults, which
may be a function of both dysfunctional beliefs/motivational deficits of older smokers and clinician inaction. The
proposed work will develop and test a readily translatable Stage 1 motivational intervention for smoking
cessation in older adults consisting of: (1) a novel patient-informed motivational message promoting smoking
cessation, and (2) clear access routes to evidence-based smoking treatments (EBSTs) within a healthcare
setting.

title: Identifying tissue-specific interactions to bridge mechanisms underlying muscle degeneration in Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation and Dystroglycanopathies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MAINE ORONO
phr: Both Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation and dystroglycanopathies are clinically heterogenous, multisystem
disorders that can lead to severe progressive neural and muscular degeneration, which deleteriously impacts
quality of life and is frequently lethal. There are currently no cures for these diseases. We aim to leverage a
zebrafish model to gain insight into tissue-specific impacts of dpm3 expression on neuromuscular development
and provide foundational knowledge on mechanisms of disease progression in vivo.

application_id: 10770366
activity_code: F31
title: Neurological effects of toxic metal exposure in model rodents collected in zones of industrial agriculture on the U.S.-Mexico border
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Yuma County, Arizona is an agricultural hub producing more than 90% of the leafy greens consumed in the United States during winter with application of more than 2.6 million kilograms of pesticides per year. My previous quantitative research demonstrated concentrations of neurotoxic metals (e.g., copper [Cu] and manganese [Mn]) consistent with symptom manifestation as reported in the peer-reviewed literature. This study will utilize the Peromyscus eremicus rodent model to elucidate mechanisms of neurotoxicity due to Cu and Mn exposure, individually and combined, using transcriptomic and lipidomic techniques as well as gather samples for a preliminary exposure risk assessment on Cocopah Tribal lands.

application_id: 11229768
activity_code: F31
title: The cellular mechanisms and molecular evolution of mammalian gliding membrane outgrowth
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Disruptions in fundamental cellular and genetic processes during embryogenesis can lead to developmental
defects, which in humans are a major public health burden and leading cause of infant mortality. Interestingly,
heritable disruptions to these processes can also lead to morphological diversity over evolutionary time. By
studying the developmental basis and molecular evolution of a key morphological innovation, the mammalian
gliding membrane, we can gain new insight into how mammals sculpt and shape mesenchyme and skin during
development.

application_id: 11070474
activity_code: F31
title: Balancing Act: Equalizing Sex Specific Differences in CENP-A levels is Critical for Mammalian Embryogenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chromosomal abnormalities (i.e., aneuploidy) in oocytes and embryos are a major cause of infertility and recurrent pregnancy loss, with about 30-80% of embryos derived in IVF clinics being aneuploid. While it has been known that chromosome segregation errors occur during female meiosis, recent findings reveal that an increasing number of aneuploidies originate during mitotic divisions in the embryo, affecting only subsets of cells, and contribute to embryo mosaicism. Thus, this project proposes and tests the role of maternally inherited CENP-A in regulating early mammalian embryo development and preventing mosaicism.

application_id: 11172555
activity_code: F31
title: High-throughput thermodynamic and kinetic measurements for variant effects prediction in a major protein superfamily
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Missense mutations directly cause or exacerbate human diseases by introducing changes to the amino acid
sequence of a protein that disrupt its native biophysical properties. Attempts to predict these functional effects
from sequence variation have leveraged recent advancements in sequence-based machine learning, but have
suffered from a lack of large, biochemical datasets connecting sequence variance to descriptive measurements
of in vitro effects. Here, I will develop a cutting-edge experimental strategy to make quantitative biophysical
measurements for thousands of mutations in a major class of human proteins and establish a framework for
leveraging this data to enhance sequence-based predictive models for variant effects prediction.

application_id: 11060176
activity_code: F31
title: Identifying sources of variable penetrance and expressivity in monogenic diseases at population scale
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to enhance our understanding of the factors that influence the variable penetrance and
expressivity of monogenic variants. Here, we define “penetrance” as the percentage of carriers that show
disease and “expressivity” as the severity of the phenotype within carriers. Identifying factors that affect
penetrance and expressivity has a direct impact on public health by identifying monogenic carriers who will
need more aggressive treatment to manage their symptoms.

application_id: 11196058
activity_code: F31
title: The cis-regulatory basis of lineage-specific phenotypes among human groups
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Genomic and phenotypic data from our closest human relatives, Neanderthals and Denisovans (archaic
humans), is becoming ever more accessible due to technological advancements, but the relationship of
specific genetic changes between human lineages and their downstream phenotypic effects is still unmapped. I
propose to use modern, high-throughput functional genomics techniques to dissect how lineage-specific
variants in cis-regulatory regions led to the emergence of unique phenotypes in both archaic and modern
humans. The proposed study will be the most thorough investigation to-date of how lineage-specific derived
variants have played a role in the evolution of recent human groups.

application_id: 11069044
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the interplay between cohesin-mediated loop extrusion and heterochromatin in single cells.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Fragile X syndrome (FXS) is the most common cause of heritable intellectual disability, thought to be driven by instability of a CGG short tandem repeat tract and local DNA methylation of the FMR1 gene. We have recently observed a large Megabase-sized domain of heterochromatin encompassing silenced long synaptic genes and co-localized with severe misfolding of the 3D genome. Using cutting-edge imaging techniques coupled with genomics methods and new computational analyses, I will investigate the interplay between cohesin-mediated loop extrusion and heterochromatin in single-cells in the context of FXS.

title: Dissecting the functional relevance of unique subpopulations of striatal dopamine receptors in opioid use disorder
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Opioid addiction is a highly prevalent and devastating disorder with no effective treatments, resulting in an enormous burden on family and society. application_id: 10795851
activity_code: K23
title: ADRD Prevention Messaging to Increase Smoking Cessation Attempts in Older Adult Smokers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Older adult smokers are at elevated risk for cognitive decline and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) development and, unless they can successfully quit smoking, their prevalence will continue to rise. Unfortunately, older smokers are half as likely to attempt to quit smoking compared to younger adults, which may be a function of both dysfunctional beliefs/motivational deficits of older smokers and clinician inaction. The proposed work will develop and test a readily translatable Stage 1 motivational intervention for smoking cessation in older adults consisting of: (1) a novel patient-informed motivational message promoting smoking cessation, and (2) clear access routes to evidence-based smoking treatments (EBSTs) within a healthcare setting.

application_id: 11188955
activity_code: F32
title: Long-range exchange of expectation, experience and error during auditory-motor learning
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Sensations we perceive arise from both the external environment as well as our own actions, and we learn to
expect that certain movements will elicit predictable acoustic consequences. However, the same motor action
can often lead to different sensory outcomes, and it is known that the suppression of self-generated sounds in
the auditory cortex is highly sensory specific, but the degree to which the motor system reflects both the
execution of an action, and its anticipated sensory outcome, is unclear. This proposal will provide valuable
insights into the brain’s mechanisms for predicting and updating its encoding of self-generated sounds in real
time and uncover fundamental principles underlying the dynamic information flow between sensory and motor
regions of the brain.

application_id: 10843933
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular mechanisms driving cessation of neural crest migration and aggregation into cranial ganglia
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative  The neural crest is an important stem-like cell population, known for its migratory behavior, and essential for the normal development of numerous derivatives, such as the craniofacial skeleton, epidermal pigment cells, and much of the peripheral nervous system. Abnormalities in the neural crest contributes to neurocristopathies like familial dysautonomia as well as neural crest-derived cancers (i.e., neuroblastoma, pheochromocytoma, melanoma). Further study of the neural crest and its development will lend insight into this unique population of cells and give further indication of how irregularities in its development can contribute to these health conditions, allowing for the development of targeted treatment.

application_id: 11084484
activity_code: K23
title: Understanding the structural, functional, and prognostic implications of cortical excitability in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease is the most common cause of dementia in the United States, but recent drug trials have
failed to show an improvement in cognitive outcomes. Cortical excitability (measured using noninvasive brain
stimulation) has the potential to serve as a novel prognostic marker or treatment target, and is relevant to
public health due to its potential future applications in Alzheimer’s disease clinical trials. This is directly relevant
to the NIH’s mission of advancing the diagnosis, prevention, and cure of human diseases.

application_id: 10846759
activity_code: K23
title: Behavioral Change Following Culturally-Informed Biomarker Disclosure in Alzheimer’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative The proposed project will assess long-term changes to health/lifestyle, advanced planning, and research engagement that Black and White patients with Amnestic Mild Cognitive Impairment (aMCI) make following disclosure of positron emission tomography-based amyloid and tau burden and associated risk of conversion to Dementia-Alzheimer’s Type. Healthcare access will be explored as potential barrier to or facilitator of behavior change. The project therefore addresses a critical ethical and practical challenge facing all AD researchers by determining whether, in the absence of validated treatments for DAT, biomarker disclosure itself may result in adaptive behavior change in patients from diverse racial backgrounds.

title: Geriatric depression in India: Inadequate access to water and sanitation as risk factors
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Millions of India’s older adults above the age of 60 live in homes without access to water and sanitation and

inadequate access to these core resources exacerbates the relationships between underlying pathologies and
depression is unknown. I will fill this gap by using data from the largest longitudinal study ever conducted in
India on women and men above the age of 45.

application_id: 11249516
activity_code: F31
title: A Multiscale Computational Model of the Heart for Patient-specific Diagnosis of HFpEF
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project seeks to non-invasively estimate patient-specific myocardial stiffness via computational means in
patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF). As a result, this work will help elucidate
deeper insights into how myocardial stiffness changes over the course of HFpEF. Such knowledge could help
physicians stratify patients into risk groups and devise management plans that are optimal for each patient as
well as predicting HFpEF in the long-term, ultimately improving clinical outcomes and enhancing their quality of
life.

application_id: 11252170
activity_code: F31
title: The Impact of STING Activation and Fever on Regulatory T cell Function and Stability
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The cGAS/STING pathway and regulatory T cells (Tregs) share essential but opposing roles in many
inflammatory immune responses – which often coincide with elevated temperatures (e.g. fever-range
temperature, FRT) – yet the mechanisms intertwining FRT and STING activation in Tregs remain unknown. This
proposal aims to establish the individual and combined effects of FRT and STING activation on Treg function
and metabolism. We hypothesize that cGAS/STING pathway activation glycolytically reprograms Tregs into
inflammatory T cells and that this is potentiated at FRT during inflammatory immune responses.

application_id: 11257147
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Role HIV Viral gRNA and Gag-Pol Incorporation in HIV-1 Maturation
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative:
HIV is a continual threat to public health which, despite advances to prevent infection and slow disease
progression, has no definitive cure. Many existing treatments target enzymes essential for delivery of infectious
HIV virions, including protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase; however, the mechanism governing
incorporation of their host polyprotein, Gag-Pol, and the function of viral gRNA in HIV maturation efficiency are
not defined. This proposal aims to understand the mechanism of Gag-Pol incorporation via fluorescent light
microscopy and elucidate the role of viral gRNA in viral maturation efficiency via Cryo-Electron Tomography,
opening avenues for novel drug targets and advanced treatment of HIV.

application_id: 11257241
activity_code: F31
title: The impact of Wnt signaling on hematopoietic stem cell aging and its influence on fracture repair
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Precise regulation of the Wnt signaling pathway is necessary to maintain hematopoietic stem cell (HSC)
maintenance and quiescence. After a fracture event, the bone marrow environment, where the HSC niche
resides, is then disordered and recruits various inflammatory cytokines as part of the healing process. Injury and
age can lead to a dysregulation of Wnt signaling which this proposal hypothesizes alters HSC localization and
differentiation, affecting the quality of fracture repair, and thus can be an unexplored target to treat age-related
fracture complications.

application_id: 10993978
activity_code: F31
title: Aging impairs temporal fidelity in the auditory brainstem and underlies spatial hearing deficits
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Individuals 65 and older can suffer from central or “hidden” hearing loss, which makes localizing sound and detecting speech in noise difficult, but does not affect hearing in quiet environments. This type of hearing loss is difficult to treat because of our lack of understanding about the mechanisms that lead to this hearing loss. In this proposal, we will look at age-related changes in the auditory brainstem and cochlea that may correlate with a decrease in spatial hearing performance to better understand the mechanisms of age-related hidden hearing loss.

application_id: 11141622
activity_code: K23
title: Behavioral Change Following Culturally-Informed Biomarker Disclosure in Alzheimer’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed project will assess long-term changes to health/lifestyle, advanced planning, and research
engagement that Black and White patients with Amnestic Mild Cognitive Impairment (aMCI) make following
disclosure of positron emission tomography-based amyloid and tau burden and associated risk of conversion
to Dementia-Alzheimer’s Type. Healthcare access will be explored as potential barrier to or facilitator of
behavior change. The project therefore addresses a critical ethical and practical challenge facing all AD
researchers by determining whether, in the absence of validated treatments for DAT, biomarker disclosure
itself may result in adaptive behavior change in patients from diverse racial backgrounds.

application_id: 11109378
activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering the Role of GPR39 in the kidney
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative One of the main functions of the kidney is to match water output (urine volume) to water intake (drinking volume). I discovered that a receptor, known as GPR39, is found in specialized cells in the kidney that control how dilute or concentrated the urine is. In this proposal I will uncover how GPR39 influences the function of these cells (Aim 1), and how GPR39 contributes to our ability to maintain water balance (Aim 2).

application_id: 10988220
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the interactions of auxillary subunits with the Nav1.5 channel
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The Nav1.5 subunits of FGF12A, FGF12B, and calmodulin have been shown to individually modulate the cardiac sodium current. This proposal looks to determine the combined effects on these subunits have on Nav1.5 and class 1b anti-arrhythmic drug interactions with Nav1.5. The results from the proposed studies will clarify potential differences in the treatments of pro-arrhythmic patients and will develop methods to characterize similar auxiliary subunits on voltage gated ion channels.

application_id: 10917055
activity_code: F31
title: The Hillock: A Newly Discovered Airway Epithelial Structure and its Relationship with Squamous Metaplasia
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: The Hillock is a newly discovered cellular structure within the mammalian airway. While it is injury resistant and is able to regenerate the airway post-injury, it also has some similarities to squamous metaplasia, which is thought to be a precancerous lesion. This work will define the relationship of the hillock with squamous metaplasia as well as provide critical information on the molecular pathways that regulate hillocks’ differentiation and expansion.

application_id: 11089289
activity_code: F30
title: Molecular mechanism of INAFM2 in metastasis
org_name: GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Metastasis is the major cause of mortality in cancer patients today, with over 90% of cancer deaths being due to
metastasis, and there are very few treatment options available for treating metastatic cancer. In my preliminary
work, I discovered a novel mediator of metastasis: the human INAFM2 protein, which may present a future
druggable target. The aims of this grant are to fully confirm INAFM2’s role in cancer metastasis by using mouse
models and to determine INAFM2’s biological function and molecular mechanism.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The studies proposed in this application will examine the role of myosteatosis as a determinant of
cardiometabolic syndrome (CMS) in obesity. Using a mouse model of obesity individually crossed with models
of Galectin 3 (GAL3), NADPH Oxidase 1 (NOX1), and stearoyl-CoA desaturase-1 (SCD1) deletion, we will test
the hypothesis that obesity-driven ectopic fat accumulation in SKM drives vascular dysfunction and impaired
angiogenesis in a GAL-3 and NOX1 dependent manner and is the underlying cause of CMS, which is ameliorated
by the reversal of myosteatosis, improving cardiovascular health by restoring insulin sensitivity. This proposal
seeks to explore a novel and poorly understood risk factor in metabolic syndrome and will, through the targeting
of the GAL3, NOX1, and SCD1 g...

application_id: 11118706
activity_code: F31
title: Self-Assembling Peptide Nanoparticles for in vivo Genome Editor Delivery to Hematopoietic Stem Cells
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Therapeutic genome editing has shown great promise for treating inherited hemoglobinopathies such as sickle cell disease, yet current approaches require ex vivo procedures that cannot generally be conducted in parts of the world where the prevalence and mortality of these diseases are greatest. The proposed project will engineer
peptide-based nanoparticles for the delivery of therapeutic genome editing agents to targeted cell populations in vivo, eliminating the need for resource-intensive ex vivo processing. This research could serve as the basis for the development of hemoglobinopathy genome editing therapies that can be used globally, and establish an in
vivo gene delivery platform for the treatment of other illnesses.

application_id: 10987006
activity_code: F31
title: Contributions of Astrocyte Kir4.1/5.1 Channels to Disordered Breathing in Rett Syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT STORRS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Rett syndrome (RTT) is a neurodevelopmental disorder with a high mortality rate caused in part by respiratory dysfunction. RTT is caused by mutations in the gene encoding methyl-CpG-binding protein 2 (MECP2) and evidence suggests MeCP2 regulates expression of astrocyte Kir4.1 channels; however, it is not known how loss of MeCP2 or Kir4.1 channels disrupts breathing. This project seeks to identify astrocytes and Kir4.1 channels as the cellular and molecular basis for breathing problems in a mouse model of RTT.

title: Mechanisms of Heterochromatic Transcriptional Gene Silencing
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative Heterochromatin is a conserved feature of eukaryotic chromosomes with roles in the regulation of gene expression and maintenance of genome stability, and contributes to normal development and disease progression in humans. A basic understanding of the mechanisms that mediate the assembly and epigenetic inheritance of heterochromatin will not only provide a framework for understanding how the epigenetic regulatory mechanisms can fail, but also provides the substrate and knowledge to design therapeutic strategies based on intervention.

title: Promoting Independence with Compensatory Cognitive Rehabilitation: A Pilot Clinical Trial for Early-Stage Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF DELAWARE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is one of the leading causes of disability in older adults. Because pharmacological
approaches do not seem to prevent or slow the disease, clinicians need non-pharmacological interventions that
might help people with AD remain independent for as long as possible. This study aims to evaluate the effects
of a new behavioral treatment for adults with mild cognitive impairment designed to promote independent living
skills by explicitly teaching the use of strategies and tools to compensate for cognitive weaknesses.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Smoking cessation treatment for hospitalized patients must continue when they leave the hospital in order to
achieve the best results. Most smokers, however, return to smoking soon after leaving the hospital. This research
will develop and test interventions to engage smokers in post-discharge smoking cessation treatment which will
positively impact the long-term health of more than 4 million smokers that are hospitalized every year in the U.S.

activity_code: R36
title: Outpatient Mental Health Treatment, Median Household Income, and Residential Segregation: Improving Measurement of Spatial Accessibility for Medicaid Recipients
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal examines two structural factors that may contribute to reduced spatial accessibility to Medicaid- accepting mental health providers: neighborhood-level median household income and residential segregation. The results of this dissertation will directly serve marginalized groups by identifying potentially modifiable factors that could increase provider access for low-income and racialized individuals. Moreover, the developed methods will provide a pragmatic and iterative technique for identifying provider shortage areas and reducing access disparities over time.

title: Dissection of Hematopoietic Stem and Progenitor Cell Differentiation during Embryogenesis
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hematopoietic Stem Cells (HSCs) sustain lifelong hematopoiesis and are clinically valuable in hematopoietic replacement therapies to treat cancers and a variety of hematological diseases. application_id: 10865100
activity_code: K23
title: Cognitive and Neural Mechanisms of Antisocial Behavior in Frontotemporal Dementia
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE Antisocial behavior is common in frontotemporal dementia and can have severe consequences when patients break the law. The proposed research will use moral decision-making tests and neuroimaging to better understand the cognitive and neural mechanisms leading to these behaviors. This knowledge will inform future studies aimed at identifying, preventing, and treating antisocial behavior in dementia.

title: Predoctoral Training Program in Quantitative Mechanobiology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA SANTA BARBARA
phr: Project Narrative This Program trains predoctoral researchers in mechanobiology: the relationship between molecular events and mechanical forces in living systems. Training in both the quantitative and biological sciences will foster creative thinking across disciplinary lines, leading to the development of innovative materials, methods and measurement tools to advance human health. Trainees from diverse disciplines, backgrounds, and groups will be prepared to lead rigorous biomedical research programs and promote scientific advances in academia, industry, national labs, and technology and policy careers.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The proposed project will identify the relationship of historical and contemporary residential segregation on cognitive aging and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementia in older adults. application_id: 11041190
activity_code: K23
title: A Meal Delivery and Exercise Intervention to Increase Resilience in Homebound Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Older adults often fear loss of independence and the inability to stay in their homes as they age. There is a need
to identify the mechanisms behind this loss of function and to evaluate interventions which could help older adults
maintain their autonomy. Thus, this proposed pilot clinical trial will: 1) intervene to minimize the impact of frailty
using a home-based exercise program, administered through a meal delivery service, in homebound older
adults, and 2) explore possible specific frailty biomarkers to target for early interventions.

application_id: 10896079
activity_code: K43
title: A mobile phone short message service intervention to increase retention in HIV care among HIV-positive men who have sex with men in Peru (WelTel Peru)
org_name: UNIVERSIDAD PERUANA CAYETANO HEREDIA
phr: Project Narrative HIV disproportionally affects men who have sex with men, who report low retention in HIV care, which is associated with decreased survival, viral resistance, and HIV transmission. The World Health Organization strongly recommends mobile technologies to improve adherence; however, evidence on the role of mobile health for retention in HIV care is scarce. I will adapt the WelTel system, which has been proven to increase adherence to HIV treatment, in order to deliver a text messaging-based intervention and assess the efficacy to increase retention in HIV care among HIV+ gay men in Peru.

org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Older adults with HIV (OAH) experience more co-morbidities than their HIV negative peers despite viral
suppression with antiretroviral medications, and inflammatory changes with aging in the setting of HIV remain

this proposal are to investigate cfmtDNA in plasma and urine as a biomarker of geriatric syndromes in a
longitudinal study of OAH, evaluate the immunostimulatory potential of cfmtDNA, and foster the career
development of a physician-scientist who has, ...

application_id: 11042144
activity_code: K22
title: Elucidating regulatory control of Acinetobacter baumannii persistence and spread
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: NARRATIVE
Nosocomial infections caused by the emerging multi-drug resistant pathogen Acinetobacter baumannii
frequently occur as a result of patient contact with bacteria persisting on hospital surfaces in a desiccated state.
This proposal addresses the regulation of the long-term desiccation tolerance of A. baumannii by a conserved
Lon protease, as well as the molecular link between desiccation tolerance and virulence in this organism. Results
obtained from this research will strengthen our understanding of how A. baumannii causes disease and create
a framework for studying how transmission of this and other nosocomial pathogens occurs.

application_id: 10896349
activity_code: K23
title: Deriving TMS Targets for Mood Valence and Mood Stabilization in Bipolar Disorder
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative  New treatments are needed for bipolar disorder (BD), a lifelong illness in ~3% of United States adults that is associated with disability, chronic medical problems, and premature death. Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) is a focal, non-invasive form of brain stimulation that shows promise for BD, but the optimal brain regions to target for mania, depression, or mood stabilization are unclear. The purpose of this project is to derive treatment targets for BD using brain lesions that cause mania or depression, and to validate these lesion- derived targets in patients with BD using functional neuroimaging and behavioral testing.

title: Improving Access to Care and Chronic Condition Management for Rural Older Adults in the US
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Older adults make up a growing portion of rural communities in the United States, but are experiencing a two-
pronged challenge of an increasingly severe burden of chronic conditions and limited access to health care—
both of which have the potential to exacerbate existing rural-urban health disparities. The goal of this proposal
is to measure trends in access to care, chronic condition management, and rural-urban disparities in these
outcomes among older adults in the United States, and measure the effects of a novel strategy designed to
bolster rural health care delivery, known as global budgets, on these outcomes using a mixed methods
approach.

title: Vanderbilt-Mozambique Biomedical Informatics (VM-BMI) Training and Research Program
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE
Mozambique (a country in Africa with the fourth highest HIV prevalence globally) has made good progress in
HIV pandemic control, but significant gaps remain. Leveraging proven leadership and expertise as
Mozambique’s lead HIV Strategic Information partner and a history of highly successful partnerships between
the University of Eduardo Mondlane (UEM) and Vanderbilt University Medical Center (VUMC), the Vanderbilt-
Mozambique Biomedical Informatics (VM-BMI) Training and Research program will increase biomedical
informatics (BMI) capacity for HIV research at UEM. Over the course of the five-year project, VM-BMI will train
6 UEM faculty fellows, 10 master’s-level UEM students, and 200 Mozambican workshop and bootcamp
participants with an eye towards long-term sustainability.
