application_id: 10874443
activity_code: F31
title: Pathophysiology and prevention of degeneration of heterograft biomaterials due to advanced glycation end products and serum protein infiltration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative:  Advanced glycation end products and serum protein infiltration is one of the leading mechanisms that cause structural valve degeneration of bioprosthetic heart valves. The proposed research aims to further elucidate the basis of this mechanism by examining the effects of pretreating bioprosthetic heart valve leaflet material with two model compounds: pyridoxamine, an AGE-inhibitor, and Poly-2-methyl-2-oxazoline, which we have shown to significantly reduce protein infiltration. This work will provide insight into novel strategies to potentially mitigate AGE and serum protein infiltration and enhance biocompatibility, thus circumventing a leading mechanism of SVD that has not been investigated before.

application_id: 11229607
activity_code: F31
title: Roles for integrins in control of lung barrier function
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is a serious lung condition with high morbidity and mortality rates,
making it a significant public health concern. The human lung maintains airspace fluid by balancing leak with
fluid removal, however, claudin organization due to chronic alcohol abuse disrupts this balance by increasing
leak and when paired with ARDS leads to catastrophic lung flooding. This project aims to target lung cell
junctions as an approach to ameliorate lung leakage.

activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of SHP2-PLCG1 interaction in PDAC calcium signaling and metabolism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Greater than 90% of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma (PDAC) cases harbor a mutation in KRAS that allows continuous, sustained activity. We’ve found that SHP2 is essential for PDAC development by playing a role in promoting KRAS signaling, but we’ve found it functions in other signaling pathways that can drive tumor progression, as well. Therefore, we propose that SHP2 acts in multiple ways to promote tumor growth, providing tumors resistance to treatments that block KRAS signaling alone.

Female genital mutilation/cutting (FGM/C) affects girls and women throughout the world, with 3 million girls cut

application_id: 10917063
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of Protein Kinase C Theta by Phosphorylation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal seeks to elucidate the regulation of protein kinase C (PKC) theta () by phosphorylation and how this impacts function and biology. I have selected several critical phosphorylation residues (T538, S662, S676, S685, and S695) for further analysis with the goal of determining how each of these regulate PKC function and signaling. These fundamental studies will offer novel insights into the regulation of PKC in T cells and platelets, with application in immune and blood disorders.

title: The role of the Htr1B serotonin receptor in the neonate autoresuscitation reflex and embryonic serotonergic neurogenesis as a potential mechanism in Sudden Infant Death Syndrome
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) is an idiopathic disease in which subtle brainstem defects in the
serotonergic system have been observed. The goal of this proposal is to understand the mechanistic role of
serotonin receptors in both 1) the neonate auto-resuscitation reflex and 2) how the loss of it can alter the
neuronal activity dynamics and 3) control serotonergic neurogenesis. Findings will inform key processes
thought to be disrupted in many SIDS cases and yield critical insights for the development of future
therapeutics and diagnostics.

application_id: 10994227
activity_code: F31
title: Input specific adaptations to glutamatergic strength and plasticity on PFC PV-INs following ethanol drinking
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Although both men and women are affected by alcohol use disorder (AUD), women often progress through AUD disease milestones faster and experience more severe health consequences than men; however, little is known about the synaptic and molecular mechanism driving these sex-differences. The sexually dimorphic prefrontal cortex (PFC) integrates excitatory glutamatergic inputs from subcortical structures to modulate cortical excitation, yet the mechanism by which ethanol alters these inputs remains unclear. This proposal seeks to investigate how ethanol alters glutamatergic strength and plasticity at discrete excitatory inputs to the PFC in female and male mice.

application_id: 11175480
activity_code: F31
title: Defining How Macrophage Allorecognition Impacts Tissue Repair After Transplantation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Graft injury is unavoidable during heart transplant procedures, where surgical procedures and graft ischemia
times will generate potent immune responses that may contribute to the development of immunosuppressant-
resistant fibrosis and vasculopathy leading to chronic rejection. This proposal will study how macrophage
recognition of allogeneic MHCI components within the graft confounds their reparative functions. I will also define
novel macrophage allorecognition pathways and target them to evaluate if this blocks chronic rejection.

title: Mechanisms of Large Animal RV Dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
This proposal aims to train Jenna Mendelson in molecular and cellular cardiac physiology in order to become
an independently funded scientist conducting cardiovascular basic science research to combat cardiovascular
disease. Her project investigates the role of mitochondrial fatty acid metabolism in a large animal model of right

target for right ventricular dysfunction.

application_id: 11031297
activity_code: F31
title: Examining the Function of a Novel Protein in the Cardiac Junctional Membrane Complex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cardiac muscle contraction is a tightly controlled process where electrical excitation is translated to mechanical contraction by junctional membrane complexes in cardiomyocytes. Cardiomyopathy and heart failure have been linked to dysregulation of proteins within the junctional membrane complex which ultimately impacts contraction of the heart. This application will explore the role of a newly identified protein in the junctional membrane complex, Junctophilin-1, in heart physiology and disease.

org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY

This research aims to create an entirely new hybrid viral vector delivery vehicle for CRISPR gene therapy which

activity_code: F31
title: Investigation of Sex Differences in Physical Activity and its Relationships with Stress After Myocardial Infarction
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The growing burden of cardiovascular diseases among women under 60 years old is alarming; however, the unique pathophysiological risk factors causing this burden are unknown. Stress likely plays an important role in this early manifestation, potentially through reducing motivation to be physically active. This project will use data from NHLBI-funded study: Mental Stress and Myocardial Ischemia After MI: Sex Differences, Mechanisms and Prognosis (MIMS3) to investigate the role of stress and physical activity in a group of post-MI women and men to understand the importance these factors in this under-investigated population.

application_id: 11094223
activity_code: T32
title: Training in Environmental Health Biostatistics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Narrative
Most environmental health research involves statistical analysis of the data collected, for which input from
biostatisticians is critically important. PhD programs in Statistics and Biostatistics offer rigorous training in
statistical theory and methods, but generally do not train their graduates how to interact with multidisciplinary
scientists as team players. In this training program, predoctoral and postdoctoral trainees become deeply
embedded in environmental health science research collaborations under co-mentorship, where they learn
how to become team players and thereby help to fill this gap.
terms: <Biometrics><Biometry><Biostatistics><Environmental Health><Environmental Health Science><Training>

application_id: 11159529
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of cardiac small-conductance calcium-activated potassium channels by PIP2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Atrial Fibrillation (AF) is the most common type of cardiac arrhythmia, affecting over 40 million people
globally. Small conductance calcium-activated potassium channels (SK channels) retain a unique role in the
integration of the intracellular Ca2+ and membrane potentials within the heart. Our approaches integrating
patch-clamp, TIRF microscopy, super-resolution immunofluorescence imaging, optogenetics and genetic
manipulations may elucidate the mechanism of SK2 channels regulation by PIP2, and present a potential,
novel therapeutic target for atrial arrhythmias.

application_id: 10832561
activity_code: K99
title: Time-varying spatiotemporal causal interactions in the functional brain networks
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative This project aims to develop a novel computational paradigm for comprehensively characterizing and interpreting the causal system and its variability in functional brain networks. The findings will be translated in clinical studies of patients with different severity of neurological disorders to guide therapeutic practices, and particularly, post- concussive vestibular impairment syndrome will be used as one illustrative example in this study. Upon conclusion, we will understand the information transfer in resting state brain networks as well as its role in differentiating the subtypes of vestibular impairment.

title: Metabolic Programming of Hematopoietic Stem Cell Function by Prenatal Folate
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Folate-mediated one-carbon metabolism regulates biological processes critical to hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) establishment and function, however how folate supplementation affects the developing hematopoietic system is unknown. title: Loss of myeloid Paired Immunoglobulin-like Receptor B promotes atherosclerosis through inhibition of autophagy and metabolic rewiring
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronically activated, pro-inflammatory macrophages are believed to be critical determinants of disease progression in atherosclerosis. application_id: 11239955
activity_code: F32
title: Utilizing CRISPRa engineered stem cells for regeneration of the temporomandibular joint
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: The temporomandibular joint (TMJ) is prone to osteoarthritis (TMJ-OA) and it has been shown that one third of patients undergoing total replacement of the TMJ had TMJ-OA without comorbid conditions. Currently, no regenerative therapies to help patients with mandibular condylar cartilage pathology exist and the only surgical treatment option is end-stage arthroplasty with a total joint prosthetic. In this proposal, we develop a novel tissue engineering based therapeutic strategy for regeneration of the TMJ.

title: Metabolic Programming of Hematopoietic Stem Cell Function by Prenatal Folate
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Folate-mediated one-carbon metabolism regulates biological processes critical to hematopoietic stem cell (HSC)
establishment and function, however how folate supplementation affects the developing hematopoietic system
is unknown. We hypothesize that varying prenatal folate status will affects alter HSC function across adulthood
by perturbing HSC metabolism during development. The objective of this grant is to identify the effect of varying

title: Loss of myeloid Paired Immunoglobulin-like Receptor B promotes atherosclerosis through inhibition of autophagy and metabolic rewiring
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chronically activated, pro-inflammatory macrophages are believed to be critical determinants of disease
progression in atherosclerosis. I have identified the immunomodulatory receptor Paired immunoglobulin-like
receptor B (PirB) as a novel regulator of macrophage pro-inflammatory phenotype through its ability to regulate

application_id: 11235821
activity_code: F31
title: Using a novel model system to explore the causes and consequences of altered sleep need during pregnancy
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Pregnancy is a particularly vulnerable state in which inadequate sleep is linked to negative health
outcomes, and this might explain why women are reported to sleep more during their first trimester of
pregnancy. I developed a novel paradigm in Drosophila melanogaster to model increased sleep drive
during gravidity with the goal of understanding its causes and consequences. Further, studying the
mechanisms that drive this form of increased sleep need will provide a potential avenue for studying
how sleep need is measured, regulated, and fulfilled in general, as is my long term goal as a career
scientist.

application_id: 10817317
activity_code: F31
title: Effects of early Retinoic Acid signaling on valve development
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative Among structural congenital heart defects (CHDs), abnormal valve development makes up the most common malformations, including defects in valve leaflet anatomy and function. While perturbations of retinoic acid (RA) signaling are associated with CHDs, little is known about the role of RA in vertebrate valve development. The results of this proposal will illuminate mechanisms underlying the requirements of RA in vertebrate valve development, which may provide insight into the causes of valve defects and may aid in the generation of novel strategies for the prevention, repair, and maintenance of valve diseases.

application_id: 11112194
activity_code: K99
title: Elucidating Drug Interactomes for Cardiac Protection by SGLT2 Inhibitors
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Heart failure is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, and the off-target mechanisms by which
sodium-glucose transporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors provide cardiac protection in this context remain largely elusive.
Defining the SGLT2 inhibitor “interactome” – how it affects gene expression, metabolite abundance, and forms
direct protein interactions – presents an opportunity to resolve protective pathways. This proposal uses human
induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)- derived cardiovascular cells and three-dimensional tissue engineering to
identify molecular interplay conferred by SGLT2 inhibition in health and disease, with proteomic interrogation
and animal validation to identify cardioprotective targets.

application_id: 11051768
activity_code: F31
title: Host-Pathogen Interactions in a Cystic Fibrosis Polymicrobial Infection Model
org_name: DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cystic fibrosis (CF) affects more than 100,000 people worldwide and causes the formation of thick mucus in the lungs, leading to the chronic infection of various pathogens that are prone to developing antibiotic tolerance. This application proposes that preexposure to bacterial extracellular vesicles leads to epigenetic changes in host bronchial epithelial cells that alter the host immune response, contributing to the establishment of chronic lung infections. The project will serve as a foundation for the candidate to develop skills as an academic researcher and could lead to the development of novel therapeutic treatments for CF.

application_id: 10996765
activity_code: F31
title: The regulatory impact of transposable elements (TEs) on differential gene expression in closely related species during cellular differentiation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS MED BR GALVESTON
phr: Project Narrative Understanding how phenotypes vary between and within species despite high degrees of DNA similarity continues to limit our ability to predict disease susceptibility and severity. Transposable elements can regulate gene expression between different tissues within a species and the same tissue between species. By understanding the role TEs play in generating different phenotypes in human and chimpanzee heart-relevant cell types, we can bridge the gap between knowing the DNA sequence and predicting an individual’s disease risk and presentation.

activity_code: K08
title: Endothelial Metabolic Reprogramming by Interferon-gamma in Coronary Artery Disease
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal will define how interferon-gamma, a major T-cell cytokine abundant in atherosclerotic plaques, alters fuel utilization by the vascular cells that line the innermost layer of the coronary arteries, thereby making these vessels susceptible to atherosclerosis progression. This study, if successful, will improve our understanding of the adverse changes involving vascular metabolism in atherosclerosis and may lead to new therapeutic strategies to prevent heart attacks, strokes, and limb loss.

activity_code: T35
title: Medical Student Summer Research Program in Infection and Immunity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
There is currently a shortage of medical student trainees who are entering into careers in academic medicine
as physician-scientists; furthermore, diversity in the pool of physician-scientist pathway trainees fails to reflect
the diversity of our communities. This training program entitled “Medical Student Summer Research Program
in Infection and Immunity” aims to augment medical research-training opportunities and mentorship for a
diverse pool of medical students at the University of Minnesota Medical School by taking advantage of the
strength of our immunological sciences. With success in this effort, an increased and more diverse proportion
of our medical student population will enter into physician-scientist training pathway residency programs.

title: Genetic circuitry governing skin colonization of Candida auris
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Candida auris is an emerging fungal pathogen with high transmission rates within healthcare facilities, and high mortality rates associated with bloodstream infections. This project will investigate the genetic mechanisms facilitating the ability of C. auris to colonize skin, which serves as a source for transmission between patients as well as the reservoir for deadly bloodstream infections. application_id: 11086573
activity_code: F31
title: Quantitative OCT angiography toward 4D blood flow analysis in embryonic cardiovascular system
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Congenital heart defects are present in 1.8% of newborns and account for one third of congenital defects, yet
80% of cases have no identifiable causes. This project proposes a high-resolution in vivo imaging approach for
quantitative blood flow analysis in the early mouse embryonic cardiovascular system to build the first dynamic
volumetric cardiac flow pattern in early embryos, set a platform for functional cardiovascular phenotyping, and
study the biomechanical forces that regulate cardiac development. This will provide valuable insight to the role
of blood flow in regulating cardiovascular development and allow investigation into the interplay between genetic
and biomechanical factors in cardiac development and disease.

title: Adapting cell-based therapies for the treatment of pulmonary fibrosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative This project will create and test the efficacy in vivo of a novel T cell therapy to treat pulmonary fibrosis via TGF- β inhibitor payload delivery and profibrotic fibroblast depletion. T cells will co-express a Synthetic Intramembrane Proteolysis Receptor (SNIPR) and a Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR), both of which target profibrotic fibroblasts. title: Serotonin-2B receptor in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is the most common inherited cardiac disorder. Cardiac fibrosis is a key determinant of adverse outcomes in HCM, yet there are no therapeutics that prevent fibrosis or attenuate established fibrosis. Serotonin-2B (5-HT2B) receptor expression is significantly increased in HCM patients and has demonstrated anti-fibrotic effects in several cardiopulmonary diseases; eliminating 5-HT2B activity is a novel therapeutic approach to reduce fibrosis in HCM.

title: Role of Vascular Chemerin as a Regulator of Blood Pressure and Contributor to Cardiovascular Disease
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Development of effective therapies for obesity-associated hypertension have been hindered by our limited
understanding of the mechanisms by which excess adiposity drives cardiovascular disease (CVD). This
fellowship proposal tests how the adipokine chemerin that is produced in the vasculature supports blood
pressure and drives adiposity-associated hypertension and vascular stiffening. Through completion of this
project, the applicant will develop key technical skills to highlight chemerin as a therapeutic target in
obesity-associated hypertension and CVD as well as professional skills that will prepare her for a successful
career as a clinical industrial scientist.

application_id: 11175243
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanistic Analysis of Aortic Growth and Remodeling due to Hyperglycemia and Recovery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Diabetes is a major global health problem that results in systemic cardiovascular complications, such as
hypertension and increased arterial stiffness, and there is evidence that suggests treatment of the diabetes itself
can reverse the cardiovascular effects. The proposed research will investigate how diabetes affects large arteries
like the aorta, whether these changes can be reversed with treatment to recover glycemic control, and the
mechanisms behind these processes. This work will therefore describe how aorta biomechanics and
mechanobiology change due to diabetes and its treatment, providing critical information for the understanding of
how cardiovascular disease manifests and can be addressed in diabetic patients.

application_id: 11260127
activity_code: F30
title: Coordination of gene expression and molecular function in known pathways
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: NARRATIVE
The proposed research will use statistical modeling, curated pathway models, and single cell RNA seq atlases
to identify within pathways which genes’ expression are informative about regulation of pathway activity and
how this is specific to or shared by tissues. Combining our knowledgebases with our vast quantities of publicly
available RNA sequencing data will immediately provide a reference for basic, translational, and clinical
researchers to which to compare their samples. In the long-term, it will provide a foundation for computational
tools employing an interpretable dimensionality reduction of the 20,000 genes in the transcriptome to latent
variables describing the transcriptional regulation of each pathway.

application_id: 10755303
activity_code: K23
title: Leveraging implementation and behavioral science to reduce harmful overuse of diagnostic testing in critically ill children
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative Overuse of blood cultures to evaluate critically ill children for bacteremia can lead to a cascade of harm in the form of false positive results, unnecessary antibiotics, increased cost, and longer length of stay. The patient- level, clinician-level, and organizational-level determinants of overuse of diagnostic tests such as blood cultures are poorly understood, and no work has evaluated overuse reduction strategies. I will use implementation and behavioral science to define those determinants, and then develop and test strategies to safely reduce blood culture overuse in critically ill children.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project Narrative
Previous efforts at developing a computational framework to predict arrhythmia risk based on a drug’s chemical
structure have not been able to predict the risk associated with binding the hERG channel and prolonging the
cardiac action potential. Atomistic modeling of state-specific drug interactions with the hERG, hNaV1.5 and
hCaV1.2 channels is a promising solution. The proposed experiments will allow us to classify the arrhythmogenic
risk associated with common drugs, and aid in the screening of new compounds for proarrhythmic risk.

title: Investigating the functional role of a degron in ASXL1 in hematopoiesis and disease
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE ASXL1 is one of the most mutated genes in hematological disorders, but it is poorly understood how these mutations are mechanistically linked with these disorders as very little is known about the protein itself. In my preliminary studies, I discovered a degron that regulates the levels of ASXL1 protein, and this proposal will test if and how it regulates hematopoiesis. phr: Project Narrative This research project aims to better understand how lymphatic flow is affected in patients with lymphedema and how surgical interventions like lymphaticovenous anastomoses (LVA) can improve their condition. title: Cerebral oxygen extraction efficiency in patients with sickle cell anemia
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sickle cell anemia (SCA) is the most common and severe form of sickle cell disease and predisposes individuals for increased lifelong risk for stroke or brain injury. While protocols exist for children, there are currently no validated screening protocols for assessing stroke risk in adults with SCA. This project focuses on using novel proposed neuroimaging biomarkers to characterize brain health in adults with SCA and to assess how these biomarkers change after treatments that are known to reduce stroke risk; findings are intended to provide new information on the mechanisms contributing to brain injury in SCA with the long-term goal of using this information to triage patients for appropriate treatments.

title: Translation fidelity as a modulator of hematopoietic stem cell fitness and longevity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) regenerate all blood cells throughout life, but their function declines with age. This proposed research will investigate the role of translation fidelity in promoting protein homeostasis, HSC fitness, and longevity during aging. Investigating the role of translation fidelity in HSC function and longevity is critical for developing new therapeutic targets that mitigate age-associated functional declines of HSCs and to enhance the regenerative capacity of stem cells in older adults.

title: Exploring a Novel Myeloid Subset for Transporting Polyomavirus to the CNS
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative
JC Polyomavirus can cause debilitating and often fatal brain diseases. This fellowship will explore this CNS
infection modeled in vivo with Mouse Polyomavirus to address the current gap of how the virus traffics from the
common reservoir in the urinary tract to the brain. Specific myeloid cell subsets, such as CD13+ macrophages,
demonstrate potential for supporting active virus replication and facilitating entry at the brain barrier sites of the
choroid plexuses and ependyma.

title: Molecular Mechanisms of Intramuscular Adipose Tissue in Chronic Limb Threating Ischemia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative
The stagnant therapeutic development in peripheral arterial disease (PAD) and lack of success of angiogenic
therapies warrants further investigation of non-vascular cells contribution to limb functionality. Experiments
outlined in this predoctoral fellowship seek to understand how the accumulation of intramuscular adipose tissue
(IMAT), an understudied hallmark of severe PAD, contributes to ischemic limb function using genetic and
pharmacological approaches in preclinical models. This fellowship will provide thorough technical and intellectual
training to a promising young scientist in several areas including stem cell, muscle, and vascular biology.

application_id: 11192220
activity_code: F31
title: Structural Mechanism of Activation and Modulation of Tie2-mediated Angiogenesis
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The cardiovascular system is vital for the growth and healing of wounds, playing a crucial role in overall well-
being. This proposal utilizes a multidisciplinary approach to investigate the activation mechanism and
modulation of the Tie2 receptor, an essential membrane protein responsible for creating tight cell-cell junctions
at the endothelial monolayer that lines the blood vessels. The results of this study will provide a more complete
picture of the regulation of Tie2 on the structural level to improve our understanding of Tie2 biology and provide
a platform to investigate the dysregulation of Tie2 in human vasculature diseases.

application_id: 10896417
activity_code: K99
title: Cell-cell interactions driving gut inflammation and tolerance
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative Dysregulated mucosal immune responses to dietary or microbial antigens can cause severe diseases. The main goal of this research program is to study the nature of immune cells communication that dictates tolerance or inflammation to food and microbes. This work will provide both a new perspective for the understanding of intestinal disorders and a foundation for the development of new therapies targeting specific cells, genes, and pathways.

activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the Role of Notch/HES Signaling in Alveolar Epithelial Type II to Type I Cell Differentiation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF) is a progressive and fatal fibrotic lung disease with no known cure, resulting in respiratory failure and a median survival of only 3-5 years. Our preliminary data suggest a potential role of the Notch signaling pathway in the differentiation of lung alveolar epithelial cells (AECs), a regenerative process that becomes disrupted in the development of IPF. This project aims to investigate whether AECs utilize the Notch pathway to restore the alveolar epithelium after injury and, in doing so, will provide novel insight into potential therapeutic targets in the Notch pathway that may be leveraged to inform the development of curative IPF therapies.

These experiments will elucidate how anatomically distinct cortico-basal ganglia circuits contribute to motor

application_id: 10994834
activity_code: F31
title: Identifying Inflammatory Signals Facilitating Ectopic Krt5+ Cell Migration and their Function in the Alveoli
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Respiratory viral infections and interstitial lung diseases are characterized by the large-scale expansion and migration of ectopic cells to alveoli, where they participate in a dysplastic form of remodeling, leading to long- term reduced lung function as well as increased mortality in cases of Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis. The signals facilitating this dramatic proliferative/migratory event and whether these cells contribute to pulmonary fibrosis is unknown. This proposal will therefore investigate the role of NF-κB signaling in dysplastic remodeling and the pro-fibrotic function of these ectopic cells, thereby providing insight into the underlying mechanisms governing their migration and fibrotic pathology.

application_id: 10995201
activity_code: F31
title: Kidney-resident memory CD8+ T cells promote hypertension and memorize salt sensitivity.
org_name: UNIV OF ARKANSAS FOR MED SCIS
phr: High blood pressure, a leading cause of heart disease and the primary cause of death in the world lacks a complete understanding of its root causes and mechanisms of often-seen recurrence. Recent studies have indicated that the immune system may contribute to this rise in blood pressure, with our lab revealing immune cells interacting with kidney cells, leading to increased salt and water retention. This study aims to identify factors triggering immune cell residence in the kidney and understand their role in long-term blood pressure elevation and recurrence, offering insights for improving medications and ultimately aspiring toward a cure for hypertension.

application_id: 11194958
activity_code: F31
title: The innate antiviral response in airway epithelium: a cell type-resolved, functional genomics approach
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Early innate immune responses in airway epithelia, including the type I interferon response, are a critical, first-
line defense against the diverse milieu of respiratory viruses humans encounter in their environment.
Mathematical models of this response hint at specialized responses to interferon in individual airway cell types.
Through the development and application of a novel technology platform, this project will experimentally define
mechanisms of cellular defenses against influenza virus in the airway in individual cell types, highlighting novel
targets for influenza therapy and intervention.

application_id: 11315925
activity_code: F31
title: Applying spatial transcriptomics and mouse molecular genetics to understand the mechanisms of fibrotic scarring for promoting neural repair after spinal cord injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Following injury to the central nervous system, fibroblasts undergo drastic transcriptomic and morphological
changes to preserve tissue integrity and limit secondary damage in a process termed fibrotic scarring. However,
chronic fibrotic scarring has negative effects on CNS repair. This research proposal aims to utilize mouse
genetics, behavioral assays, and spatial transcriptomics to investigate fibrotic scarring in the spinal cord and
elucidate its impact on recovery and repair processes following spinal cord injury.

application_id: 11349971
activity_code: K23
title: Administrative Supplement to Physiology-driven seizure management post-cardiac arrest
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiac arrest impacts the lives of 500,000 Americans every year, and nearly half of patients
surviving the initial resuscitation will develop seizures or seizure-like brain activity as a complication of
hypoxic-ischemic brain injury. We aim to reduce secondary brain injury and neurological disability
from seizures after cardiac arrest by personalizing seizure treatment using EEG and brain MRI
biomarkers of neuro-recovery. We expect that the outcome of the proposed studies will provide
critical knowledge about epileptogenesis after hypoxic-ischemic brain injury andguide the
development of goal-directed seizure treatment and patient selection in future clinical trials.

application_id: 10996339
activity_code: F31
title: The innate antiviral response in airway epithelium: a cell type-resolved, functional genomics approach
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Early innate immune responses in airway epithelia, including the type I interferon response, are a critical, first- line defense against the diverse milieu of respiratory viruses humans encounter in their environment. Mathematical models of this response hint at specialized responses to interferon in individual airway cell types. Through the development and application of a novel technology platform, this project will experimentally define mechanisms of cellular defenses against influenza virus in the airway in individual cell types, highlighting novel targets for influenza therapy and intervention.

application_id: 10996021
activity_code: F31
title: Illuminating ER Lumen AMPK Dynamics for Maintaining Vascular Homeostasis via ACE2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative We are studying a key metabolic signaling molecule, AMPK, and its critical function in cardiovascular health and disease such as facilitating blood pressure maintenance through its interaction with the protein ACE2. While previous work identified that AMPK stabilizes ACE2 to offer cardiovascular benefits, our understanding of where and how this interaction occurs is limited. The work proposed here should lead to a better understanding of AMPK/ACE2 interactions to improve our understanding of blood pressure regulation and dysregulation, which affects millions of adults worldwide every year.

title: UMB RAMP: UMB Research And Mentoring Program to develop skills and promote interest in STEM fields through hands-on exposure to academic research among West Baltimore Youth
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Workforce diversification is essential to meet growing US demands in aging research, biomedical science and broader STEM fields. application_id: 11175269
activity_code: F31
title: Advancing a biopsychosocial model of bedtime procrastination
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although emerging research suggests that circadian disruption serves as a risk for cardiometabolic disease,
there is a lack of research investigating behavioral contributors to circadian disruption. Bedtime procrastination,
or delays in bedtime in the absence of external obligations, may disrupt circadian rhythms through alterations in
sleep timing. This project seeks to evaluate the impact of bedtime procrastination on circadian disruption and
cardiometabolic health, while also investigating the roles of anxiety, reward, and self-regulation in the
development of daily bedtime procrastination in order to inform novel treatments for bedtime procrastination and
circadian-sleep disturbances.

activity_code: F32
title: Genomic Mechanisms of BDNF Signaling in the Brain
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although neurotrophins, such as brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), play an essential role in neural circuit development and function, little is known about how neurotrophin-regulated transcription influences experience- dependent plasticity in the brain. This study seeks to characterize the genomic mechanisms by which BDNF regulates the maturation of inhibition within the mouse visual cortex. By identifying specific transcription factors and target genes that mediate the genomic response to BDNF in the brain, this proposal will provide novel insights into the etiology of neurological disorders that display a loss of BDNF production, including Alzheimer’s and Huntingtin’s disease.

application_id: 11198995
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanism of Intracellular ANGPTL3 and ANGPTL8 Trafficking
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
High levels of blood triglycerides are a risk factor for cardiovascular disease in humans, so new ways to
manage blood triglycerides are needed. Lipoprotein lipase (LPL) is an essential enzyme for the clearance of
triglycerides from the blood. This project aims to determine the trafficking pathways and subcellular
localization of two LPL inhibitors, angiopoietin-like (ANGPTL) protein 3 and ANGPTL8, which could lead to
ways to boost the activity of LPL by blocking its inhibition.

application_id: 10998553
activity_code: F31
title: New biophysical and immunoregulatory mechanisms in neutrophil extracellular trap mediated lung dysfunction in cystic fibrosis
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are being increasingly recognized as important contributors to the pathogenesis of muco-obstructive airway diseases including asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and most prominently in cystic fibrosis (CF). This project will 1) evaluate new potential mechanisms of NETs in altering the biophysical properties of mucus and 2) assess how airway mucus composition modulates the formation of NETs. Successful completion of this study will identify novel mucosal drug targets to catalyze the design of new therapeutics that reduce the impacts of NETs in CF and other chronic pulmonary diseases.

application_id: 11241072
activity_code: F31
title: Arterial Cell Reprogramming by Disturbed Flow and Hypercholesterolemia
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Atherosclerosis, the leading cause of death worldwide, develops in response to disturbed flow and
hypercholesterolemia. In this study, we propose to investigate the effects of disturbed flow alone,
hypercholesterolemia alone, and the plaque-inducing combination of disturbed flow and hypercholesterolemia
on arterial cell reprogramming, especially flow-induced reprogramming of endothelial cells (FIRE), and screen
for novel gene targets for atherosclerosis. Our study is designed for better understanding of the
pathophysiological mechanisms of atherogenesis and identification of novel therapeutic targets to combat this
widespread and lethal disease.

application_id: 10912580
activity_code: K23
title: Role of visceral adipose tissue in frailty among patients with Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) is an increasingly prevalent chronic lung disease with no available cure; frailty affects 50% of IPF patients, and is associated with increased severity of illness in response to acute insults, which are the most common cause of death in IPF. We identified that visceral adipose tissue is a risk factor for frailty and propose to investigate potential mechanisms linking visceral adipose tissue and frailty, as modifiable risk factors for morbidity and mortality in this growing patient population. Our findings may lead to novel therapies and clinical trials evaluating the role of existing medications in the prevention and treatment of frailty in IPF.

activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of Yap/Taz in neural crest-derived cardiac development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: Project Narrative The Hippo signaling pathway is fundamental for proper organ development, but little is known about its role in neural crest-derived heart development and how genetic mutations of the pathway contribute to congenital heart defects. The project aims to uncover how Yap/Taz, downstream effectors of Hippo signaling, regulate cardiac neural crest cell migration and differentiation using animal and cell models. This work will lead to an increased understanding of proper heart development and the identification of potential new therapeutic targets for treating and preventing congenital heart defects.

application_id: 11322511
activity_code: F31
title: The impact of Alzheimer's disease on novel genetic signatures of hippocampal memory traces
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a debilitating and irreversible neurodegenerative disease associated with major
disruptions to memory. While memories are thought to be stored in engrams, or ensembles of neurons
throughout the brain that are necessary and sufficient for memory expression, it is unknown what biophysical
changes occur in these individual engram cells and whether their disruption drives AD-associated memory loss.
Here, I will identify and manipulate molecular signatures unique to engram cells in the hippocampus (Aim 1) and
assess how these signatures are altered in a mouse model of AD (Aim 2) in order to identify novel molecular
targets for AD-associated memory loss.

application_id: 10896402
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Roles of ADARs and A-to-I RNA Editing in Germline RNA Regulation
org_name: TRUSTEES OF INDIANA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Regulation of gene expression at the RNA level in the germline is crucial for directing proper germ cell function and gametogenesis while maintaining the plasticity necessary for embryonic development. This study will explore the role of ADARs, a class of RNA binding protein, in germline RNA regulation. Insights gained here will increase our understanding of germline and reproductive biology and the complex regulation required for fine-tuning the molecular processes of gametogenesis and early embryonic development.

application_id: 11380498
activity_code: F31
title: Acetylation as a novel post-translational modification of MafA
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
β-cell dysfunction is one of the major characteristics of type II diabetes (T2D). MafA is an essential
transcriptional regulator of β-cell biology, and is necessary for glucose sensing and insulin secretion, but it is
often dysregulated in β-cells in animal models and people with T2D. In this proposal, I test whether MafA
acetylation affects the β-cell functional response to obesity.

application_id: 10999052
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding the Genetic Contributions to Variation in Ozone Exposure Response
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ozone is a common ambient air pollutant that causes pulmonary inflammation and decreased lung function upon acute exposure and is further associated with chronic respiratory diseases including asthma and COPD. There is ample evidence of interindividual variation in response to ozone in humans, but the genetic contribution to this variation is largely unknown. This study will use an in vitro model of the human lung featuring genetic diversity to identify risk variants for severe response to acute ozone exposure, then assess their overlap with asthma and COPD risk variants, which will guide identification of high-risk individuals.

application_id: 11292840
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Genetic and Epigenetic Control of Basal Cell Identity
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Basal cells are progenitor cells for multiple organs of the body that differentiate into specific cell types for each
organ. In this study, I aim to investigate how genetic and epigenetic mechanisms determine the specificity of
these differentiation programs in basal cells of the airways in the lung. Specifically, I plan to target genetic and
epigenetic modifiers in mouse airway basal cells to identify potential targets that prevent abnormal differentiation
and associated organ dysfunction and diseases.

application_id: 11318023
activity_code: F31
title: Unveil the functional role of RNA spatial localization in neuronal function and neurodegeneration by developing novel CRISPR-TO tools
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
RNA molecules regulate neuronal function, but dysregulated RNA localization in neurodegenerative diseases
lacks targeted intervention tools. We developed CRISPR-mediated transcriptome organization (CRISPR-TO) to
program RNA spatial dynamics, leveraging patient iPSC-derived neurons and AAV delivery to model disease
mechanisms. This work will define RNA localization’s role in neurodegeneration and advance therapies to restore
neuronal health through precision RNA localization targeting.

application_id: 10996829
activity_code: F31
title: Import of double-stranded RNA and cholesterol into cells in C. elegans
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Double-stranded RNA-based therapeutics, once delivered to a cell, can prevent the expression of disease- related genes of similar sequence through the RNA interference pathway. Despite this clinical application, some basic aspects of how double-stranded RNA moves through cellular membranes into cells remain unclear. This project will broadly contribute to better a better understanding of double-stranded RNA import by characterizing understudied members of a transporter family and specifically contribute insights that could guide the development of therapeutics for diseases by revealing mechanisms that connect double-stranded RNA import with cholesterol trafficking.

application_id: 11260203
activity_code: F31
title: Import of double-stranded RNA and cholesterol into cells in C. elegans
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Double-stranded RNA-based therapeutics, once delivered to a cell, can prevent the expression of disease-
related genes of similar sequence through the RNA interference pathway. Despite this clinical application,
some basic aspects of how double-stranded RNA moves through cellular membranes into cells remain unclear.
This project will broadly contribute to better a better understanding of double-stranded RNA import by
characterizing understudied members of a transporter family and specifically contribute insights that could
guide the development of therapeutics for diseases by revealing mechanisms that connect double-stranded
RNA import with cholesterol trafficking.

activity_code: F31
title: Investigating COUP-TFII in human vein development
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Knowledge of human blood vessel formation, including veins, is crucial for successfully vascularizing engineered
tissues in regenerative medicine and effectively treating organs affected by vascular diseases. However, the
processes guiding the development of human veins remain poorly understood due to the scarcity of samples
and the lack of relevant model systems. Here, I propose to study COUP-TFII, a transcription factor implicated in
venous differentiation in other species, and its mechanisms of action by leveraging a recently developed human
pluripotent stem cell differentiation protocol with the long-term goal of identifying new therapeutic pathways.

application_id: 11065998
activity_code: F31
title: Unraveling the Role of Modified HDL in Atherogenesis
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is the leading cause of death globally, with atherosclerosis being a major cause of CVD. Our research is designed to study how natural or environmental stresses render high density lipoprotein (HDL), “the good cholesterol”, dysfunctional so that it can no longer protect against CVD. The outcomes of these studies will provide a better understanding of the mechanisms by which dysfunctional HDL promotes atherogenic pathways which could help identify novel therapeutic strategies for preventing atherosclerosis.

Roughly half of all patients with myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS), a group of blood disorders diagnosed in

title: The Interplay Between mTOR Inhibitors and Endothelial Cell-to-Cell Junction Dynamics: Implications for Vascular Barrier Dysfunction
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite their widespread use in transplant medicine and coronary stent intervention to regulate cell growth and
proliferation, immunosuppresants, like everolimus cause vascular complications impacting patient care. Recent
advancements in these types of drugs, such as torin-2, offer potential solutions, though the mechanisms
remain unclear, stalling further innovation in this area. This research proposal will probe how these closely
related medications produce different effects on blood vessels by using physiological relevant cell models that
measure temporal dynamics to accurately characterized vascular barrier effects in live systems.

org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) is amongst the most common hematological malignancies of the elderly, with
limited treatment options and understanding of disease pathogenesis, and may often progress to acute myeloid
leukemia (AML). While germline mutations in the DEAD-box Helicase 41 gene (DDX41) have been identified in

common secondary mutation in CUT-like homeobox 1 (CUX1) cooperate to drive hematopoietic dysfunction.

application_id: 11092330
activity_code: F31
title: Addressing Algorithmic Unreliability and Dataset Shift in EHR-based Risk Prediction Models
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
This proposed research is relevant to public health because we aim to improve upon predictive analytic tools
that inform clinical decision-making by developing frameworks to address dataset shift, a cause of model
deterioration in dynamic settings, a critical step to accurately identify high risk patients who may benefit from
earlier interventions. This proposal will evaluate extent and mechanisms of dataset shift during the emergence
of the COVID-19 pandemic in an existing EHR-based risk prediction model, understand how rapid shift in model
performance impacts case-management, and assess competing methods of model revision and retraining that
address dataset shift and optimize predictive accuracy over time. Findings from this work are relevant to the
NIH’s mission by gen...

activity_code: F31
title: Childhood Socioeconomic Disadvantage and Antisocial Behavior: Investigating the Role of Reward Processing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Understanding how and why antisocial behavior (e.g., aggression, rule breaking) develops in the context of disadvantage is of critical importance to promoting health in adolescents and decreasing societal costs related to these disruptive behaviors. The proposed project will use longitudinal methods to examine whether socioeconomic disadvantage increases risk of engagement in antisocial behavior via reward-related brain structure and function. Results will inform reward-related interventions for adolescents at most risk for deleterious outcomes due to exposure to disadvantage.

title: Clinical Addiction Research and Education (CARE) Program
org_name: BOSTON MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The CARE Program will make important contributions to the development of the next generation of physician

application_id: 11120889
activity_code: K08
title: Kinase-independent mechanism of resistance to FLT3 inhibitors in AML
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative
Fms-like tyrosine kinase 3 (FLT3) mutations are some of the most common mutations in acute
myeloid leukemia. Phenylalanine 691 to leucine (F691L) mutations in AML occur following
targeted FLT3 inhibitor therapy, making these types of AML especially difficult to treat. The
proposed research will explore the mechanisms underlying this F691L mutation, aiming to target
this exquisitely resistant form of cancer genetically and pharmacologically, with a goal of
identifying novel targeted therapies against this high-risk group of leukemias.

application_id: 11120912
activity_code: F31
title: Cell-type and whole-brain dynamics underlying operant social stress resiliency
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative
Affective mood disorders, such as major depressive disorder (MDD), are challenging to treat and have a
disproportionate impact on society and health care costs. A key challenge in developing effective treatments
for MDD is that it is more commonly diagnosed in women that in men and coping strategies are typically
different. The aim of this proposal is to incorporate social decision making and motivation in a sex-dependent
manner to understand the spectrum of social stress coping mechanisms and identify the brain-wide neural
signature of social resiliency.

application_id: 11094900
activity_code: F31
title: Developing a Framework to Advance and Accommodate Behavioral Variability in Toddler Autism Screening: A Mixed-Methods Approach to Improve Autism Screening Accuracy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: The primary objective of this proposal is to develop and assess a framework for developing highly predictive parental-report screening measures to improve the accuracy of future ASD screeners for use with a range of individuals. The proposal will apply mixed methods data collection and analytic techniques in a novel manner to precisely characterize this dual heterogeneity and to develop prototype algorithms that predict ASD in young children.

title: Mechanism of CD8 regulation of natural killer cell biology
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Multidimensional immune correlative analysis revealed a striking negative association between CD8 expression on donor NK cells and treatment response in a clinical trial of relapsed/refractory AML patients treated with memory-like NK cells. The Emerging Infectious Disease and HIV Scholars at Brown University program is a collaboration between the Alpert Medical School of Brown University, the affiliated hospitals, and the Rhode Island Department of Health. application_id: 10900267
activity_code: F31
title: Examining subtypes of suicidal youth in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Suicide is the second leading cause of death for youth ages 10-14, and rates of suicidal thoughts and behaviors escalate dramatically across late childhood and into adolescence. Developmental risk for suicide, particularly in late childhood and pre-adolescence, remains very poorly understood, and research has yet to rigorously examine subtypes of youth who experience suicidal thoughts and behaviors during this developmental period using variables across multiple domains of functioning and units of analysis. The proposed research will leverage neurobiological, behavioral, and psychological data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study to provide one of the first multimodal investigations of suicide subtypes among youth and associations between subtypes ...

application_id: 11144913
activity_code: F31
title: Human Cortical Ripples as a Mechanism for Neural Binding
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Binding Problem asks how the diverse contents of awareness are unified, a cortical process that is
fundamental to understanding healthy and disrupted cognition in humans. This study will test if bursts of high-
frequency cortical oscillations (i.e., ‘ripples’) possess the properties crucial to a possible role in binding. If
successful, this work will lay the foundation to study neural binding in the human cortex, thereby facilitating the
investigation of how information processing is disrupted in many form of neuropsychiatric disease.

application_id: 10896957
activity_code: F31
title: Between- and Within-Person Heterogeneity in Adolescent Resting State Networks: Associations with Internalizing Psychopathology
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Functional connectivity brain networks are a promising tool for the identification of biomarkers of adolescent psychopathology, yet heterogeneity between and within individuals limits the validity of inferences made from group-averaged, time-invariant networks. This project will examine the level of data aggregation (group, subgroup, or individual) and time scale (static or dynamic) of resting state connectivity networks necessary for valid individual-level inferences and use these findings to predict adolescent anxiety and depression. Findings will be critical to determining methods of estimating functional connectivity networks that are representative of individuals such that inferences made using them have improved potential for clinical translation.

application_id: 10897801
activity_code: K23
title: Music-based Intervention for Insomnia in Persons Living with Dementia and their Caregivers
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Untreated insomnia symptoms in community-dwelling persons with Alzheimer’s Disease and related
Dementias contribute to increased institutionalization and accelerated disease progression. Because of the
adverse side effects associated with drug treatment, effective, low-cost, easily adaptable in-home interventions
are needed to improve insomnia symptoms in persons with Alzheimer’s Disease and related Dementias.
Examining the effects of a tailored music-based mobile application aimed at improving insomnia symptoms in
this population will inform future efficacy trials, potentially increasing the number of evidence-based options for
non-pharmacologic approaches.

application_id: 11178543
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding Changes in Hippocampal Representations by Measuring Memories with Natural Language Processing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The hippocampus plays a key role not only in encoding new episodic memories, but in separating neural
representations for highly similar—and potentially interfering—memories. The proposed research will
leverage Natural Language Processing algorithms and advanced computational and neuroimaging methods
to test whether separation of hippocampal representations predicts distortions in how events are actually
remembered (i.e., the content of episodic memories). The project will lead to fundamental new insight into
how the hippocampus resolves interference between similar memories and will establish exciting new
approaches for investigating these mechanisms.

application_id: 11228756
activity_code: F31
title: Role of early-life sleep and sensory environment in shaping the development of affiliative behavior and PV-interneurons in the somatosensory cortex of the highly social prairie vole.
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Early life sleep lays the foundation for the neural circuitry that facilitates affiliative behavior. This work will
investigate how early life sleep disruption impacts the developmental trajectory of inhibition in brain regions
involved in the display of affiliative behavior in the highly social prairie vole (Microtus ochrogaster). Finally, we
directly probe a mechanism by testing whether an enriched sensory environment rescues impairments in circuit

application_id: 10871671
activity_code: F31
title: Synthetic Hydrogels for Islet Vascularization and Engraftment
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Type 1 diabetes (T1D) is a chronic, debilitating disease that arises from the autoimmune destruction of insulin- producing β-cells located within pancreatic islets. Pancreatic islet transplantation has shown promise as a potential T1D treatment but is severely limited in clinical settings due to instant blood-mediated inflammatory reaction and delayed re-vascularization of transplanted islets within the intrahepatic site. The objective of this project is to engineer a synthetic biomaterial platform to induce successful vascularization and engraftment of islets in the subcutaneous space, an attractive alternative transplant site with high clinical potential for cell therapies to treat T1D.

application_id: 11097139
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the nigrostriatal circuit basis of false auditory perceptions.
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Deficits in sensory processing are common in brain disorders, but the neural substrates of these deficits are
poorly understood, and the treatments for them are limited. Evidence suggests that higher-order auditory
processing is subject to modulation by dopamine signaling in the striatum—a process that may go awry in
diseases like schizophrenia. The current study will combine genetic circuit manipulations with in vivo imaging
and behavior to link activity in specific dopamine projections and the modulation of specific striatal neuron
types to auditory perception deficits that may be relevant to psychiatric disease.

application_id: 11234232
activity_code: F31
title: Racial Microaggressions, Neural Vigilance Circuitry and PTSD Outcomes in Youth Victims of Violence
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN MILWAUKEE
phr: Project Narrative
This project will examine how experiences of microaggressions, a common and understudied form of racial
discrimination, affect Black youth’s neurobiological vigilance circuitry, and subsequent PTSD symptoms several
months after a traumatic assault. The results of the study will highlight both the immediate effect of
experiencing microaggressions as well as the impact of cumulative experiences during the first year of
recovery from a traumatic assault. Results may suggest a mechanism linking experiences of racial
microaggressions to posttraumatic stress symptoms, and thus, inform a target for intervention that considers
Black youth’s lived experiences with racial discrimination as they intersect with additional traumatic events.

application_id: 10901270
activity_code: F31
title: Computational Modeling of Interoceptive Perception across the Psychosis-Spectrum
org_name: TRUSTEES OF INDIANA UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Disrupted interoception (i.e., detection, integration, and interpretation of internal body states) may be a critical process in distressing psychosis symptoms (i.e., hallucinations and delusion). Manipulation of internal body states during predictive coding experiments is necessary to move beyond descriptive research, overcome limitations of existing tasks, and elucidate how interoceptive perception is related to key clinical features in the psychosis-spectrum. Using an individual differences design, computational modeling will dissect interoceptive predictive coding into subprocesses and disentangle their relationships with psychosis symptoms versus cognition to provide translational insights that may inform future treatment targets.

activity_code: DP5
title: The Behavioral Cost of Carbon
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The downstream behavioral impacts of carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions remain unknown in the US and globally, so they are not yet included in the US federal “social cost of carbon,” a metric used to quantify the human damages associated with a one-ton increase in greenhouse gas emissions. This proposed project evaluates the hypothesized behavioral costs of carbon for substance use, sleep outcomes, and physical activity. Characterizing how observed changes in climate, caused by each additional unit of greenhouse gas emissions today, impact future human health behaviors can have major implications for climate and health policies, which currently do not consider the behavioral costs of societies' emissions choices.

title: CD4+ Tissue resident memory T-cells in Crohnâs Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Memory T-cells are strongly implicated in the pathogenesis of Crohn’s disease (CD). We find that tissue resident memory (TRM) T-cells, a recently described population of memory T-cells, exhibiting a pathogenic profile are enriched in active CD. This proposal will define a role for CD4+ TRM in the pathogenesis of CD, which may lead to novel therapeutic targets in the treatment of CD.

application_id: 11101100
activity_code: F31
title: Computational Modeling of Interoceptive Perception across the Psychosis-Spectrum
org_name: TRUSTEES OF INDIANA UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 Disrupted interoception (i.e., detection, integration, and interpretation of internal body states) may be a
critical process in distressing psychosis symptoms (i.e., hallucinations and delusion). Manipulation of internal
body states during predictive coding experiments is necessary to move beyond descriptive research, overcome
limitations of existing tasks, and elucidate how interoceptive perception is related to key clinical features in the
psychosis-spectrum. Using an individual differences design, computational modeling will dissect interoceptive
predictive coding into subprocesses and disentangle their relationships with psychosis symptoms versus
cognition to provide translational insights that may inform future treatment targets.

application_id: 11071317
activity_code: F31
title: THE ROLE OF PARACINGULATE SULCAL MORPHOLOGY IN AFFECTIVE NETWORK CONNECTIVITY
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The cingulum bundle serves a critical role in mediating connectivity among mood-related regions and is implicated in several psychiatric disorders. The proposed research will investigate how variable local paracingulate sulcal pattern alters structural and functional effective cingulum bundle connectivity. This work will highlight the contribution of interindividual anatomical variability to brain connectivity in addition to informing patient-specific, neurosurgical approaches targeting the cingulum bundle for treatment-refractory psychiatric disorder intervention.

application_id: 11127685
activity_code: R25
title: FAIR DOs: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable Development of Open Simulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The gastrointestinal tract and kidneys provide respectively about 200 and 350 square meters of body surfaces
to the outside world - by far the largest surfaces in the body. The FAIR DOs program will educate the next
generation of researchers in using the latest computational tools to study transport processes across these
crucial surfaces. The use of FAIR methods will also ensure that students appreciate the importance of open
science, data sharing, standardization and large-scale collaborative modeling efforts.

application_id: 11180060
activity_code: F31
title: Improving Brief Assessment of Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors in Youth: An Engaged, Stakeholder-Informed Approach to Measure Development, Testing, and Implementation
org_name: TEXAS TECH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Invalid assessments of suicidal thoughts and behaviors (STBs) have profound implications for development of
suicide theories, research on suicide risk and protective factors, and clinical management, ultimately
hampering suicide prevention, but extant research has documented wide discrepancies in reports of STBs
across measures. This study will use qualitative and quantitative approaches to refine single-item STB
measures, test their psychometric properties, and identify other factors impacting response accuracy in youth
ages 15-24 with lived experience of STBs. Results of this study will provide actionable recommendations for
brief assessment of STBs, improving management of risk and data collection accuracy for STBs assessed in
research and clinical contexts.

application_id: 11060177
activity_code: F31
title: Structural, Functional, and Neurobehavioral Consequences of Adolescent COVID-19
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
In the United States, SARS-CoV-2 has infected more than 16 million children and adolescents, who are
undergoing critical periods of brain development and might be particularly susceptible to the neuroinflammation
associated with COVID-19. However, the extent to which these inflammatory effects during the acute illness
phase impact adolescent brain structure and function, and consequently cognitive abilities and risk for mental
illness, remains unknown. A better understanding of these neurological effects will provide knowledge to
identify adolescents most at risk for poor psychological health and cognitive deficits and the potential for early
intervention through cognitive training or mental health therapy.

application_id: 10897062
activity_code: K99
title: Early Life Stress and Cardiovascular Disease Risk: Identifying the Role of Microbial Metabolites
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE One in three children in the US experience early life stress such as abuse, neglect, and household dysfunction, which puts them at greater risk of cardiovascular disease later in life. The gut microbiota are known to produce metabolites that mediate cardiovascular function. This project seeks to determine how early life stress induced changes in gut microbial composition and metabolic function leads to increased risk of cardiovascular disease.

application_id: 11144915
activity_code: F31
title: Dynamic relations between interpersonal stressors, affective states, and binge eating in adolescent girls
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Binge eating (BE; i.e., overeating while experiencing a loss of control over eating) is the defining feature of
binge-spectrum eating disorders (e.g., bulimia nervosa and binge eating disorder), which typically onset and
show peak prevalence during adolescence. Given the importance of interpersonal influences and
socioemotional development during adolescence, a promising theory of BE in adolescence posits that
experiencing interpersonal stressors predicts BE eating and this relationship is mediated by increases in
negative affect. To inform targets for novel, real-time interventions, the proposed study will examine momentary
relations between interpersonal stressors, affective states, and BE among adolescent girls with binge-spectrum
eating disorders.

title: Norepinephrine and second messenger encoding of temporal threat proximity
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Survival requires the accurate prediction of threats at diverse proximity, and aberrant threat computation processes underlie psychiatric disorders such as post-traumatic stress disorders. application_id: 11180053
activity_code: F31
title: Advancing Implementation of Single Session Interventions in Schools
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative.
Youth commonly seek help for their mental health concerns in schools, but many schools don’t have enough
mental health providers to help everyone in need. Research studies show that digital, self-guided,
single-session interventions can help reduce students’ mental health concerns, but we don’t know how to get
schools to use these kinds of interventions in real life outside of research studies. In this study, we will work
together with students, parents/caregivers, teachers, administrators, and school mental health providers to find
out the best ways that digital, self-guided, single session interventions can be practically implemented in

application_id: 11001681
activity_code: F32
title: Determinants of elongation rate differences between B. subtilis and E. coli RNA polymerases
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Elongating RNA polymerase (RNAP) is a well-established nexus of gene regulation in bacteria, yet it is unknown why RNAP from the model gram-positive bacterium Bacillus subtilis travels much faster than RNAP from its gram-negative counterpart, Escherichia coli. The goal of my proposal is to determine why B. subtilis RNAP is faster than E. coli RNAP and how this impacts the function of B. subtilis RNAP-associated factors in co-transcriptional gene regulation. The results of these studies will establish mechanistic details of B. subtilis RNAP elongation, which will be relevant in understanding transcription in related pathogenic bacteria.

application_id: 10993269
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding gene regulation in schizophrenia-associated loci using high-throughput epigenetic CRISPR screens
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Despite its high heritability, the genetic foundations of schizophrenia (SCZ) remain largely unknown. While genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified loci linked to SCZ risk, our capacity to interpret and pinpoint causal variants and target genes lags behind our ability to identify disease- associated loci. Our lab employs CRISPR-Cas9 technologies to dissect candidate cis-regulatory elements in SCZ-associated loci and elucidate their target genes, promising insights into the genetic basis of SCZ.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA

application_id: 11192747
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding the mechanisms by which vCA1 orchestrates avoidance behavior
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The research project described in this proposal aims to enhance our understanding of the neural circuitry that
orchestrates avoidance behavior. Using the latest cutting-edge technologies, I will determine how the
hippocampal network encodes learned cues that predict aversive outcomes, and how the network uses this
information to promote adaptive avoidance behaviors. Given that maladaptive avoidance is relevant to the
human anxiety state, these findings could provide direction for future therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 11088019
activity_code: F31
title: Influences Of Social Determinants Of Health And Parental Participation In Hospital Care On Development Of Parenting Confidence Among Parents Of Children With A Congenital Heart Defect
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Parental participation in the care of hospitalized children has many positive outcomes, including helping parents to develop confidence in caring for their children, but there are many barriers to parents’ participation. This study will help us to define the factors unique to each family influence parental participation and parenting confidence among parents of children diagnosed with congenital heart defects. The study findings will provide important information that can be used to develop new ways to improve the health of children with congenital heart defects and their families.
